The lot is accessed via an existing access road and estimated resources are 50 000 m3 of pit run gravel and 10 000 m3 of topsoil. Quarrying activities are to occur annually between mid-August to freeze-up for approximately 20 years. Hauling of material may occur May to October.The existing quarries are located approximately 1km north of Watson Lake, just outside municipal boundaries. The Project is located in the Traditional Territories of the Liard First Nation and the Ross River Dena.

projectScope.summary: The Proponent is proposing to replace the Fox Creek Bridge, located at km 228 of the North Klondike Highway. The current Fox Creek Bridge was built in 1965; the expected life-span of the new bridge is 75 years. The new bridge will be in the same location as the current bridge. Additional components of the Project include the installation of streambank protection measures (rock riprap) to protect the bridge abutments from water flows, the installation and decommissioning of a temporary detour road and bridge, and road improvements along the Klondike Highway and Fox Creek Road. The Project is planned to start in April 2018 and will take approximately seven months to complete.The Fox Creek Bridge is located at approximately km 228 of the North Klondike Highway, south of Fox Lake. The Project falls within the Traditional Territories of the Kwanlin Dün First Nation and the Ta'an Kwäch'än Council.

projectScope.summary: The purpose of the Project is to continue stocking 19 lakes throughout southern and central Yukon with rainbow trout, kokanee, and arctic char. The Proponent proposes to purchase and transport fertilized eggs and fry from commercial facilities to Whitehorse Rapids Fish Hatchery where they will over winter until they reach an adequate size for release in spring. The Proponent will then transport the fish to 19 designated lakes for release.The Project is located within 10 First Nation Traditional Territories including Kwänlin Dün First Nation, Ta'an Kwäch'än Council, Carcross/Tagish First Nation, Na-Cho Nyak Dun First Nation, Champagne & Aishihik First Nations, Liard First Nation, Ross River Dena Council, Teslin Tlingit Council, Little Salmon/Carmacks First Nation, and Selkirk First Nation. Lakes include Cantlie Lake, Chadden Lake, Hidden Lake 1, Hidden Lake 2, and Scout Lake near Whitehorse; Judas Lake, Salmo Lake, and Marcella Lake in the Tagish and Teslin areas; Veronica Lake, Rantin Lake, Hour Lake, and Lucky Lake near Watson Lake; Fisheye Lake, Whiskers Lake, and Coffee Lake in the Faro and Ross River areas; Gloria Lake II near Carmacks; Wrong Lake in the Stewart and Pelly area; and Haldana Lake near Keno.

projectScope.activities: The Proponent proposes to construct a power line to Lot 1241-3, along the North Klondike Highway. The Project involves the installation of new power poles, line and a transformer on private property. The Proponent intends to undertake clearing on Crown land. There will be no fuelling of equipment on-site.Located along an unnamed road on the right side of the highway heading north, the project access is at km 12 of the North Klondike Highway, almost 24 km north of downtown Whitehorse, approximately 300 m south of the Burma Road turnoff. Within the Traditional Territory of the Kwanlin Dün First Nation and the Ta'an Kwäch'än Council, the Project is adjacent to Settlement Land parcel: KDFN C-159B.The anticipated project timeline is two days - one for clearing and the other for construction. The Proponent anticipates undertaking project activities in summer of 2015.Project activities will include:- Use of heavy equipment (e.g. digger truck and trailer, service truck, chipper, etc.) and associated fuel use- Clearing and grubbing to within 100 mm of the ground on Crown land (approximately 17 m long x 12.2 m wide)- Installation of two new power poles and one transformer on private property- Stringing 17 m of overhead wire
projectScope.summary: The Proponent, Yukon Electrical Company, Ltd., is proposing to construct a powerline to lot 1241-3 North Klondike Highway. For this, the Proponent will clear a 17 m by 12.2 m area within 100 mm of the ground for the powerline right of way, with no vegetative mat being removed. This will include the brushing, limbing, and potentially chipping of trees on site, and stringing 17 m of wire across a parcel of crown land.  Equipment used will include chainsaws, a wood chipper, a 14 tonne truck, and a three tonne service truck. Fueling of any equipment will be done off site. The proponent anticipates that it will take approximately one day to clear brush from the site, and one day to construct the powerline itself.The Project is located within the rural residential area of the North Klondike Highway. The site is within the Kwanlin Dün First Nation and Ta'an Kwäch'än Council's Traditional Territories, and is adjacent to Kwanlin Dün  Settlement Land Parcel KDFN C-159B.

projectScope.activities: The proposed Project involves the construction, installation and operation of a 217 m long powerline to service lot 1509 on Woodland Drive in the Ibex Valley, approximately 30 km northwest of Whitehorse. Project activities are anticipated to start in July 2014 and will take approximately 1 week to complete. Once constructed, the powerline will require regular maintenance and will have an indefinite lifespan.Project Activities Include:•	Clearing and brushing of a 187 m x 12.1 m corridor (total cleared footprint of 2,262.7 m2);•	Use of heavy equipment;•	Installation of 3 poles, 3 anchors, a transformer and 217 m of wire;•	Disposal of cleared debris and salvage of cut trees;•	Ongoing maintenance of the powerline.A seeking views and information (SVI) period was held from June 16 to June 30, 2014. The Designated Office issued its recommendations and evaluation report (ER) for the Project on July 14. A Decision Document will be issued by the Decision Body by August 14, 2014.

The project is within the Ibex Valley and is subject to the Ibex Valley Local Area Plan.The project location is approximately 30 km North of downtown Whitehorse on the Alaska Highway off of Woodland Drive.

projectScope.summary: The purpose of the Project is to rehabilitate the roadway stream crossing infrastructure of McIntyre Creek at Mountain View Drive. There are two corrugated steel pipe culverts at that crossing. The work includes repairing damage to the north culvert inlet and remediation of the area immediately upstream (approximately 20m) of both culvert inlets to avoid further erosion. The Project will also remove deposited sediments that have accumulated in the streambed. Project activities include: installation of a coffer dam to isolate the north culvert work area, direct pumping of water out of the work area, replacement of the damaged section of the north culvert, installing a structural tie-down system around the new culvert section, placement of riprap around the culvert inlet, placement up riprap along stream banks immediately upstream from the culvert, and excavation of sediment and debris from the streambed. The work is proposed to take place between June 2018 and July 2018. The Project site is within the Traditional Territories of the Kwänlin Dün First Nation and the Ta'an Kwäch'än Council.

projectScope.summary: The proposed project is a commercial adventure tourism operation within Kluane National Park and Reserve (KNPR). The Project proposes a combination of backcountry skiing, ski mountaineering, day hiking and multi-day backpacking trips in KNPR along existing trails and described routes. Mountaineering trips will have up to 10 participants, be between 7 to 20 days and will occur 1 to 4 times annually between April and June. Trips will be led by guides certified as Full Ski Guides and/or Full Mountain Guides by the Association of Canadian Mountain Guides and the International Federation of Mountain Guides Associations. Access to KNPR will be via rotary or fixed wing aircraft operated by aviation companies possessing a Business License to land within park boundaries.Hiking activities include day and multi-day trips along designated routes and trails within KNPR. Trips will occur between June and October with a maximum of 10 participants led by qualified guides possessing Wilderness First Responder training and knowledge of environmental and cultural resources.

projectScope.activities: Project Scope: The proposed project involves the realignment of the existing power transmission line between Crestview and the North Klondike Highway (Mayo Road) from the north side of the Alaska Highway to the south side of the highway in order to facilitate future highway upgrades. The proponent proposes to construct a new transmission line largely within the existing highway right-of-way and has applied for a Work within a Right of Way permit from YG Highways and Public Works. Two segments of the line will be on parcels designated as Commissioner's Land, 780 metres apart that are outside of the existing right of way and will require the issuance of a License of Land Occupation. The existing transmission line will be removed, holes filled and poles and wire salvaged. The proponent anticipates commencing work March 12, 2012 and to be completed by October 15, 2012. This project has an indefinite lifespan.

projectScope.activities: The proposed project involves installation of two test wells in the Selkirk Aquifer in the Riverdale south well field for potential use in the municipal water supply. The test wells will be located in the City of Whitehorse along the south boundary of the Riverdale Subdivision, adjacent to the Yukon Energy Corporation transmission line easement. Once completed, test wells will be used for monitoring aquifer temperature and water levels; and possible future production, and hence will not be decommissioned. Well drilling will commence in July 2012 and will take approximately 4 days to complete. Well testing will take approximately 5 days per well. This scope does not consider production wells, as the proponent would need to seek a separate assessment and permits should the test wells prove viable.

projectScope.summary: The proposed Project is the operation of a Class 4 placer mine on 40 claims on Upper Wade Creek, a tributary of the Donjek River. The claim block is within Kluane First Nation (KFN) and White River First Nation (WRFN) Traditional Territories, and partially overlaps with KFN Settlement Land block R-49B. Project activities are planned to occur between May 15th and October 31st annually for a period of ten years, beginning in 2016.The proposal was submitted on March 21st, 2016 and was reviewed by the Haines Junction Designated Office to determine whether there was adequate information to commence the assessment. The adequacy review identified information gaps, and an Information Request was issued to the Proponent on April 4th. The Proponent provided the requested information, as well as a number of revised proposal documents, between April 5th and April 14th. The proposal was deemed adequate on April 14th.The Designated Office provided an opportunity for comment on the Project during the initial Seeking Views and Information (SV&I) period from April 14th to April 28th, 2016. The SV&I period was extended until May 5th, 2016 following a request for extension from Government of Yukon (YG).  Comments were submitted by the Department of Fisheries and Oceans (DFO) Canada, five departments of YG, KFN, and the Canadian Wildlife Service (Environment and Climate Change Canada). Concerns raised in comment submissions were related to the potential for Project activities to adversely impact environmental quality (water, soil and air), biodiversity, wildlife and wildlife habitat (including migratory birds and species at risk), First Nations subsistence harvesting, economic opportunities related to outfitting, and heritage resources. The Designated Office identified four valued components in the assessment: Aquatic Resources, Environmental Quality, Wildlife and Wildlife Habitat, and Heritage Resources. It was determined that the Project will have significant adverse effects on all of the identified valued components. However, by implementing the recommended mitigation measures in the Evaluation Report, in addition to those that the Proponent has committed to and application of relevant legislative requirements, the Designated Office is satisfied that the adverse effects can be eliminated, reduced or controlled such that they are not significant. The rationale for this determination is presented in the Evaluation Report.The Evaluation Report and Recommendation was issued on June 14th, 2016. The Designated Office recommended to the Decision Body (Government of Yukon) that the Project be allowed to proceed, subject to specified terms and conditions. The Decision Document has not yet been issued for this Project.The Project is located approximately 30 km northwest of Burwash Landing, within the Kluane Wildlife Sanctuary.

projectScope.summary: The Proponent is proposing to film the spring migration of the Porcupine caribou herd as they head north to their calving grounds through the Vuntut and Ivvavik National Parks. Project activities are proposed to occur from March to June, 2015. The goal of the Project is to produce a 60 minute wildlife documentary on the world's longest mammal migration.       The Dawson Designated Office issued two Information Requests, one on October 30, 2014 and the second on November 25, 2014. The Proposal was determined to be adequate on December 10, 2014, and the Dawson Designated Office sought views and information on the proposed Project until January 16, 2015. Following Seeking Views and Information, the Dawson Designated Office determined that supplemental information was required from the Proponent regarding drone use for the Project. A third Information Request was issued on January 22, 2015. As per the Dawson Designated Office rules, the Proponent has until February 19, 2015 to either submit the requested information or advise us as to when the information will be submitted.Filming will be focused in northern Yukon in areas including the Vuntut and Ivvavik National Parks, within the traditional territory of the Vuntut Gwitchin First Nation and the Inuvialuit Settlement Region.

projectScope.activities: The Project involves the continued operation and management of the Canyon Creek Solid Waste Disposal Facility (SWDF). The SWDF is located at km 1609.4 of the Alaska Highway on Champagne and Aishihik First Nations settlement land block R-14B and serves approximately 25 users from the community of Canyon Creek. The SWDF is an unattended modified transfer station, which is open 24/7. The continued operation and management of the SWDF will occur for a period of ten years, under the guidance of its Solid Waste Management Plan for the 2013-2023 planning period.Project Activities:•  Operation of a modified transfer station, accepting municipal solid waste (MSW) and recyclables•  Closure of former landfilling cells•  Transfer of MSW to Son of War Eagle SWMF (in Whitehorse): approximately 830 kg/week•  Transfer of recyclables to a Whitehorse recycling processor as required•  Ongoing groundwater monitoring•  Weekly site inspections, which include general inspection and maintenance as required
projectScope.summary: Government of Yukon - Department of Community Services submitted a proposal for permit renewal for the continued operation and management of the Canyon Creek Solid Waste Disposal Facility (SWDF).  The proposal was reviewed to determine whether the information was adequate to commence an assessment.  The adequacy review identified information gaps in project information, and additional information was requested from the Proponent.  The Proponent supplied the requested information (YOR documents 2014-0153-015-1, 2014-0153-18-1), and the proposal was deemed Adequate on Oct 29, 2014.  The Project went into Seeking Views and Information with a comment submission deadline of Nov 12, 2014.  Comments were received, a Recommendation issued on Dec 2, 2014, and a Decision Document issued on Dec 29, 2014.The Project is located at km 1609.4 of the Alaska Highway.  The nearest community is Canyon Creek, situated 4.5 km to the east of the Project, and the nearest watercourse is the Dezadeash River.  The Project is located within the Southwest Assessment District, within the traditional territory of the Champagne and Aishihik First Nations.

projectScope.summary: The Proponent, ATCO Electric Yukon, is proposing to install a power line to Lots 62 and 63, Fossil Point, in the Deep Creek area, on the western shore of Lake Laberge.      Under s. 56(1)(a) of the Yukon Environmental and Socio-economic Assessment Act, the Whitehorse Designated Office recommends to the Decision Bodies for the Project that the Project be allowed to proceed, as it determined that the Project will not have significant adverse environmental or socio-economic effects in or outside Yukon.The Designated Office accepted comments on the Project from December 2, 2015 to December 16, 2014. The Government of Yukon was the only party to submit comments and it indicated it had no concerns with the Project.       The Designated Office identified two valued environmental and socio-economic components potentially affected by project activities: wildlife and wildlife habitat, and environmental quality. The following effects characterized these valued components including, disturbance to nesting and breeding birds, deposition of deleterious substances, and invasive species. In determining whether any adverse effects were significant, the Designated Office considered the requirements for the Proponent in relevant legislation, the Proponent's commitments, and various measures such as probability, magnitude, and context.Just east of the North Klondike Highway, these lots are approximately 51 km north of Whitehorse, near the Grizzly Valley subdivision and the Lake Laberge campground. The Project will overlap with the Traditional Territories of Kwanlin Dün First Nation and Ta'an Kwäch'än Council.

projectScope.activities: The purpose of the Project is the development of a 14-lot subdivision and expansion of a water distribution system on Lot 1036 in the community of Beaver Creek. Lot 1036 is located on interim protected land block WRFN C-16B. The subdivision is planned to include 14 lots in total: 12 for residential purposes (lots 1 to 4 are already developed) and 2 for a cultural center. The existing water distribution system in Beaver Creek will be expanded to provide water services to the new subdivision. The Project will also include the expansion of Lot 1036 by 40 m to the west and the development of new access into the subdivision. Construction activities are scheduled to begin in the summer of 2015 and will be ongoing for 5 years, while the Project will have an indefinite lifespan.Project Activities:Site Preparation•	Clearing and removal of vegetation and overburden and lot grading (use of 3 500 m3 of granular fill)•	Installation of drainage ditches and culvertsUtilities•	Expansion of overhead power and communications systemsAccess•	Extend and develop access road (approximately 450 m x 6 m)•	Construction of driveways Water System 1 Expansion•	Trenching and burying insulated water main system to new and existing lots•	Upgrade insulation, plumbing and ancillary mechanical upgrades in the water treatment plant•	Decommission two wells on Lots 1 to 4 of Lot 1036Building Construction Phase•	Construction of 8 single-family residential houses •	Construction of a cultural center•	Installation of septic systems, including septic fields for each house and the cultural center•	Installation of fuel oil or propane heating systems Accessory Activities•	Use of heavy equipment•	Water Use: less than 100 m3/day from existing Water System #1 for dust control•	Fuel Storage: 600 L diesel in a Tidy Tank•	Site reclamationWaste Management

The Proponent supplied the requested information (YOR document 2015-0005-015-1), and the proposal was deemed Adequate on Mar 10, 2015. The Project is located within the Southwest Assessment District, within the traditional territories of the White River First Nation and Kluane First Nation.

projectScope.summary: The Proponent is proposing to mine placer gold deposits on 13 claims adjacent to the McQuesten River using a bucket line dredge. The Project is located approximately 500 m east of the Klondike Highway approximately 75 km west of Mayo. Project activities are proposed to occur between August 1st and November 1st annually for the next ten years.Views and information were solicited between April 7 and May 12, 2015. The public comment period was extended twice at the request of participants in the Process. During this period comments were provided by Environment Canada, Department of Fisheries and Oceans Canada, Yukon Energy Corporation, Tr'ondëk Hwëch'in First Nation, Mayo District Renewable Resources Council, Yukon Government and the First Nation of Na-Cho Nyak Dun. From comments received and other relevant considerations aquatic resources, heritage resources, birds and environmental quality were identified as environmental and socio-economic values affected by the Project. Significant adverse effects are expected to all values identified. Additional mitigations are recommended to reduce, eliminate or control the significant adverse effects identified. The Project changed in numerous ways during the assessment and the Project assessed is substantially different than the one originally proposed. The assessment took considerable time to complete in order to provide time for consideration of the changes to the Project. In addition, the nature of the proposal, particularly the proposal to operate within the riparian zone of a watercourse with high habitat quality for salmon required careful evaluation.The Project is located approximately 500 m east of the Klondike Highway approximately 75 km west of Mayo.

During this period, the Designated Office received comments from the VHJ. The VHJ noted regulatory reminders such as applicable bylaws and permit requirements for the Project. The rationale for this determination is presented in the Evaluation Report.The Project is located at 119 Logan Street in Haines Junction (Block 37, Plan 57384).

Project activities are proposed to occur between February 15 and November 15 for 10 years.Project Activities:•	Use of heavy machinery•	Water use (withdrawal up to 15 724 m3/day)•	Clearing and stripping (mechanical- •	1 350 000 m3/year)•	Material processed (450 000 m3/year)•	Construction and use of settling facilities (out of stream)•	Construction of diversions (permanent, temporary and seasonal- 14 000 m length total)•	Crossing watercourses (existing and new- 10 total)•	Exploration activities (auger drilling- 200 m2/ year clearings,  use of explosives)•	Construction of new roads and trails (4000 m length, 5 m width)•	Use of camp facilities (two camps, up to 16 people, five trailers, 5 RVs) •	Fuel handling (76 000 L diesel, 5 695 L propane, •	3 268 L gasoline)•	Storage and disposal of wastes•	Reclamation and decommissioning activities
projectScope.summary: The project is a large placer mine on 76 claims centered on Haggart Creek. This project will involve mining the Haggart Creek valley from one side to the other down to bedrock where possible. Although this project is proposed for 10 years at this time, the Proponent has stated that an additional 10 years or more is likely for a total project life in excess of 20 years. Haggart Creek at the location of the project forms the northern legal boundary of Settlement Land block NND-R-20B owned by the First Nation of Na-Cho Nyak Dun. The Haggart Creek will be permanently diverted from its current location to accommodate mining activities. The Mayo Designated Office initially sought views and information on this proposed project from April 22, 2014 - May 6, 2014. During that period, Yukon Government requested a two-week extension to the public comment period to further consider the potential effects of the project to the significant arctic grayling run in Haggart Creek. Following this request we received a request from the First Nation of Na-Cho Nyak Dun (NND) for an additional extension. The total public comment period for this assessment was 35 days. The commnents received during the assessment highlighted a number of potential environmental and socio-economic effects of the project. Of particular focus were the potential effects to the arctic grayling run. Haggart Creek provides critical migration between the summer habitat for a large grayling run in Lynx Creek and the winter/spawning habitat in the South McQuesten River. In addition to the concerns regarding potential effects to grayling, NND noted the effects to their Category B settlement land block and the boundary for this block. NND's comments concluded that due to the potential cumulative effects of this project, they will not grant the Proponent access to their Category B settlement land to conduct the proposed project. Following the submission of NND's comments we received a request from the Proponent to extend the Preparing Reccomendation period to allow them additional time to meet with NND regarding their project. As we felt the results of these meetings and discussions was likely to produce valuable information for the assessment, we granted an extension to July 7, 2014. On July 7, 2014 we issued our Evaluation Report to the Decision Bodies recommending the project be allowed to proceed subject to terms and conditions.This project is located on Haggart Creek, approximately 34 km north of Mayo Yukon. Haggart Creek flows into the South McQuesten River.

projectScope.summary: The proposed project is the operation of a Class 4 placer mine on 12 claims on Larose and Rabbit Creeks, tributaries of Twelth of July Creek within the upper reaches of the Jarvis and Alsek Rivers. The claims are located within Champagne and Aishihik First Nations (CAFN) Traditional Territory (TT) and the access road is located within CAFN, White River and Kluane First Nations TTs. Project activities are planned to occur between April 25th to October 15th annually for a period of ten years, beginning in 2016.    The Haines Junction Designated Office provided an opportunity for comment on the Project during the Seeking Views and Information period from March 17th to April 6th, 2016. Comments were submitted by the WRFN, Government of Yukon (YG), KFN, Department of Fisheries and Oceans (DFO), CAFN and the local outfitter. Within comment submissions, concerns that were raised spoke to new access, use of existing access roads, heritage resources, water quality, the environment, wildlife, and fish. The Designated Office identified four valued components in the assessment: water quality, environmental quality, wildlife and wildlife habitat and heritage resources. It was determined by the Designate Office that the Project will have significant adverse effects on all four valued components. However, by implementing the recommended mitigation measures in the Evaluation Report, in addition to those that the Proponent has committed to and relevant legislative requirements, the Designated Office is satisfied that the adverse effects can be reduced, controlled or eliminated such that they are not significant. The rationale for this determination is presented in the Evaluation Report.The Project is located approximately 43 km southeast of Destruction Bay and 52 km northwest of Haines Junction, straight-line distance.

projectScope.activities: City of Whitehorse (CoW) aims to build a more sustainable community and is going through a building infrastructure consolidation process. The CoW is applying for a land lease from the Government of Yukon to construct and operate a new Municipal Operations Building, including parking as well as storage and maintenance areas for equipment, materials and aggregate. In addition, CoW proposes road development for accessing and servicing the Municipal Operations Building. The project lease area is approximately 6.8 hectares and is located east of the existing commercial properties along Range Road and south of Two Mile Hill. All vehicle access is projected to connect to Range Road; pedestrian access will be developed from Two Mile Hill Road and the City's Public Safety Building. Project activities are anticipated to start in spring 2015 and will be completed by 2017. The Project has a permanent lifespan.Project activities include:• Clearing and preparation of land for construction (up to 6 hectares)• Earthworks (e.g. excavating, trenching, infilling and grading)• Use of heavy equipment and associated fuels• Construction and use of building, parking and staging areas• Access development (total of 700m by 10m wide)• Installation of water, sewer, storm, electrical and communication utilities• Storm water management• Fuel storage for heating: approximately 2200 L of propane and 1100 L of fuel oil ( during development); 4550 L of propane or 4550 L of fuel oil (during operation)• Installation of temporary and permanent fencing• Waste management• Landscaping

projectScope.activities: The Proponent proposes to develop a 114.4 ha granular reserve located at km 156 of the South Klondike Highway for the purposes of quarrying and producing sanding aggregate, crushed surfacing aggregate and granular materials for road projects. The Proponent requires granular resources for highway reconstruction and maintenance activities as existing quarries are not sufficient to meet the need for aggregate. While the total size of aggregate deposit will be determined after geotechnical testing, it is anticipated that up to 2.5 million m3 of granular resources will be available for extraction over a lifespan of 50 years. The Project involves geotechnical testing, the clearing of organics to access the granular resources, as well as the extraction, stockpiling, crushing, screening and transportation of aggregate. The Proponent intends to construct an access road from the South Klondike Highway along an existing trail, and will undertake progressive reclamation of the site. The Project overlaps Traditional Territory for Kwanlin Dün First Nation and Ta'an Kwäch'än Council. The estimated start for the Project is September 2015. Project activities will occur annually for 50 years.Project Activities:Geotechnical testingUse of heavy equipmentHandling and storage of fuel on-site (diesel up to 35000L, gasoline up to 8000L, propane up to 2000L, oils and lubricants up to 600L)Waste management (e.g. burning, firewood available to public, etc.)Water use (up to 200 m3 per day)Trail maintenanceReclamation     - Stockpiling organic matter for use in restoration     - Progressive reclamation.     - Remediation of any contaminated soilsClearing, grubbing and stripping for pit development, initial clearing is 4.5 ha with subsequent clearing of 1 to 2 ha per yearAccess road construction:     - 8 m wide by 550 m long     - Use of granular material as fill sourced from ditch cuts and proposed pit     - Overlaps for 120 m with local firewood trailAggregate extraction and production     - Use of explosives     - Excavation and stockpiling (approximately 10 000 to 15 000 m3 per year)Screening and crushing
projectScope.summary: The proponent, Government of Yukon's Highways and Public Works Department, is proposing the development of a quarry south of Whitehorse at km 156.0 Klondike Highway. The 114 ha gravel reserve was set aside in 2005 in order to better meet the granular resource needs for highway reconstruction and maintenance activities. The development of the site will be used in the immediate future for the production of sanding aggregate, crushed surfacing aggregate and granular materials for road projects in the area. The site will be used for approximately 50 years and pit development will be staged progressively until the source area is exhausted.The proposed project is near the Carcross Cut-off, along the South Klondike Highway. It overlaps both the Golden Horn and Mt. Lorne Community boundaries. The Project is within the Traditional Territories of Kwanlin Dün First Nation and Ta'an Kwäch'än Council, and several parcels of Settlement Land surround the project area.

projectScope.activities: The Project is the repair of three clear span bridge crossings along the Pelly River Road #702. The bridge abutments and decking of the bridges at Willow Creek (km 2.0), Grayling Creek (km 10.0) and Caribou Creek (km 29.0) will be replaced. Project activities are scheduled to occur during summer of 2015; each bridge replacement is expected to take approximately 15 days to complete. Depending on site conditions, contractor availability and budget constraints the Project may take place in a subsequent year.Project activities include:•	Removal of beaver dam on Willow Creek•	Work within the riparian area of Willow Creek, Grayling Creek and Caribou Creek•	Installation of temporary detour road and bridge crossing near each site (within 5-10 m of existing bridge)•	Removal of existing bridge abutments•	Installation of galvanized steel bridge abutments•	Installation of pressure treated wood wing walls•	Use of 750 m3 of gravel excavated from existing gravel pits•	Use of 120 m3 of rip-rap from an existing source located at km 543 of Klondike Highway•	Water use of •	Installation of a temporary self-contained work camp for up to 10 people comprised of RVs•	Active traffic control•	Fuel storage (12 000 L diesel; 4 000 L of gasoline; 400 L propane; 400 L oils and lubricants)•	Installation of erosion and sediment control•	Final reclamation of disturbed areas including seeding with native species in highly erodible areas
projectScope.summary: The Project is the repair of three clear span bridge crossings along the Pelly Ranch Road #702. The bridge abutments and decking of the bridges at Willow Creek (km 2.0), Grayling Creek (km 10.0) and Caribou Creek (km 29.0) will be replaced. Project activities are scheduled to occur during summer of 2015; each bridge replacement is expected to take approximately 15 days to complete. Depending on site conditions, contractor availability and budget constraints the Project may take place in a subsequent year.The Mayo DO sought views and information on the Project between December 2 and 16, 2014. Comments were received from the Selkirk RRC, Yukon Government and Environment Canada. Potential effects noted in comment submissions included effects to fish from the release of contaminants and effects to migratory birds as a result of Project activities.    Analysis of the project effects determined that aquatic resources was the only value likely to be affected by the Project. The Proponent's well-designed mitigations are adequate to reduce significant effects of the Project and no additional terms and conditions are recommended.The bridges are located north west of Pelly Crossing along the Pelly Ranch Road located at km 2.0, 10.0 and 29.0.

projectScope.summary: The proposed Project is the continued operation of the solid waste transfer facility located approximately 4 km south of Stewart Crossing along the Klondike Highway. The current proposal details the next nine years of operations (to 2023) at the site in accordance with the Solid Waste Management Plan and the temporal scope aligns with this plan. The facility will accept municipal solid waste and some recyclables for transfer to the Son of War Eagle Landfill in Whitehorse and a recycling processor in Whitehorse respectively. Comments on the Project were sought for two weeks beginning on December 9, 2014. Comments were received from the Mayo District Renewable Resources Council, Yukon Government and Environment Canada. From comments received and other relevant considerations, water quality was identified as the only value likely to be affected by the Project. The Project design, including Proponent commitments, in addition to legislative requirements are sufficient to mitigate significant adverse effects to water quality in the Project area. projectScope.activities: The temporal scope for the Project is until 2023, and includes the following proposed activities, but excludes any landfilling activities on the reserve land parcel •	Acceptance of the following wastes: municipal solid waste (MSW), construction & demolition waste (C&D), scrap metal, white goods, clean wood and tree trimmings, tires, reusable goods, E-waste, animal carcasses, compostables, and hazardous wastes•	Accepting, storing, and transferring recyclables•	Coverage and compaction (landfilling) of MSW and C&D•	Spatial expansion of facility operations: relocation of stockpiled tires, autobodies, scrap metals, and burn pit onto eastern side reserve land parcel•	Operation of a burn vessel for burning brush, cardboard, and clean wood•	Removal and landfilling of accumulated ash from burn vessel/burn pit •	Surface water improvement works: construction of ditches, infiltration ponds, installation of culverts•	Groundwater and surface water quality monitoring•	Heavy equipment use•	Use of an electric fence•	Diesel fuel storage (400 L)•	Site management (inspections, maintenance)
projectScope.summary: The Project is currently in an Additional Seeking Views and Information PeriodThe Proponent, City of Dawson, is proposing to continue operations and expand the Quigley Solid Waste Management Facility (SWMF) located in Dawson City. To enable the operation of the facility, renewals of their Solid Waste Permit, Air Emissions Permit, and the Special Waste Permit are required. The SWMF serves Dawson City, and the Tr'ondëk Hwëch'in First Nations residential areas, and is projected to receive 24 314 tonnes of municipal solid waste and 9 370 tonnes of construction and demolition waste from 2014 to 2023.    The Proposal was submitted on November 12, 2014, and the Dawson Designated Office issued an Information Request on November 24, 2014. The Proponent submitted a response to the Information Request on February 13, 2015, at which point the Dawson Designated Office determined that the response was insufficient, and supplemental information was required from the Proponent. A second Information Request was issued on February 24, 2015, which requested additional information regarding the proposed facility expansion in particular. A response was submitted on April 8, 2015, and the Proposal was determined to be adequate on April 16, 2015.Comments received during Seeking Views and Information from Tr'ondëk Hwëch'in raised concerns regarding surface water quality and settlement land. In addition, Environment Yukon recommended updates to the Proponent's Spill Contingency Plan. Based on these comments, the Designated Office issued an Information Request following Seeking Views and Information on May 20, 2015. Under the Designated Office rules, the Proponent has until June 17, 2015 to either submit the requested information, or to advise our office in writing when the information will be submitted.     A response to the Information Request was submitted on June 19, 2015. Following review of the response, the Designated Office determined that the response was insufficient. An Information Request was issued on June 24, 2015, which requested supplemental information pertaining to the revised expansion plan. The Proponent responded on July 6, 2015, and based on material changes to the project proposal that was submitted since the evaluation commenced, the Designated Office determined that an Additional Seeking Views and Information period was necessary. comments can be received until July 23, 2015.The Project is located approximately 5 km east of Dawson City, immediately south of the Dawson City Yukon Government Transportation Maintenance Complex. The Dredge Pond Country Residential Subdivision is the nearest developed area to the west, approximately 1.3 km to nearest residence. The Bear Creek Mining Complex is the nearest development area to the east, approximately 2.0 km cross-country. The facility is located within the Klondike River Watershed, and the Tr'ondëk Hwëch'in First Nation Traditional Territory.

records.title: Quigley Solid Waste Facility, City of Dawson, Yukon

projectScope.summary: The proposed project is a commercial wilderness guide certification operation and associated activities within Kluane National Park and Reserve (KNP&R).  The proposal was reviewed to determine whether the information was adequate to commence an assessment, and the proposal was deemed Adequate on Jan 29, 2015.  The Haines Junction Designated Office provided an opportunity for comment on the Project during the Seeking Views and Information period from January 29th to February 12th, 2015. Comments were submitted by the Kluane First Nation.  The Designated Office identified four valued components in the assessment: fish and wildlife, human and environmental health, visitor experience and heritage resources. Included in heritage resources was also a consideration of potential impacts to traditional harvesting activities. It was determined by the Designated Office that the Project will not result in significant adverse effects to the above-mentioned valued components upon implementation of commitments and mitigations proposed by the Proponent, in addition to compliance with the relevant legislation.The Project is located on established or existing routes within (KNP&R).  The nearest communities are Haines Junction, Destruction Bay and Burwash Landing.  The nearest watercourses and waterbodies to the Project are Slim River, Sheep Creek, Bullion Creek, Canada Creek, Alsek River, Kluane Lake, Kathleen Lake, Louise Lake, Donjek River, Copper Joe Creek, St Elias Lake, Mush Lake, and the Dezadeash River.  The Project is located within the Southwest Assessment District, within the traditional territories of the Champagne and Aishihik First Nations, Kluane First Nation, and the White River First Nation.

projectScope.summary: The Proponent is proposing to operate a placer mine on one claim located on an unnamed tributary to Dominion Creek, situated within the Indian River watershed. Project activities are proposed from May 1 to October 31, for five years.      The Proposal was submitted on April 9, 2017. Following review of the submitted documents, the Designated Office determined that supplemental information was required, and thus an Information Request was issued on April 27, 2016. The Proponent provided additional information on May 5, 2016. Based on the information submitted as part of the Proponent's response, additional information was again required, and thus a second Information Request was issued on May 10, 2016. The Proponent provided the requested information on May 29, 2016, and the Designated Office determined that the Proposal was adequate on June 15, 2016. The Project was in Seeking Views and Information from June 15, 2016 until June 29, 2016. Based on comments received the Designated Office issued an Information Request which requested additional information on the Proponent's proposed access into the project area. The Proponent provided the additional information on July 12, 2016, and the Project has moved into Reviewing Information.The Project is located approximately 60 km from Dawson City and is located within the Traditional Territories of the Tr'ondëk Hwëch'in and Nacho Nyäk Dun.

projectScope.activities: The purpose of the proposed project is for all-season harvesting of fire-killed timber and access development within five new fuelwood harvest sites in the Fox Lake Timber Harvest Plan (FL2-1a & FL2-2, FL2-3, FL2-4, FL2-5).The Proponent proposes to construct an access road from km 266 on the North Klondike Highway to reach the harvest sites located east of the highway. The Project is located approximately 87 km from Whitehorse and 90 km from Carmacks.The Proponent anticipates construction of the road to take two weeks with a start date of August 16, 2014. Harvesting activities are to commence in September 2014 with an expected lifetime of five-years (depending on fuelwood demands, this period could be extended).Project Activities Include:• Use of heavy equipment for harvesting and road construction (e.g. feller-buncher, rubber tired skidder, crawler tractor, log loader, haul truck, skid steer, snowmobile/ATV/pick-up truck, etc.)• Storage of fuel (Road Construction and Site Prep• Construction of all-season roads (~ 1 535 m in length by 6 m wide), temporary spur roads (~ 240 m in length by 6 m wide) and development of skid trails• Clearing of staging areas on cut blocks (5 x 0.25 m)Fuelwood Harvest• Harvesting and processing of fuel wood (~ 1 960 m3 fire-killed wood)• Salvage harvest of burned, merchantable stems with 10% retention• Disposal of logging debris by lop and scatter• Decommissioning of roads and staging areas after completion of post-harvest silviculture activities
projectScope.summary: The Project proposes the development of five new timber harvest blocks within the Little Fox Lake Timber Harvest area. There is already a timber harvest plan (THP) in place for this location. The Proponent intends to construct a 3 km network of roads to access the harvest blocks, and clear land for skid trails and landings on-site. Access will be gated and the project area will be reclaimed upon completion of silviculture activities. There is no adjacent or overlapping Settlement Land; however the Project is within traditional territory for four First Nations (Kwanlin Dun, Little Salmon Carmacks, Champagne/Aishihik and Ta'an Kwach'an).  The Project is currently in the preparing recommendation stage. The deadline for the evaluation report with its recommendations is Wednesday August 6, 2014.The Project is approximately 87 km from Whitehorse. Located at km 266, it is on the east side of the North Klondike Highway within the Little Fox Lake Timber Harvest area.

projectScope.summary: The Proponent, Kluane Community Development Corporation (KCDC) proposed a three-weekgeotechnical testing program on their private fee-simple land, Lot 2 Group 852, in Burwash Landing.The Project involves the use of a truck-mounted drill rig to dig up to 40 holes to determine suitableland for future development. No water is required for this project, and a camp is not proposed. Thework is proposed to occur in the winter of 2014.During the two-week Seeking Views and Information period, comments were submitted by KluaneFirst Nation (KFN). Two valued components were identified: environmental quality and heritageresources. The Designated Office determined that with the implementation of the Proponent'smitigation measures and relevant non-discretionary legislation, significant adverse effects would notoccur from proposed project activities. Under s. 56(1)(a) of the Yukon Environmental and Socio-economic Assessment Act, on Feb 3, 2014 the Haines Junction Designated Office recommended to the Decision Bodies for the Project that the Project beallowed to proceed, as it determined that the Project will not have significant adverse environmental and socio-economic effects in or outside Yukon.The decision body accepted the recommendation of the Designated Office, that the Project proceed, in a Decision Document issued on May 10, 2014.The Project is located on private fee-simple land within the community of Burwash Landing, on the shores of Kluane Lake.  It is located within the Southwest Assessment district, within the Traditional Territories of the Kluane First Nation and White River First Nation.

projectScope.summary: Silver Predator Corp. is proposing to maintain and remove an existing camp located adjacent to the Plata Airstrip in east central Yukon located approximately 190 km east of Mayo and 150 km north east of Faro. The pre-existing camp was developed to support exploration work in the area under a previous mining licence (LQ00231). Silver Predator Corp. is not contemplating exploration work in the area at this time due to current market conditions. Maintenance activities are scheduled to occur between May 15th and September 30th for the next three years (to September 30, 2017) with final site reclamation scheduled within that time. All movement of people and equipment will occur by aircraft.      The Mayo Designated Office solicited public comments on the Project between January 19 and February 2, 2015. During that period, comments were received from Yukon Conservation Society and Yukon Government. From the comments received and other relevant considerations, the Mayo Designated Office determined that the Project was unlikely to adversely affect any valued environmental or socio-economic components in the Project area. The Mayo Designated Office recommends that the Decision Body allow the Project to proceed as proposed.The Decision Body, Yukon Government, will review the Recommendation and the accompanying reasons described in this Evaluation Report. The Decision Body will issue a Decision Document within 30 days, as prescribed under s. 2 of the Decision Body Time Periods and Consultation Regulations, that will either a) accept the recommendation, b) vary the recommendation, or c) reject the recommendation.Located adjacent to the Plata Airstrip in east central Yukon located approximately 190 km east of Mayo and 150 km north east of Faro

projectScope.summary: This Project entails a Class 4 Mining Land Use permit and Water Use licence for placer mining on 33 claims along Nansen Creek (24 claims) and Discovery Creek (9 claims). Mining will occur over a phased approach. Access for the Project is from the Mount Nansen Road. Located approximately 62 km from Carmacks, the Project is within the White River Watershed. The Project is within Traditional Territory of the Little Salmon Carmacks First Nation. Activities will occur annually from mid-May to mid-September for 10 years.  The Designated Office gathered input from government stakeholders and interested members of the public during seeking views and information (January 26 - February 9, 2015). During this stage of the assessment, comments were received from the Department of Fisheries and Oceans Canada, a member of the public, Environment Canada and Yukon Government. Based on comments provided, as well as other relevant considerations, the Designated Office identified four valued environmental or socio-economic components (VESECs): aquatic resources, wildlife and wildlife habitat, environmental quality and heritage resources. It was determined that the Project was likely to result in significant adverse effects to all VESECs identified. Additional mitigations are recommended to reduce, eliminate or control significant adverse effects identified to each VESEC.Located approximately 62 km from Carmacks

projectScope.summary: The Proponent has applied for a 1.98 hectare (0.02 km2) lease at Canyon Creek to establish a sawmill operation that will include accessory activities such as log sorting, log and equipment storage, and lumber processing utilizing a portable sawmill.  Two potential options for accessing the site were presented, as described below in the Project Scope. Site preparation and construction of the access road, the onsite loop road and the fuel storage shed are proposed for the first year, with construction of additional buildings in subsequent years. Sawmill operations and associated uses would begin in the first year and continue for 20 years.    The Project proposal was submitted on December 18th, 2015. Following completion of an adequacy review, the Haines Junction Designated Office determined that additional information was required and issued an Information Request to the Proponent on December 30th, 2015. The Proponent supplied the requested information on February 12th, 2016. The proposal was deemed adequate on February 12th, 2016.The Designated Office provided an opportunity for comment on the Project during the initial Seeking Views and Information period from February 12th to February 29th, 2016. This period was extended until March 17th following two requests for extension from Government of Yukon, Department of Energy Mines and Resources, Land Client Services Branch (the Decision Body for the Project) and Champagne and Aishihik First Nations (CAFN). Comments were submitted by nine local residents, CAFN, and six departments of Government of Yukon. Concerns that were raised in the comment submissions included potential impacts of the Project on nearby residential properties from noise, smoke, dust and pollutants; competition with and economic impacts on another local business; safety concerns with the proposed access routes; and the lack of land use planning in the Canyon area.The Designated Office identified four valued components in the assessment: Environmental Quality, Wildlife and Wildlife Habitat, Other Land Uses and Interests, and Health and Safety. It was determined by the Designated Office that the Project will have significant adverse effects on three of these: Environmental Quality, Wildlife and Wildlife Habitat, and Health and Safety. However, by implementing the recommended mitigation measures in the Evaluation Report, in addition to those that the Proponent has committed to and application of relevant legislative requirements, the Designated Office is satisfied that the adverse effects can be eliminated, reduced or controlled such that they are not significant. The rationale for this determination is presented in the Evaluation Report. The Evaluation Report and Recommendation was issued on April 27th, 2016. The Designated Office recommended to the Decision Body (Government of Yukon) that the Project be allowed to proceed, subject to specified terms and conditions.The Decision Document was issued on June 16th, 2016. The Decision Body varied the Recommendation.The Project location is approximately 35 km east of Haines Junction near Canyon Creek.

projectScope.summary: The proposed Project is the continued operation of a Class 4 placer mine on 76 claims at Burwash Creek (a tributary of the Kluane River) and Tatamagouche Creek (a tributary of Burwash Creek). TheProject is an amendment of an existing water licence and mining land use permit for 2016, and a renewal for an additional ten years. The claims are located within Kluane First Nation (KFN) and White River First Nation Traditional Territories, and overlap with KFN Settlement Land blocks R-1A and R-49B. Project activities are planned to occur between March 1st and October 15th annually for a period of eleven years, beginning in 2016.The proposal was submitted on March 1st, 2016 and was reviewed by the Haines Junction Designated Office to determine whether there was adequate information to commence the assessment. The Designated Office determined that additional information was required and issued an Information Request to the Proponent on March 10th. The Proponent provided a response on March 16th. However, the Designated Office determined that clarification and supplementary information was required, and a second Information Request was issued on March 21st. The Proponent supplied the requested information on March 23rd, and the proposal was deemed adequate on March 24th.The Designated Office provided an opportunity for comment on the Project during the initial Seeking Views and Information (SV&I) period from March 24th to April 7th, 2016. The SV&I period was extended until April 12th, 2016 following a request for extension from KFN.  Comments were submitted by the Department of Fisheries and Oceans (DFO) Canada, six departments of Government of Yukon (YG), KFN, and WRFN. Concerns raised in comment submissions were related to the potential for Project activities to adversely impact water and soil quality, wildlife and wildlife habitat, biodiversity, and heritage resources.The Designated Office identified four valued components in the assessment: Aquatic Resources, Environmental Quality, Wildlife and Wildlife Habitat, and Heritage Resources. It was determined that the Project will have significant adverse effects on all of the identified valued components. However, by implementing the recommended mitigation measures in this report, in addition to those that the Proponent has committed to and application of relevant legislative requirements, the Designated Office is satisfied that the adverse effects can be eliminated, reduced or controlled such that they are not significant. The rationale for this determination is presented in the Evaluation Report.      The Evaluation Report and Recommendation was issued on May 19th, 2016. The Designated Office recommended to the Decision Body (Government of Yukon) that the Project be allowed to proceed, subject to specified terms and conditions. The Decision Document was issued on June 30th, 2016. The Decision Body varied the Recommendation.The Project is located approximately 20 km southwest of Burwash Landing, within the Kluane Wildlife Sanctuary.

projectScope.activities: The proposed project is for the continued operation and management of the Beaver Creek Solid Waste Disposal Facility (SWDF). projectScope.activities: The purpose of the proposed project is the construction of a driveway to a new lot that will be created by the subdivision of lot 1104 on the Mayo Road (North Klondike Highway). The Proponent has applied to the Government of Yukon for a land use permit to build a driveway that will connect the newly created lot to the highway via Boreal Road. Crossing both the right-of-way for the Boreal Road, and a buffer strip of crown land (6m), the Project entails clearing vegetation, bringing in infill and placing a culvert for the construction of the driveway. The Project is located at approximately km 6.5 of the Mayo Road, which is approximately 20 km north of downtown Whitehorse. Project activities are anticipated to commence in the fall of 2014 and will take approximately 2 days to complete. The Project has an indefinite lifespan. There will be no storage of fuel or machinery on-site. Project Activities Include:•	Construction of a gravel surfaced driveway (approximately 21m long by 6m wide)•	Clearing and grading of land •	Application of 20 m3 of infill•	Installation of a culvert•	Use of heavy equipment•	Waste management
projectScope.summary: The Proponent, Bobby Stevenson, has submitted a proposal to develop a driveway to a new lot that will be created by the subdivision of her property (lot 1104) on the Mayo Road. The driveway will be 21 m long and 6 m wide. 6 m of the driveway will be constructed on Crown land and 15m on private property (lot 1104). Project activities are anticipated to commence in the fall of 2014 and will take approximately 2 days to complete. The Project is open for public comments from August 28 until September 11, 2014.The Project is located on Boreal Road, which branches off the Mayo Road at approximately km 6.5 and is approximately 20 km north of downtown Whitehorse. Hotsprings Road is approximately 650 m south of the junction Boreal Road/Mayo Road.

projectScope.summary: ATAC Resources Ltd, is proposing to continue advanced exploration work on their Rackla Gold Property located approximately 185 km northeast of Mayo. The Project is a continuation of past operations (previously assessed under YOR 2011-0050) with a primary focus in three distinct zones located in the eastern half of the claim block. This renewal includes exploration work on additional claims in four distinct blocks along the northern boundary of the previous claim block. Project activities are proposed between May 1st and October 31st for the next ten years.Project activities include those common to advanced quartz exploration such as drilling, trenching, line cutting, road development, camp use and development and explosives use. The Project is considered relatively large and covers an extensive area (~100 km2). Public comments were sought on the Project beginning February 22, 2016. At the request of the First Nation of Na-Cho Nyäk Dun, the Seeking Views and Information (SVI) period was extended to March 15, 2015. During the SVI period(s), all commenters raised concern regarding the proposed 40 km of new roads to be developed. An affected outfitter noted the potential impacts of the extensive road development and new camps in an important area of his outfitting concession. Based on the comments received and other relevant considerations, outfitting, wildlife, environmental quality and heritage resources were identified as valued environmental and socio-economic components likely to be adversely affected by the Project. Significant adverse effects to the values identified can be adequately reduced, eliminated or controlled through the application of additional mitigation measures.The Project is located approximately 185 km northeast of Mayo.

projectScope.activities: •	Clearing of an additional walking trail along the eastern bank of Deadman Creek up to 6 km in length with a maximum width of less than 1.5 m.•	Sampling for presence and distribution of juvenile Chinook using minnow trapping in Deadman Creek.•	Collection of habitat suitability information (e.g. water temperature, substrate suitability, water velocity/discharge).•	Scouting egg planting sites in Deadman Creek and Morley River •	Brood stock collection - eggs from up to 15 to 20 females, milt from up to 35 to 45 males•	Long-term deployment goal of up to 50 000 to 75 000 fertilized Chinook eggs into Deadman Creek and 5 000 to 6 000 fertilized eggs into Morley River. Shorter-term deployment (i.e. two years) will see 20 000 to 30 000 eggs deployed in Deadman Creek.•	Monitoring of winter conditions and incubation success.
projectScope.summary: Teslin Tlingit Council (TTC) is proposing a Project directed at the restoration of a spawning Chinook salmon population in Deadman Creek. This Project follows from a successful 2016 trial project (YESAB Project 2016-0124). Project activities will be focused on the lower reaches of Deadman Creek and will make use of the Morley River as a control stream from Morley Bay in Teslin Lake to Morley Lake. Project activities other than monitoring are proposed to occur as soon as relevant authorizations are granted in 2017 for a full Chinook salmon cycle (6-8 years). Monitoring activities are expected to continue for a full Chinook salmon cycle beyond egg planting activities, until approximately 2033.Both watercourses are located in the Teslin River watershed and fall within TTC Traditional Territory. Deadman Creek is located approximately 30 km northwest from the community of Teslin; the mouth of the Morley River is approximately 15 km southeast.

projectScope.activities: The Proponent applied to Government of Yukon for title on a 5 ha parcel in the Kulan Industrial subdivision for the purpose of developing an RV storage facility, including access road. The proposed project involves clearing and grubbing of land, installation of a power line, and re-routing the Trans Canada Trail. It is intended to address the growing need for storage because of smaller lot sizes in higher density residential development. Located on the Traditional Territory of the Kwanlin Dün First Nation, the property is accessed from Laberge Road in the Kulan Industrial subdivision, 3.5 km from downtown Whitehorse. Situated behind several road-front properties, the proposed lot is within the Whitehorse 2010 Official Community Plan applicable zoning area: "future planning". There is a laneway to the edge of the property line. The Proponent will upgrade and extend this laneway as an access road along the entirety of the property boundary. The Proponent anticipates clearing, grubbing, and road work will begin fall of 2015 with completion of additional activities in late summer/fall of 2016.Project activities will include:Access road construction and upgrades     - clear and grub (approximately 8 755 m2)     - infill with gravel road mix base     - initial 125 m will be 25 m wide while following 300 m will be 30 m wideLot preparation     - clear and grub (approximately 54 000 m2)     - infill with gravel (approximately 3 823 m3)     - fence perimeter of lotPower line installation     - 1 power pole, 1 transformer, 1 anchorStorage construction     - 1 office building     - 150 - 175 storage unitsTrail building     - clear (approximately 180 m x 3 m)Waste management     - salvage trees 10 cm (4 inches) and over     - mulch and remove all woody debris     - dispose of garbage in landfill     - use of porta-potty on-siteHeavy equipment use
projectScope.summary: The Proponent, Randy Carey, is proposing to build an RV storage facility in the Kulan Industrial Area. This will involve clearing the site, creating an access road, installing powerpoles/powerline to the property, and re-routing the Trans Canada Trail which currently intersects the project area.      The Proponent proposes to level the five hectare lot and top with crushed, packed gravel. This will include the clearing, grubbing, and removing of all debris. The debris will be moved to the Proponents's existing property on Laberge Road, and all trees wider than four inches will be salvaged. The property will eventually include a 20 by 40 foot office building and 150-175 storage units, bordered by a six foot tall chain link fence. Any garbage will be disposed in rented dumpsters on site, and there will also be a porta-potty on the property. The access road will be a total of 425 m long on the north side of the property boundary.The Project resides approximately 7.8 km south of downtown Whitehorse, on the western edge of Kulan Industrial Area. It is within Kwanlin Dün Traditional Territory, and approximately 200 m away from the nearest parcel of Settlement Land (KDFN C 53-B).

projectScope.activities: The purpose of this Project is the remediation of the Marwell Tarpit, a 'Yukon Designated Contaminated Site', in order to protect human and environmental health. Located within the industrial area of downtown Whitehorse, the Project is on the Traditional Territory of the Ta'an Kwäch'än First Nation and the Kwanlin Dün First Nation. The site contains hydrocarbon contamination (e.g. LEPH and HEPHs). For the majority of contaminated material, remediation will entail use of thermal desorption i.e. removal of organic contaminants from soil, sludge or sediment, and/or stabilization/solidification i.e. binding of treated soil with cement and other binders/additives for final disposal at a designated landfill. There is the possibility that the Proponent may choose another remediation approach (outlined in the project proposal), which would likely result in similar impacts to environmental and socio-economic values. Selected volumes of contaminated soil may be transported off-site for treatment. The Project is anticipated start in spring 2015 and end in fall 2016 with ongoing monitoring and maintenance until summer of 2021.Project activities will include:- Site preparation and temporary stockpile construction- Use of heavy equipment and associated fuels (gasoline, stored in 20 L jerry cans, and diesel, not stored on-site)- Land clearing and grubbing of vegetation (done incrementally)- Construction of temporary access road (150 m x 6.5 m) using aggregate fill (1000 m3)- Establish stockpile area of contaminated material (approximately 70 x 100 m)- Surface water management, including water treatment- Waste management (e.g. no burning of brush, only separation of felled logs and mulching of brush for reclamation)- Use of water sprays for dust controlExcavation/segregation- Soil excavation (approximately 25 700 m3)- Segregation of contaminated and clean soil (approximately 18 250 m3 and 7 500 m3 respectively) for temporary stockpilingTreatment/removal of contaminated material- Fuel storage for thermal desorption unit (propane, stored in 18 000 L aboveground storage tank)- Disposal of special waste- Transport of contaminated soil off-site to approved location for treatment (approx. 41 000 tonnes or 600 truckloads)- Site restoration (e.g. seeding, etc.)Post remediation Monitoring and Maintenance- Surface water and sediment sampling/monitoring- Groundwater monitoring
projectScope.summary: The Yukon Government, via the Department of Environment's Site Assessment and Remediation Unit, is proposing to remediate the Marwell Tar Pit. The Marwell Tar Pit is classified as a 'Designated Contaminated Site' under the Government of Yukon's Environment Act (1998), and has been identified as Yukon's largest single-source hydro-carbon contaminated site. The first part of the project will involve excavation of the tar pit and segregation of contaminated soils from non- contaminated soils. The second part of the project will include treatment and removal of the stockpiled contaminated material. The goal upon completion of this project is that the site could eventually be used for industrial land use following remediation.The Marwell Tar Pit is located at the foot of the Takhini East bluffs in Whitehorse's Marwell Industrial Area, less than 500 m from Two Mile Hill, and just north of Downtown.

A two person campsite will be established over an area of approximately 0.25 ha, situated approximately 13 m from the ordinary high water mark (OHWM) of the Teslin River.The proposed project will occur seasonally from July to September for two years with a possible one year extension.

Camps may be established at this location over the next ten years, from 2015-2025, for an approximate duration of 10 to 40 days.Project Activities:•	Establishment of a camp for up to 50 workers; over the ten-year period, camps will often be much smaller (10-15 people)•	Camp may include up to 20 trailers for living quarters, a kitchen and wash-up trailer•	Waste management: domestic and human waste management, which may include a septic leach field•	Potable water for camp will be delivered or a drinking water well will be established•	Establishment of an equipment service area for heavy machinery and fuel storage•	Fuel storage in fuel trucks, tidy tanks, or Enviro tanks: diesel (65 000 L), gasoline (8 000 L), propane (60 000 L) and oils and lubricants (900 L)
projectScope.summary: Government of Yukon - Department of Highways and Public Works submitted a proposal for establishing a temporary camp, equipment storage/service area and fuel storage area within an existing gravel pit at Bear Flats.  This temporary camp would serve contractor operations for the Shakwak Highway Project, including reconstruction and paving, as well as repair and maintenance of Alaska Highway surface affected by permafrost.  The proposal was reviewed to determine whether the information was adequate to commence the assessment.  The adequacy review identified information gaps in project information, and additional information was requested from the Proponent.  The Proponent supplied the requested information (YOR document 2014-0212-010-1), and the proposal was deemed Adequate on Dec 30, 2014.  The Project went into Seeking Views and Information on December 9, 2014 with a comment submission deadline of January 13, 2015. Comments were submitted by Canadian Wildlife Service, White River First Nation, Government of Yukon and Kluane First Nation. The Designated Office identified two valued components in the assessment: environmental quality and wildlife and wildlife habitat. It was determined by the Designate Office that the Project will have significant adverse effects on environmental quality that can be mitigated and recommended on Jan 23, 2015 that the Project proceed with terms and conditions.  The Decision Document was issued on Mar 30, 2015.The Project is located at km 1815.9 of the Alaska Highway.  The nearest community is Beaver Creek, situated 54 km to the north of the Project.  The nearest watercourses are White River and Koidern River.  The Project is located within the Southwest Assessment District, in the traditional territories of the Kluane First Nation and White River First Nation.

projectScope.summary: The Proponent, Northern Freegold Resources Ltd, proposes to continue and expand a Class 3 Exploration program located within the larger Freegold Mountain Property, a large consolidated claim block owned by Northern Freegold Resources Ltd., focusing on the Revenue and Nucleus mineral deposits. Extensive exploration work has occurred in the Revenue Project area previously. An intensive drilling and trenching program was conducted on the property from 2006-2012 in the Freegold Mountain area. The Project is located approximately 60 km from Carmacks at the terminus of the Freegold Road and the start of the Casino Trail. Project activities are proposed to occur annually for five years from April 1 - November 30, 2015. The Mayo Designated Office solicited views and information on the proposed project from March 4 - March 19, 2015, at which time a request for additional information was issued regarding the extent of existing access and reclamation. During this period, comments were submitted by Yukon Conservation Society, Environment Canada, Yukon Government, and Selkirk First Nation. Based on the comments received and other relevant considerations, four valued environmental and socio-economic components were identified: Environmental Quality; Fish and Fish Habitat; and Wildlife and Wildlife Habitat.The Mayo Designated Office determined that the Project will result in significant adverse effects to all valued components with the exception of Health and Safety, but that the application of existing legislation, the Proponent's commitments (Appendix A), as well as the recommended mitigation measures (listed below) are adequate to mitigate the significant adverse effects of the Project.Approximately 53 km northwest of Carmacks

projectScope.activities: The scope developed for the Project is as follows:EFLO Energy Yukon Ltd. is proposing to complete workovers on two existing wells (B-38 and I-48), develop a quarry at the Kotaneelee Gas Field, and renew their Air Emissions Permit (60-009). Depending on when the authorizations are received to begin work, the well workovers will take approximately three months to complete and may occur in the winter (approximately January to March 15, 2015) or in the summer (June to September 15, 2015). The Quarry development is expected to occur during the summer of 2015.   Project Activities:•	Two well workovers (B-38 and I-48)•	Development of a quarry•	Water injection into the M-17 well and/or disposal at an off-site facility•	Workover fluid disposal •	Operation/maintenance of a low pressure flare stack, an amine scrubber tower, a glycol scrubber tower, produced water storage tanks, wellheads (B-38, I-48 and L38). •	Use of existing camp and re-establishment and use of temporary camp•	Drilling of groundwater monitoring wells•	Use of existing winter road (January - March)•	Use of existing summer access road from barge landing (NWT) to the site (Yukon) (June - September)•	Use of existing airstrip•	Waste management•	Fuel storage*Note: This project does not propose hydraulic fracturing (fracking).
projectScope.summary: EFLO Energy Yukon Ltd. is proposing to complete workovers on two existing wells (B-38 and I-48) an to develop a quarry at the Kotaneelee Gas Field in southeast Yukon. The site will be accessed via an existing winter road beginning in B.C. and continuing to the project site. The Proponent is proposing to begin these activities in January 2015 and be completed by March 15, 2015.The Project is located in the southeast Yukon assessment district, in the extreme southeast corner of the Yukon, near the NWT and BC borders. The site is located approximately 270km east of Watson Lake. The closest community is Fort Liard, approximately 35km away in the NWT.The Project is located within the traditional territories of the Liard First Nation and the Ross River Dena.Nearby waterbodies include the La Biche River and tributaries.

projectScope.activities: The Proponent proposes to establish 35 campsites for RVs, truck campers, trailers and walk-in-tent campers. The Project entails upgrading 0.9 km of an existing access road and constructing an additional 0.6 km of new road to reach the campground, as well as the establishment of common use areas (e.g. cook shelter, playground, firewood storage cribs and water wells, etc.). The Proponent anticipates clearing of land to commence in fall and winter of 2014 over a period of one month. While road construction work may occur in fall of 2014 with completion in summer 2015 for a period of three months. The installation and construction of campsite infrastructure is in 2015 with the campground opening spring of 2016. The Proponent intends the campground to be summer-use only. The operation and maintenance of the campground is indefinitely.Project activities include:- upgrading and extending approximately 1.5 km of gravel roadway within the campground- building camping facilities (e.g. camp sites, sanitary facilities, water wells, picnic shelters, playground, firewood cribs, etc.)- creating approximately 2.5 km of new walking trails- clearing and grading of land- storing fuel- using heavy equipment- maintaining and operating the campground
projectScope.summary: The purpose of the proposed project is the development of a campground to expand recreational and camping opportunities in the Southern Lakes district. The project intent is also a means to preserve the nearby Conrad historic site from further disturbance by redirecting unregulated camping that occurs on the historic site.Due to the nature of land dispositions to affect and alter the character of land and/or a community in combination with the traditional and historical importance of Carcross and Conrad, the Project was initially in Seeking Views and Information for three weeks with an extension of one week to allow opportunity for comment from interested parties. The Designated Office gathered input from government stakeholders and interested members of the public during seeking views and information (July 22 to August 20, 2014), as well as two public meetings held in Carcross and Whitehorse (July 30 and July 31, 2014). As a result of concerns raised during this period, the Designated Office issued an additional information request on August 26, 2014. The Proponent has not yet provided a response to the IR.The Project is located along the western shore of Windy Arm on Tagish Lake, adjacent to the historic site of Conrad, approximately 16 km south of Carcross, and is reached via the South Alaska Highway. It is within the traditional territory of the Carcross Tagish First Nation.
