projectScope.summary: The proposal is to conduct class 4 placer mining on 12 claims centered along Back Creek near Victoria Mountain, located approximately 45 km west of Carmacks. The Project is located on the uppermost claims on Back Creek and abuts Little Salmon/Carmacks Settlement Land block LSC R-31A. Project activities are proposed to occur between April 1st and September 15h annually for the next ten years.      Project activities include works typical of placer mining operations in the area including extensive watercourse diversion, processing of economical material through a wash plant and final site reclamation and closure. The Mayo Designated Office sought views and information on the Project from February 12th, 2015 to February 26, 2015. Comments about the Project were received from the Department of Fisheries and Oceans Canada, Transport Canada, Environment Canada, and Yukon Government.From comments received and other relevant considerations, environmental quality, wildlife and wildlife habitat and environmental quality were identified as valued environmental components likely to be affected by the proposed Project. Additional mitigation measures are recommended to mitigate significant adverse effects from the project to the values identified.Approximately 45 km west of Carmacks

projectScope.summary: Tusk Explorations Ltd. has submitted a proposal for a Class 4 placer mining operation on lower Sulphur Creek. The Project includes 19 contiguous claims. The proposed timeline for project activities is for the duration of the applied Water License and Mining Land Use Permit, which is 10 years. This property was part of a larger claim block recently assessed by the Dawson Designated Office as project # 2014-0006: Sulphur Creek and Brimstone Gulch. The proponent for that project was Lucky Lady Placers. The claim block was part of several other Mining Land Use Permits that pre-date YESAA (LP 00014, 00123, 00429).One information request was issued in order to clarify a discrepancy in the application regarding the use of diversions. The response indicated that the Project would not include any diversions. The designated office posted their recommendation to the online registry on June 3, 2014.The Project is located in the North Yukon Assessment District within the traditional territory of the Tr'ondëk Hwëch'in and Na-cho Nyäk Dun. It is approximately 60km southeast  from the nearest community, Dawson City. Sulphur Creek is part of the Indian River watershed, which feeds into the Yukon River. Sulphur Creek and the Indian River have been heavily placer mined over the last 100 years.

projectScope.summary: The Proponent is proposing to continue their placer mining operation on 15 claims along Last Chance and Hunker Creeks. On August 27, 2014 the Dawson Designated Office (DO) requested additional information about the Project. The Proponent responded with additional information on October 29, 2014. On October 31, 2014 the DO determined that the proposal was adequate and the evaluation began. The Project was open for public comment from October 31 to November 14, 2014. During the public comment period, it was noted that information regarding a proposed culvert was not provided. In response to Government of Yukon's comments, the Proponent provided details on the proposed culvert on November 17, 2014. Based on the additional proposal information provided during and after the public comment period, the DO determined that an additional public comment period was necessary. The Project will be open for public comments until Friday, November 28, 2014.The Project is located in the North Yukon Assessment District in the Tr'ondek Hwech'in Traditional Territory. Last Chance and Hunker Creek are tributaries of the Klondike River, and situated in the Yukon River Drainage Area. The Project is located approximately 20 km from Dawson City.

projectScope.summary: The Project will replace two diesel thermal units scheduled for retirement in 2014 and 2015 respectively with newer, more efficient natural-gas fired engines and related infrastructure which will result in reduced fuel costs, reduced greenhouse gas and particulate emissions, and increased capacity and reliability.  This will also in effect modernize the WTGS and retain flexible and reliable thermal grid generation at Whitehorse (including installation of new fossil-fuel fired generation capacity required for reliable grid generation at peak winter loads or during drought conditions).The Project will ensure that Yukon Energy maintains the infrastructure necessary in the next few years to address the YUB's recommended reserve capacity planning criteria requirements, has reserve capacity for seasonal low water periods and drought years, and is able to meet short term non-industrial load growth and peak demand with the least impact on ratepayers.(Extracted from Project proposal section 1.5)

projectScope.activities: The principal activity of the proposed project includes the disposition of a 65 ha parcel of Crown land and conversion of approximately 55 ha of fen wetlands into fields for the purpose of a sod farm. This will include watercourse diversion to drain the wetlands, construction of two dug-out holding ponds and water use for agricultural irrigation in later years of the project; land clearing, trenching and tilling of soil to develop two fields; and construction of an access road and a future homestead. The proposed project is located north of the proponent's existing sod farm in Grizzly Valley along the North Klondike Highway, approximately 33km north of downtown Whitehorse. The project area forms the headwaters of the north-eastern tributary of Horse Creek. The proponent has proposed that the site will be developed over five to six years and sod farming will occur for approximately 25 years after that. The project has an indefinite lifespan as once its viability for sod farming is exhausted the site will be converted to conventional agriculture.

projectScope.activities: Overland Resources Yukon Limited is proposing a Class 3 mineral exploration program at the Selous Project, which consists of 1 554 claims, in the southeast Yukon approximately 125 km north of Ross River. Access will be via the North Canol Road and existing winter trail (Feb - Apr), or aircraft. Activities are scheduled to take place throughout the year (May 1st to April 30th), for 5 years.Project Activities:•	Use of heavy equipment•	Exploration, geophysical and geotechnical sampling, geological mapping, soil sampling •	Environmental monitoring and data collection•	Heritage and socio-economic data collection•	Use of existing camp: maximum 50 people •	Use of existing winter trail annually from Feb to Apr•	Construct new trails: up to 20 km, 4 m wide •	Clearings: up to 15 per claim (20 m2 each if vegetative mat removed; 20 m2 if trees/brush removed), 210 m2 total for up to 14 helipads•	Drill pad construction•	Drilling: up to 50 reverse circulation drill holes (total 5 000 m) and up to 15 diamond drill holes (total 5 000 m)  over a 5-year program •	Helicopter use •	Use of existing airstrip and use of fixed wing aircraft•	Water Use: (approx. •	Waste management •	Fuel storage (120 000L): 76 000 L diesel; 38 000 L Jet B; 2 000 L gasoline; 1 000 lbs. propane•	Progressive reclamation and final camp decommissioning
projectScope.summary: The Proponent, Overland Resources Yukon Limited, submitted a proposal for a Class 3 mineral exploration program, consisting of 1 554 claims, at the Selous Project in southeast Yukon. Access will be via the North Canol Road, existing winter trail and aircraft. Activities are scheduled to take place throughout the year (May 1st to April 30th), for 5 years.The proposal was submitted on March 28, 2014; the Designated Office issued an information request in an effort to obtain and clarify details of project activities. Once the information was received, the Designated Office was able to develop a Project Scope summarizing the main project components. An extension to the initial 21-day public comment period of 14 days was requested and granted. Public input was sought by the Designated Office until June 4, 2014. During this time, the Designated Office received an additional request for an extension and an additional 14-day extension was granted. Public comments were sought on this Project until June 24, 2014. The recommendation report was issued on June 30, 2014.Listed at the bottom of this summary page are the main documents describing the Project. Other documents that may be of interested are available on the Project Details screen including comments submitted in relation to this Project.Located within the Southeast Yukon Assessment District, the Project site is to the west of the North Canol Road, approximately 125 km north of Ross River. The Project is within the traditional territories of the Ross River Dena and the Liard First Nation. The nearest community, by road, is Ross River. By direct flight, Faro is approximately 110 km to the southwest.Water bodies in the area include several unnamed lakes and creeks, tributaries to the South and North Macmillan Rivers and the Riddell River.

projectScope.summary: Duke Ventures Ltd. has submitted a proposal to establish an all season and a winter forest resource road and to harvest 8770 m3 of fire killed trees for fuel wood in the Little Fox Lake timber harvest area. Road construction included the establishment of a bridge and placement of culverts. The project is part of the Little Fox Lake Timber Harvest Plan (Block A and B) . The project is intended to start in summer 2014 and to operate year round for a period of approximately 2 years. It is expected to be completed in April 2016. A camp will be set-up as part of this project, with up to 4 people, resulting in 700 person days by season. As a forest resource road the road will not be open to the public, and the road will be gated. The Project is in Adequacy Review until June 9, 2014. At this date the Designated Office will either submit an information request to the Proponent or deem the Project adequate and move it into the Public Comment Period.The proposed Project is accessed from km 260 North Klondike Highway, where a gate is already established for an existing forest resource road.The Proponent plans to upgrade the first 800m of the existing road and then to build the new road to the proposed harvesting blocks. The route crosses the timber harvest operation of Matthew Carpenter.

projectScope.activities: *Note: The scope of the Project is limited to the installation of the new tanks. The scope does not include the remediation of the existing tank farm site or the re-location of tanks from that site. The Project is the construction and operation of a storage tank system for petroleum products in the village of Old Crow, adjacent to the airport apron. The fuel handling equipment will include a public access card lock for diesel and gasoline. Project activities are expected to commence in 2014 and will be completed by August 2015. The Project will have an indefinite lifespan.Project Activities•	Installation of five new fuel storage units:o	35,000 litre Aviation fuel 100LLo	35,000 litre Jet A Fuelo	45,000 litre Home heating fuel;o	45,000 litre Low sulphur diesel fuelo	45,000 litre Low lead auto gasoline	•	Use of gravel for fill•	Establishment of four new fuel handling equipment enclosures•	Installation of perimeter fence•	Establishment of access road•	Use of heavy equipment•	Waste Management
projectScope.summary: The Project is the establishment of a new fuel tank depot. Five new fuel tanks were delivered to Old Crow via the winter road during the 2014 operating season. These tanks will be used to establish a public card lock, as well as an airport refueling facility. Prior to the submission of this project proposal, an earlier project submission was initiated that involved the establishment of a new fuel depot at the location of the exiting tank farm. The existing tank farm is known to be contaminated through hydrocarbon spill, and will require some form of remediation. The earlier submission also involved the establishment of a land treatment facility. That project was ultimately cancelled. The current Project involves a different location with different proposed activities.The Project is located in the village of Old Crow, adjacent to the airstrip and within the 'airport reservation' land disposition . The Project is occurring on Crown Land.

projectScope.activities: The purpose of the proposed project is the installation of a septic system at the Marsh Lake Bible Camp and the conversion of the existing leach pit into a dry well. The Proponent has applied to the Yukon Water Board for a Type B water licence for a septic system intended to serve between 40 and 70 people. The septic field will measure 12 m x 17.75 m and will be approximately 3 m deep. Excavated materials will be stockpiled and then backfilled. No clearing will be required for the project and no fuel will be stored on-site. Project activities will commence once the required permits are in place, ideally in the fall of 2014 but potentially in the spring of 2015. Project activities will take two days to complete and the expected lifespan of the septic system is 20 years.Project Activities Include:•	Use of heavy equipment (backhoe and dump truck)•	Excavation to a depth of approximately 3 m for installation of the septic system•	Temporary storage of organic materials

projectScope.activities: The purpose of the Project is to close the Keno City Waste Disposal Site. The site will remain as a waste transfer station after the closure of the waste disposal site. The buried materials will be capped with a low permeability material (a compacted local soil). The site will then be contoured to promote drainage away from the buried and capped waste cells. Any waste remaining on site will be disposed of properly. A 25-year groundwater and surface water monitoring plan will be implemented. Additional monitoring for site safety, erosion, and maintenance requirements is planned at regular intervals.

projectScope.activities: The proposed project is for the development of a gravel quarry located north of Crestview within City of Whitehorse. The Project involves clearing of 3.95 ha in multiple stages, starting with an area of 2 ha in the first season. Approximately 350 000 m3 of granular resource (sand/gravel) has been determined to be available for extraction. Material will initially be stockpiled and crushed at the existing Cee & Cee quarry (Land Disposition 930069) on east side of the Alaska Highway, approximately 900 m north of the proposed project. Following the first season, the Proponent anticipates adequate space to allow for screening, crushing and stockpiling on the new quarry site. The estimated start for the Project is spring 2015. Project activities will occur annually for 25 years from May through October.     Project activities will include:• Clearing, stripping and grubbing (2 ha in first year, 0.5 ha for the following five years)• Excavation of aggregate (20 000 m3 to 30 000 m3 per year)• Transportation of aggregate from proposed quarry to existing Cee & Cee quarry for the first season• Screening and crushing of material (approximately 5 000 m3 and 10 000 m3 per year, respectively)• Stockpiling of aggregate for future processing (2 to 3 piles of approximately 3 000 to 4 000 m3)• Construction of storm retention pond• Establishment of bermed staging/laydown area• Use of heavy equipment• Winterization of the site (e.g. removal of equipment)• Development of and upgrades to new haul route and existing site access• Waste management (e.g. portable privy on-site)• Progressive reclamation
projectScope.summary: Cee & Cee Dirt and Gravel Ltd. is proposing to develop a gravel quarry, operating seasonally from May to October over 25 years. The project area is approximately 4.7 ha in total and the quarry will be developed in phases. Up to 350 000 m3 of granular (sand/gravel) aggregate have been estimated to be available for extraction.  The Project will accessed via an existing trail off the Alaska Highway.The project location is within the municipal boundaries of the City of Whitehorse, approximately  11 km northwest of downtown Whitehorse and approximately 3 km southeast of the Alaska and North Klondike Highway junction.  The Project is accessed via an existing trail west of km 1435 of the Alaska Highway, which is approximately 750 m north of Crestview.

projectScope.summary: The proposed project is for educational trips within Kluane National Park and Reserve (KNPR) as part of credit and non-credit courses and seminars offered by Yukon College related to outdoor pursuits and environmental studies. Activities will occur year-round for a period of five years (2016 to 2021). Approximately 10 trips per summer (6 day trips and 4 overnight or multi-day trips) and 20 trips in fall/winter (12 day trips and 8 overnight or multi-day trips) are expected annually.      The Haines Junction Designated Office provided an opportunity for comment on the Project during the Seeking Views and Information period from April 25th to May 9th, 2016. No comments were received during this period.  The Designated Office identified four valued components in the assessment: fish and wildlife, human and environmental health, visitor experience and heritage resources and traditional pursuits. It was determined by the Designated Office that the Project will not result in significant adverse effects to the above-mentioned valued components upon implementation of commitments and mitigations proposed by the Proponent (Appendix A), in addition to compliance with the relevant legislation. For further information, please see the Recommendation in the Evaluation Report.Kluane National Park and Reserve (KNPR).

projectScope.activities: The proposed project involves replacement or movement of an overhead transmission line that spans the Yukon River to connect theeast and west banks of the river in the City of Whitehorse. The project is located near the Kwanlin Dün Cultural Centre on the westbank of the river and on Wickstrom Road on the east river bank. The project entails either replacement of the existing overhead linewith an electrical transmission line under the Yukon River or rerouting of the existing overhead line approximately 110 m furtherupstream. Which transmission option is pursued will be determined by soil suitability, which will be characterized by preliminary siteinvestigations. Should the site be suitable for underground installation, drilling and casing(s) installation will occur for 2 to 3 weeks infall 2012, and installation of the electrical transmission line will occur for 8 weeks during summer 2013. In the case where the site isnot suitable for an underground transmission line, the overhead line will be moved further upstream over the course of 8 weeksbetween May and October 2013 or 2014.

projectScope.summary: Jack and Son Adventures (the Proponent) has proposed to conduct guided hiking activities in Kluane National Park and Reserve (KNPR) for a period of five years. Trips are proposed for up to six people, including guides, and to occur as day and overnight trips. The Proponent estimates up to three trips between June and October beginning in 2016. The proposed routes are the most common routes and areas traveled to by recreational users and other commercial operators. The Haines Junction Designated Office provided an opportunity for comment on the Project during the Seeking Views and Information period from April 15th to 29th, 2016. Comments were submitted by the Kluane First Nation (KFN).  The Designated Office identified four valued components in the assessment: fish and wildlife, human and environmental health, visitor experience and heritage resources and traditional pursuits. It was determined by the Designated Office that the Project will result in significant adverse effects one valued component, human and environmental health. However, by implementing the recommended mitigation measures in the Evaluation Report, in addition to those that the Proponent has committed to and relevant legislative requirements, the Designated Office is satisfied that the adverse effects can be reduced, controlled or eliminated such that they are not significant. The rationale for this determination is presented in the Evaluation Report.The proposed project is a commercial wilderness tourism operation within Kluane National Park and Reserve.

projectScope.summary: Rockhaven Resources Ltd. is proposing to continue quartz exploration work on the Klaza Property consisting of a total of 387 claims.  The Project is located approximately 50 km west of Carmacks.  The site is accessed via an existing road network from the Mt. Nansen Road that begins at the Klondike Highway.  Project activities are scheduled to occur annually between May 15th and November 30th for five years.The Mayo Designated Office sought public input on the Project for two weeks beginning September 30, 2015. During the public input period, comments were received from the Canadian Wildlife Service, Department of Fisheries and Oceans Canada and Yukon Government. From the comments received and other relevant considerations, the Mayo Designated Office determined that the Project would affect three valued environmental and socio-economic components (VESECs). Significant adverse environmental and/or socio-economic effects where predicted to each VESEC. Additional terms and conditions are recommended to reduce, eliminate or control the significant adverse effects identified.Under s. 56(1)(b) of the Yukon Environmental and Socio-economic Assessment Act, the Mayo Designated Office recommends to the Decision Body that the Project be allowed to proceed, subject to specified terms and conditions. The Designated Office determined that the Project is likely to have significant adverse environmental and socio-economic effects in or outside Yukon that can be mitigated by those terms and conditions.The Project is located approximately 50 km west of Carmacks.  The site is accessed via an existing road network from the Mt. Nansen Road that branches off from the Klondike Highway.

projectScope.summary: The Proponent, Tim Liverton, submitted a proposal for the construction and use of an access trail from an existing road to a Placer Prospecting lease (IL00056) for the summer of 2014. The Proponent will be conducting Class 1 exploration on the lease. Class 1 exploration activities do not require an assessment and are therefore not included in the scope of this assessment. The proposed access trail will be 500 m long and 2 m wide and will be cut by hand. The trail will be used to transport a Candig trailer-mounted backhoe and ATV to the lease. The trail will be used for less than 5 return trips. The proposal was submitted on June 4, 2014. The Designated Office determined that more information was required in order to consider the proposal adequate and commence the evaluation. An Information Request (YOR 2014-0088-005-1) was therefore posted on June 11, 2014. The Proponent responded on June 13, 2014 (YOR 2014-0088-006-1). The Designated Office has until June 20, 2014 to either determine that the proposal is adequate and commence the evaluation or to request more information. projectScope.summary: The Proponent, Viking Mining, is proposing a new placer mine operation within 39 claims (approx.) along both sides of the lower Sixty Mile River at its confluence with the Yukon River. Proposed access to the mine area is by river and the establishment of approximately 3km of new land access from the mouth of the Sixty Mile River to and within the claim area.The Designated Office was able to establish a Project Scope outlining the main Project components that will be considered in the assessment. The public was notified of the project and asked to provide views and information to the Designated Office, this period ended on May 14, 2014. The Designated Office reviewed submissions made by: Tr'ondëk Hwëch'in, Yukon Government, Department of Fisheries and Oceans, Sue Guimond and the Yukon Conservation Society.  Upon this review,  the Designated Office determined that there were a number of items involving the project that needed further clarification, more information or in some cases, the Proponent was asked to consider alternative ways of undertaking some aspects of the project than those proposed.  Changes to the project as a result, included the construction of new access on and off claim, significant upgrading of an existing trail into a road and now two river crossing points for winter access and mobilization of equipment. Given these changes, the Designated Office determined that this constituted a material change and warranted an additional seeking views and information period (ended July 2).  Through the additional seeking views and information period, submissions were received from: Tr'ondëk Hwëch'in, Yukon Government, Department of Environment. Additional information was also provided from Sue Guimond and Yukon's EMR Department following a land use application assessment. Based on the comments received and other relevant considerations, four valued environmental and socio-economic components were identified: Environmental Quality, Wildlife and Wildlife Habitat, Heritage Resources and Other Users. The Designated Office determined that the Project will result in significant adverse effects to these values and while some effects could be mitigated others could not. A key finding during the course of this assessment was that the proponent did not adequately take the proposed project activities and the sensitivities of the project location into consideration. The proposal that the Designated Office was presented with and the successive changes to the project in response to raised values that would be affected demonstrated to the Designated Office that there was a significant level of uncertainty with how the project was to be undertaken.The Decision Body(s), Government of Yukon, Department of Energy, Mines and Resources, will review the Recommendation and the accompanying reasons described in this Evaluation Report. The Decision Body will issue a Decision Document within 30 days, as prescribed under s. 2 of the Decision Body Time Periods and Consultation Regulations, that will either a) accept the recommendation, b) vary the recommendation, or c) reject the recommendation.Assessment OutcomeUnder s. 56(1)(c) of the Yukon Environmental and Socio-economic Assessment Act, the Dawson Designated Office recommends to the Decision Bodies that the Project not be allowed to proceed, as it determined that the Project will have significant adverse environmental and socio-economic effects in or outside Yukon that cannot be mitigated.The proposed project is located within North Yukon Assessment District within the traditional territory of the Tr'ondëk Hwëch'in.  It is approximately 90km from the nearest community of Dawson City. The nearby watercourse is the Sixty Mile River at the confluence with the Yukon River. More specifically, the claims are along the first 4km of the Sixty Mile River beginning at the confluence. This Project is adjacent to Tr'ondëk Hwëch'in settlement land (S-79A1) and overlaps with two land parcels; Lot 1000 Quad 115O/12YT and a trapping lease disposition (PID100023361).

projectScope.summary: Gimlex Enterprises Ltd. has proposed a Class 4 Placer Mining operation on 33 claims located on Lightning Creek and McNeil Gulch. This evaluation assesses effects of the proposed Project on environmental quality and heritage resources, identified as valued components of particular relevance through the assessment process. The Project was in the Seeking Views and Information period from December 30, 2014 to January 13, 2015. During this time, comments were submitted from various departments of Yukon Government, the Canadian Wildlife Service, Department of Fisheries and Oceans Canada, and from Nacho Nyäk Dun First Nation. Based on the views and information submitted, as well as information obtained by the Designated Office, the assessment focused on effects to aquatic resources, environmental quality wildlfie and heritage resources. Aquatic resources included consideration of adverse effects to the terrestrial and aquatic environment of the Project Area, including associated effects to fish, while effects to heritage resources were considered in terms of one area with elevated potential for such resources located on claims P 16800 and P 16801. The Designated Office determined that the proposed Project may result in significant adverse effects to environmental quality, wildlife and heritage resources. The Designated Office subsequently recommended terms and conditions that would reduce or eliminate the identified adverse effects, such that they would not be significant.The Project is located approximately 6 km east of Keno City centered along Lightning Creek and McNeil Gulch.

projectScope.activities: The subdivision will consist of four land parcels, ranging between 24 to 34 ha, and will be released under Yukon Government's Agriculture Planned Land Application Review program. Each parcel will be required to have a minimum of 53% of the land developed into croplands.Prior to title being granted for these parcels, land applicants will be required to have an approved farm development plan that specifies which farming activities will occur. For the purposes of this assessment, the Dawson Designated Office will consider the full suite of potential farm development and residential activities that could take place on these parcels. It is expected that some of the parcels would be occupied on a seasonal basis only as road access is not maintained in the winter. Site preparation activities (i.e. access road upgrade/construction) are anticipated to begin in spring 2016 and will take up to 1 year to complete. The use and occupation of the subdivision parcels is permanent. projectScope.summary: This Project is for the renewal of a permit to operate a facility used for storage of special waste. The intended timeline is for 20 years until 2032. The Proponent is KBL Environment. The special waste facility has a total storage capacity of 410 L - 205 L drum equivalents. 326 tonnes of special waste was transferred to the facility in 2013, while 548 tonnes of special waste was transferred in 2014, to date. AEM's Whitehorse Wildlife Inventory Report (1998) indicates there are Significant Wildlife Areas overlapping the Project, which are important for semi-aquatic mammals such as beavers, birds and amphibians. There are three groundwater monitoring wells on-site, in the NE and SE areas of the facility. The general trend for groundwater flow is to the SE towards Porter Creek, where groundwater is expected to discharge. Depth to groundwater is approximately 22 m.  Hydrocarbons, nitrate and ammonia were found in the monitoring wells, but all below standard thresholds. There were slight exceedences of Manganese found in groundwater. The Proponent indicated that higher Manganese levels are usual across Yukon, and not considered a result of the facility or the upgradient landfill. Potential sources of groundwater contamination include special waste stored at the facility and leachate originating from the up gradient landfill. There are 3 main pathways: 1) percolation of leachate; 2) transport of contaminates down gradient in the aquifer; and 3) transport of contaminates up gradient in groundwater.Located 5 km from downtown Whitehorse, the facility is located within the Whitehorse Landfill site. It is 500 m west of the Alaska Highway (Rabbits Foot Canyon) and 1 to 1.5 km from the Porter Creek subdivision. The Project overlaps Kwanlin Dun First Nation and Ta'an Kwach'an Council Traditional Territories. The nearest water bodies are Porter Creek - 130 m S of the site, McIntyre Creek - 700 m SW and Tavern Creek - 1.1 km W. McIntyre and Tavern Creek(s) are not in the same watershed, and do not receive drainage from the facility.

The basis of the information request is to obtain project specific information regarding mining progression, timing, reclamation efforts, etc. as well as seeking proponent consideration of project alternatives (i.e. alternative ways of undertaking the project). Under the Rules of Evaluations by Designated Office, the proponent has 28 days to provide this information or advise the Designated Office in writing when the information can be submitted. projectScope.activities: The proposed project includes the clearing of land and moving of earth for the purposes of constructing an access road approximately 1 500 feet long and 10 to 12 feet wide. The road will enable access to the Ice Wireless base station from the plateau of Haeckel Hill (i.e. end of the existing road). The Project, located on Haeckel Hill, is approximately 5 km from downtown Whitehorse. Construction is anticipated for one month in summer, beginning July 1 to July 30, 2015. The road will have a graveled surface with ditches.    Project activities will include:  Construction of a gravel surfaced road (~1 500 feet by 10 to 12 feet)- Use of fill (12 to 18 inches of pit-run gravel on base of road)- Use of heavy equipment for road construction and contouring- Management of waste including disposal of organic debris at the city's solid waste facility- Ongoing maintenance of road
projectScope.summary: The Proponent intends to construct an approximately 1 500 foot gravel road that will provide access from the top of Haeckel Hill to the Ice Wireless base station. The Project entails clearing and leveling of land. The base will be covered in gravel and drainage ditches will be built on either side to control water runoff. Maintenance will be undertaken as required over the indefinite lifespan of the Project. Located in alpine tundra, the Project is in an ecologically sensitive area. Haeckel Hill is also habitat for Collared Pika, a listed species under the Species at Risk Act.The Project starts at the Haeckel Hill Plateau and ends at the Ice Wireless base station. Haeckel Hill is approximately 5 km from downtown Whitehorse.

projectScope.activities: Project Scope: Government of Yukon, Community Services is proposing to upgrade the Watson Lake's municipal drinking water pumphouse and treatment facility in 2015.  Construction of a new building and replacement of associated equipment, pumps, and treatment system (chlorination and filtration) will occur at the corner of Hyland Avenue and 6th Street next to the existing building. The upgrade is required as the existing water treatment building is not large enough to house the new equipment, pumps and pipes are old and undersized, the water treatment process required upgrading, and to address hygiene issues at the old fill station (along the Robert Campbell Highway). Work is anticipated to begin April/May 2015, with an anticipated completion of September 2015. The new water treatment facility will be tested and operational by December 2015.Project Activities:•	Use of heavy equipment•	Clearing (approximately 500 m2) and ground excavation and grading•	Hauling of gravel for use for site preparation•	Construction of a building (approximately 400 m2)  •	Replacement of associated equipment, pumps, and treatment system (chlorination and filtration)•	Upgrading pipe connections at one of the water supply wells•	Operation and maintenance of the water treatment facility•	Construction of a new community water fill station next to the new building•	Demolition of the bulk fill-up station at the Robert Campbell Highway •	Storage and use of fuel and propane•	Storage of chlorine•	Extension of the fencing•	Waste DisposalThe seeking views and information period is now closed. The Designated Office is currently preparing the recommendations for this Project.

projectScope.activities: The proposed project is to construct three revetments/berms at Rock River (km 446.5) and Cornwall Creek (km 445) immediately upstream of their respective crossing locations on the Dempster Highway. The purpose of the construction is to reinforce the naturally eroding river banks to ensure the streams stay within their channels at the road crossings, and as such prevent erosion of the Dempster Highway. In addition, there are proposed long-term maintenance activities to the revetments. Construction is scheduled for summer/fall 2015 and will take approximately two weeks to complete. The quarry has been operating since 1994, and its Quarry Lease expired on November 30, 2014. 90 percent of the project area has been cleared and grubbed and is in operation. The proposal was submitted on January 13, 2015 and is currently being reviewed by the Whitehorse Designated Office to determine if the proposal is adequate to begin assessment. On January 23, 2015 the Designated Office submitted a request for additional information from the proponent regarding the Project. The proponent has responded to this request and The Designated Office is reviewing the information.The Project falls within the City of Whitehorse, and is accessed by McLean Lake Road, approximately 3km down McLean Lake Road from the Alaska Highway. projectScope.activities: The proposed project involves the harvest of approximately 18 100 m3 of fire-killed timber in a 238 ha portion of the Little Fox Lake timber harvest area, as well as access development for three operating units (Block A, B & C). The Project is part of the Little Fox Lake Fuel Wood Timber Harvest Plan (THP) and 2014 Little Fox Lake Fuel Wood THP II. The existing access will be upgraded and extended. The Project is intended to start in late summer of 2014, as soon as the permits will be in place. The Proponent supplied the requested information (YOR document 2015-0117-015-1), and the proposal was deemed Adequate on July 29, 2015.  The Project went into Seeking Views and Information with a comment submission deadline of Aug 12, 2015.      Comment submissions were received from the Government of Yukon (YG) and Environment Canada-Canadian Wildlife Service (CWS). projectScope.activities: The purpose of the Project is to remediate petroleum hydrocarbon (PHC) contaminated soils at the Klondike River Highway Maintenance Camp (KRHMC) by implementing a monitored natural attenuation program. Extensive soil remediation work at the site has already been undertaken; however this Project proposes to allow the remaining PHC in contaminated soils under buildings to naturally break down over time and to monitor this process and any movement of contaminants. The Project area is located along the Dempster Highway, at km 65.1, approximately 100 km northeast Dawson, and is adjacent to the North Klondike River. The Project is anticipated to start in the spring of 2015 and continue for up to a period of 12 years until the fall of 2027. projectScope.activities: The Proponent is proposing to prepare and sell two 6.2 ha lots by way of a development contract for agriculture lands, under the Agriculture Planned Land Application Review (APLAR) process. In addition, the Project includes farm development activities which will be completed by the successful applicants to the APLAR. Farm development activities must be in compliance with Plan Ibex, the Ibex Valley Development Area Regulation, and the 2006 Yukon Agriculture Policy and as such may include, for example, food and forage production, poultry and egg production, livestock range, greenhousing and the production of plants and trees (note that these are examples only - for the full list and requirements please see these documents).The Proponent anticipates commencing road construction and upgrades in the fall of 2015. The temporal scope for the assessment is seven years (2015 - 2022) during which preparation and development activities will be completed. The use of the site is infinite.The Project is located in the Ibex Valley immediately west of Takhini Crossing on the south side of the Alaska Highway (km 1451.4), falls within the Traditional Territories of Kwanlin Dün First Nation and Champagne and Aishihik First Nations, and is adjacent to (500 m) the Traditional Territory of Ta'an Kwäch'än Council.Project Activities Include:• Clearing and grubbing (min 3.3 ha per lot)• Use of heavy equipment• Farm development activities• Excavation• Installation of fencing• Waste management (during construction and farming)• Reconfiguration of local trail (approx. 300 m)• Relocation of one power pole in highway-right-of-way• Drilling of water wells• Water use (up to 100 m3 per day)• Road construction and upgrade:o 465 m x 8 m upgrade of existing accesso 87 m x 8m new access development (extension of existing)o land preparation including strippingo 1500 m3 of granular materialso Removal of one culvert, installation of two culverts

projectScope.summary: The proposed Project is the continued operation of a Class 4 placer mine on 69 claims and two prospecting leases (to be converted to approximately 22 placer claims) on Kate Creek, a tributary of Lesaux Creek.  The claims are located within Tr'ondëk Hwëch'in (TH) Traditional Territory. The winter access road overlaps TH, White River First Nation (WRFN), and Kluane First Nation Traditional Territories. Project activities are planned to occur between March 1st and October 31st annually for a period of ten years, beginning in 2016.      The proposal was submitted on February 25th, 2016 and was reviewed by the Haines Junction Designated Office to determine whether there was adequate information to commence the assessment. The Designated Office determined that additional information was required and issued an Information Request to the Proponent on March 4th. The Proponent provided a response on March 7th. However, the Designated Office determined that clarification and supplementary information was required, and a second Information Request was issued on March 14th. The Proponent supplied the requested information on March 15th, and the proposal was deemed adequate on March 17th.The Designated Office provided an opportunity for comment on the Project during the initial Seeking Views and Information (SV&I) period from March 17th to March 31st, 2016. The SV&I period was extended until April 14th, 2016 following a request for extension from WRFN.  Comments were submitted by the Department of Fisheries and Oceans (Canada), the Canadian Wildlife Service (Environment and Climate Change Canada), four departments of Government of Yukon, TH, and WRFN. Concerns raised in comment submissions were related to the potential for Project activities to adversely impact water and soil quality, wildlife and wildlife habitat, biodiversity, heritage resources, and First Nations cultural traditions and subsistence harvesting rights.The Designated Office identified four valued components in the assessment: Aquatic Resources, Environmental Quality, Wildlife and Wildlife Habitat, and Heritage Resources. It was determined that the Project will have significant adverse effects on all of the identified valued components. However, by implementing the recommended mitigation measures in the Evaluation Report, in addition to those that the Proponent has committed to and application of relevant legislative requirements, the Designated Office is satisfied that the adverse effects can be eliminated, reduced or controlled such that they are not significant. The rationale for this determination is presented in the Evaluation Report.  The Evaluation Report and Recommendation was issued on May 19th, 2016. The Designated Office recommended to the Decision Body (Government of Yukon) that the Project be allowed to proceed, subject to specified terms and conditions. The Decision Document was issued on June 28th, 2016. The Decision Body varied the Recommendation.The Project is located approximately 80 km northwest of Beaver Creek in the Moosehorn Range area.

projectScope.activities: The proposed project involves the construction, installation, operation and maintenance of an underground power line along Front Street (Lot 4 Block 310 Plan 91-55 LTO) in downtown Whitehorse. The new line is part of the voltage conversion from 12kV to 25kV, and will enhance the reliability of the electrical infrastructure to handle the load growth in the Whitehorse downtown area. Project activities are anticipated to start in fall 2014, as soon as the appropriate permits will be issued  and will take approximately 1 month to complete. Once constructed, the underground power line requires regular maintenance and has an indefinite lifespan. There will be no above-ground infrastructure.Project Activities Include:•	Use of heavy equipment;•	Clearing, brushing and trenching of a corridor, approximately 250 m in length;•	Installation of 3"x 4" conduits and cables for the entire length of the trench;•	Disposal of cleared debris and trees (up to 3 poplar trees to be removed);•	Reclamation of the area to City standards•	Ongoing maintenance of the power line.

Excavation will happen through existing asphalt walkways, gravel and grassed areas near the river bank. One section of the trolley tracks will need to be lifted. Once construction is done, the existing asphalt, gravel and grass areas will be restored to City standards. Project activities are anticipated to start in fall 2014 and will take approximately 1 month to complete.Downtown Whitehorse, from White Pass Building to Trolley Shed. Construction will be done along the walking trail/trolley rails close to the river front. The affected property is Lot 4 Block 310, Plan 91-95 LTO.

projectScope.summary: This Project is currently in Information Requested from Proponent.The Proponent, Government of Yukon, Highways and Public Works, has proposed the continued operation and management of the existing Eagle Plains Solid Waste Management Facility (SWMF). This facility serves the Eagle Plains Transportation Highway Maintenance Zone, the Eagle Plains grader station, and the Eagle Plains Lodge. The Proposal was submitted on November 5, 2014, and the Dawson Designated Office issued an Information Request on November 18, 2014. The Proponent provided a response to this Information Request on December 18, 2014, however the Dawson Designated Office determined that the response was insufficient, and a second Information Request was issued on December 30, 2014. The response to the second Information Request was received on January 28, 2015, and the Dawson Designated Office determined the Proposal to be adequate on February 4, 2015. Comments during Seeking Views and Information were received by the Government of Yukon and Environment Canada. Environment Yukon raised concerns related to wildlife, open burning of wastes, and recommended updates to the Spill Contingency Plan. Based on comments received, the Designated Office issued an Information Request on March 9, 2015. Following a request for additional time to respond, the Proponent submitted a response to the Information Request on April 7, 2015. The Designated Office determined that the Proponent had not sufficiently responded to the Information Request, in particular to questions regarding the proposed management plan for municipal solid waste (MSW) at the Eagle Plains SWDF. Through email and telephone correspondence with the Proponent, the Designated Office determined that the Proponent required more time to determine their proposed management plan for MSW at the facility, prior to responding to our Information Request. The Proponent has requested an extension of time to respond to the Information Request. The response must be submitted by February 4, 2016.The Eagle Plains SWMF is located on a cleared 15 hectare area, approximately 406 km north of Dawson City and 4 km south of Eagle Plains. It is located within the Yukon River watershed, the Vuntut Gwitchin First Nation Traditional Territory, and the Tetlit Gwich'in Council Secondary Use Area.

The project is part of the Little Fox Lake Timber Harvest Plan (Block C, G and H) . The project is intended to start in summer 2015 with building roads through Block C and roads to Block G. and to operate year round for a period of approximately 4 years. It is expected to be completed in September 2019. A camp will be set-up as part of this project, with up to 4 people, resulting in 700 person days by season. As a forest resource road the road will not be open to the public, and the road will be gated.The Project is in Adequacy Review until June 11, 2014. projectScope.activities: The Project is the establishment of an all season Forest Resource Road for the harvest of birch fuel wood. The Project is part of the Bonanza Creek II Timber Harvest Plan, a stand level plan that establishes landscape strategies, management priorities and guidelines for the harvesting of forest resources. Road construction is intended to begin in later summer 2014, with harvest commencing in 2015. There is an estimated 3-5 years of harvesting opportunity. The Project is located approximately 7km southwest of Dawson City limits by road.Project Activities• Use of heavy equipment for road construction and timber harvest• Upgrades to 1.4 km of existing access along Pure Gold Gulch• Construction of approximately 7.3 km of Forest Resource all season roads and spur roads• Transport and use of fill material for road construction as needed• Clearing of skid trails and landings: • Fuel storage: up to 500L diesel, up to 500L gas• Harvest and transportation of up to 10 300 m³ fuel wood• Road decommissioning and reclamation upon Project completion
projectScope.summary: Yukon Government - Forest Management Branch, has submitted a proposal to establish a Forest Resource Road to the Bonanza Creek II timber harvest area. The Bonanza Creek II area is part of the Draft Gold Fields Timber Harvest Plan, in the Dawson Forest Resource Management Plan area. Bonanza Creek II targets greenwood birch stands. As a Forest Resource Road, this access will not be open to the public, and use of the road will require either (a) a harvesting licence;(b) a forest resources permit;(c) a cutting permit; or (d) a forest resources road permitThe road will be gated. The actual construction of the road will be contracted out.The route crosses several heritage features including the Yukon Ditch on two occasions, and the Ridge Road Trail.This Project is currently in Extended Seeking Views and Information stage, which ends on July 11, 2014The proposed Forest Resource Road is accessed from the existing Bonanza Creek road. From Bonanza Creek, there is existing access up Pure Gold Gulch that will be upgraded. The proposed road will be begin off of the Pure Gold Gulch trail.

projectScope.activities: The Proponent, the Great Northern Ski Society, proposes the continued use of water (up to 1 500 m3/day) from three local creeks for the purpose of snowmaking and water storage at the Mount Sima ski facility. The Project entails the construction of new and use of existing water management features, including a weir, sand bag dam, intake ponds and water diversion ditch and culvert. A spill plan is in place. Project activities are anticipated to occur annually from October to mid-Feburary for 10 years. The Project site is located on the Traditional Territory of the Kwanlin Dün, the Ta'an Kwäch'än Council and the Carcross Tagish First Nations, and approximately 4.5 km from the nearby residential neighbourhood of Mount Sima subdivision, while the distance from downtown Whitehorse is approximately 15 km.Project Activities Include:General • Use of heavy equipment• Storage of fuel on-site (i.e. 11 250 L diesel, 800 L gasoline and 4600 L propane)Water Use• Extraction, daily use and storage of water for snowmaking (up to 1 500 m3/day )• Use and maintenance of existing water intake pond (capacity of ~543 m3), culvert and diversion• Pumping of water in existing piping system to the ski hill Earthworks• Installation and use of new sand bag dam to control elevation of intake pond and removal of dam upon completion of weir construction• Excavation of approximately 5 m3 of material for construction of weir• Construction, use and maintenance of weir measuring approximately 3.5 m long by 1.5 m wide by 1.5 m tall• Contouring of disturbed areas to allow natural re-vegetation after construction of weirMonitoring• Weekly inspection of water system (i.e. pump house and reservoir)• Weekly monitoring of water flow while snowmaking system is in operation
projectScope.summary: The Mt. Sima Snowmaking Type B Water Licence Application is the renewal of Type B Water Licence (RE10-065) for Friends of Mount Sima Society (FMSS). The renewal will allow continued water use for snowmaking and water storage at Mount Sima ski facility, including activities to support water management including use of an existing water diversion ditch and culvert in Sima 1 and construction, use and decommissioning of a sand bag dam to control the elevation of intake pond as well as the construction, use and maintenance of a weir.There is a regional land use plan in effect at the location of the Project. The WHDO determined that adequacy could not be completed prior to an information request. The information request primarily focused on hydrology as well as site layout.      The Project moves into preparing recommendations on July 29, 2014 for two weeks.The Project is located 11 km east of Whitehorse YT at the Mount Sima ski facility.  It is within the Yukon Watershed. Nearby watercourses to the proposed project include unnamed tributaries of McRae Creek, which drains into the Yukon River - two unnamed tribs are known locally as Sima 1 and Sima 2.

projectScope.activities: The project is a placer mining operation on 24 claims on Boutellier Creek, a tributary of Christmas Creek. The project is approximately 12 km southeast of Silver City, within the Kluane Wildlife Sanctuary. Activities are proposed to occur annually from April 1st - October 31st for a period of ten years beginning in 2015.Project Activities•	Use of heavy machinery for preparation of work area and mining activities•	Use of in-stream reservoirs, out-of-stream settling facilities, by-pass and diversion channels•	Sluicing: ~ 175 000 m3 material over a ten-year period•	Water use from Boutellier Creek: ~ 4 000 m3/day•	Hydraulic and mechanical stripping•	Overburden removal of up to 1 000 m3/year•	Fuel storage: less than 4 000 L diesel and gasoline•	Use of existing Old Alaska Highway for access•	Moving (re-constructing) 3 600 m of road•	Use of existing and new fords (nine total)•	Camp for up to five people•	Waste disposal at the Silver City and/or Haines Junction dump/transfer facilities•	Progressive and final reclamation
projectScope.summary: The proposed project is a Class 4 Placer Mining operation on 24 claims on Boutellier Creek, located in the Kluane Wildlife Sanctuary. The proposal was submitted on March 28, 2014, and was reviewed to determine whether there was adequate information to commence an assessment. Department of Fisheries and Oceans Canada (DFO) provided input regarding the adequacy of the DFO worksheets included in the Class 4 Water License Application. The adequacy review identified information gaps and/or inaccuracies in project information. Additional information was then requested from the Proponent in an information request issued April 8, 2014, which can be viewed on the Project Details page in YOR document 2014-0049-012-1. The Yukon Water Board (YWB) also provided a letter addressed to the Proponent regarding the adequacy of the Water License Application, which was uploaded to the YOR.      The Proponent responded on June 15, 2014 (YOR documents 2014-0049-014-1 to 024-1. However, the response was determined to be insufficient due to several clarifications and incomplete schematics and/or maps. A second information request was issued on June 25th containing four questions (YOR document 2014-0049-025-1).    The Proponent responded on Nov 18, 2014 (YOR document 2014-0049-027-1), and the proposal was deemed adequate on Nov 25, 2014.  The Project went into Seeking Views and Information, with a comment submission deadline of Dec 9, 2014.The project is located on 24 claims on Boutellier Creek, a tributary of Christmas Creek, which drains into Kluane Lake and is part of the White River watershed. The claims are accessed via the Old Alaska Highway southeast of Silver City, approximately 60 km south of Destruction Bay, within the Kluane Wildlife Sanctuary.

projectScope.activities: Project Scope:Government of Yukon, Community Services is proposing the continued operation and management of the existing Ross River Solid Waste Disposal Facility (SWDF). The SWDF is located approximately 3 km south of Ross River on the access road to the community from the Robert Campbell Highway. The continued operation and management of the facility will occur over a nine year period beginning January 1, 2015.Project Activities:•	Operation of an unattended Class 2 facility, open 24/7.•	Acceptance of waste including: municipal solid waste (MSW), construction and demolition (C&D) waste, scrap metal, white metal, clean wood and tree trimmings, tires and special waste including lead-acid batteries, glycol, mercury-containing equipment, aerosol spray cans, ozone-depleting substances (ODS), paint, propane cylinders, residue fuel tanks/drums, used oil and autobodies.•	Landfilling approximately 272 tonnes of MSW and 105 tonnes of C&D waste per year.•	Transferring recyclables to a Whitehorse recycler on an as-needed basis.•	Management of all accepted items as described in the solid waste management plan.•	Use of heavy equipment in the operation of the SWDF.•	Site operation and regular maintenance of the SWDF by a local contractor.•	Alternatives to current operations being considered include no longer accepting special waste and no longer landfilling MSW. MSW may likely be transferred to Faro SWDF sometime in 2015 if a memorandum of understanding can be established.The Designated Office is currently preparing recommendation.

The initial scope of the Project included the installation of an underground power cable, however after correspondence between the Proponent and Yukon Electrical Company Limited, the Proponent changed the proposed project activities. As a result of the change in scope, an additional 14-day Seeking Views and Information (SVI) period will be granted to allow the public and any interested parties time to review the changes and submit comments. The Project is open for public comments until July 4, 2014The proposed project is located approximately 12 km west of Tagish and 16 km east of Carcross on Tagish Road. The Project is located near the Leased Parcel 2013-0320 near Crag Lake.

The trail will connect Burns Road on the east side of the Alaska Highway to the existing paved trail departing from the northwest corner of the Whitehorse airport fence to the Black Street stairs and trail. Two of the claims and one of the proposed camps fall within the municipal limits of Dawson City. The Proponent has completed a City of Dawson Development Permit for the proposed undertakings. projectScope.activities: Yukon's Oil and Gas Branch (the Proponent) proposes to undertake the activity of 're-abandonment' of an existing abandoned well, known as Socony Mobil's well "B-62".  B-62 is located within the Traditional Territory of the Vuntut Gwitchin  and approximately 5km east of the Miner River. The purpose of the Project is to remediate the well and well site of contaminates and  ensure the well is securely abandoned. The proposed activities to undertake the Project (listed below) will occur during the 2014 summer season in two stages June and August. Project Activities•	Helicopter Access and equipment mobilization between B-62 and Dempster Highway (approx.. 10-48 round trips)•	Staging equipment and personnel (camp) at Dempster Highway (Eagle Plains Lodge or Northern Cross's Camp 204)•	Pressure testing and evaluation of B-62•	Removal of diesel currently stored in well B-62•	Abandonment of B-62 (e.g. installation of cement plugs, cut and cap wellhead)•	Soil sampling and removal of contaminated soils•	Use of water, water withdrawal from nearby surface water (tributary of Miner River)•	Transportation and disposal of wastes
projectScope.summary: Yukon Government's Oil and Gas Branch has submitted a proposal to "re-abandon' Well B-62, a currently abandoned oil and gas well that was drilled in 1965 by Socony Mobil. The condition of the well was inspected in July 2013 and Yukon's Chief Operations Officer under the Oil and Act, determined that the condition of the well posed a risk of release if not re-mediated.  Emergency measures were taken between July-August 2013 but it was determined that further action was required to remove contaminates from the site, including the removal of diesel that was stored downhole.  These activities are proposed for June and August of 2014.The Designated Office sought views and information for the proposed project From April 29 to May 13, 2014. Yukon Government (Environment and Community Services) were the only comments received.  Under s. 56(1)(a) of the Yukon Environmental and Socio-economic Assessment Act, the Dawson Designated Office recommends to the Decision Bodies for the Project that the Project be allowed to proceed, as it determined that the Project will not have significant adverse environmental or socio-economic effects in or outside Yukon.The Decision Body, Yukon Government, will review the Recommendation and the accompanying reasons described in this Evaluation Report. The Decision Body will issue a Decision Document within 30 days, as prescribed under s. 2 of the Decision Body Time Periods and Consultation Regulations, that will either a) accept the recommendation, b) vary the recommendation, or c) reject the recommendation.The proposed project is located within North Yukon Assessment District within the traditional territory of the Vuntut Gwitchin.  It is approximately 160km from the nearest community of Old Crow; as well as activities staged from the Dempster Highway within the vicinity of Eagle Plains Lodge. The nearby watercourse is a tributary of the Miner River which is within the headwaters of the Porcupine River watershed. This Project also falls within the boundaries Land Management Unit #11, Zone III of the North Yukon Regional Land Use Plan.

projectScope.activities: The proposed Project involves the establishment of a substation to handle the electrical load growth for Whitehorse. As part of this project, the voltage for the downtown system will be converted from 12 KV to 25 KV. The fully fenced site will be brushed and grubbed with vegetation completely removed and replaced with clean gravel. Located in Riverdale, the project site is adjacent to the Whitehorse Fish Hatchery at the end of Selkirk Street and just below the Whitehorse dam. Project activities are anticipated to start once land tenure can be secured for the Lot. The anticipated timeline of project activities is summer and fall of 2014. Project Activities Include:•	Clearing and brushing  (0.325 ha)•	Use of heavy equipment •	Grading and leveling with gravel infill (1-2 ft in depth across the 0.325 ha site)
projectScope.summary: This project is the establishment of a substation to handle the electrical load growth for Whitehorse. It is part of multi-year project to convert voltage of Whitehorse downtown system from 12KV to 25KV.The site will be brushed, grubbed and fully fenced. The vegetation at site will be cleared and replaced with clean gravel. The proponent specified the volume of fill required to be approximately 1-2 ft in depth across the 0.325 ha site. There will be two pad mounted transformers, and a transformer oil containment system will be used. There were challenges with land tenure associated with the proposed project location. The proponent spoke with KDFN and YG in order to ensure land tenure.  An information request related to land tenure was issued. The anticipated timeline of the project activities is summer and fall of 2014.      The Project is in Seeking Views and Information.The Project is located in Riverdale at the end of Selkirk Street, adjacent to the Fish Hatchery alongside the Yukon River just below the Whitehorse Dam.

Specifically, the Proponent is proposing to construct a diversion of Adams Creek along six claims, and add two new ford crossings to the established diversion. The Dawson Designated Office is currently preparing a recommendation.The Project is located within the Tr'ondëk Hwëch'in (TH) traditional territory, approximately 21 km south of Dawson City. projectScope.summary: Beron Placers Co. Ltd. (the Proponent) has submitted a proposal to establish and operate a class 4 placer mine along Bonanza Creek.  The Proponents claims are immediately adjacent to Parks Canada's Discovery Claim Reserve. While the there will not be any placer mining activity within the reserve area, activities related to the mining itself are proposed to occur within the Reserve. This proposal was submitted to the Dawson Designated Office on April 25, 2014; the Designated Office issued two information requests in an effort to obtain more details on project activities. Once the information was received the Designated Office was able to establish the Project Scope which summarizes the main project components.   Public input was sought by the Designated Office for a 14 day period ending June 3, 2014.   The Dawson City Designated Office has determined that the project would result in significant adverse effects specific to Heritage Resources, but that those effects could be mitigated. As a result the Designated Office made a recommendation as per section  56(1)(b) of the Act. The project be allowed to proceed subject to recommended terms and conditions.The Project is located within North Yukon Assessment District within the traditional territory of the Tr'ondëk Hwëch'in.  The nearest community is Dawson City which is approximately 16km by road north of the Project site.This Project is adjacent Lot 587 Group 1052, Parks Canada's Discovery Claim Historic Site.The nearby watercourse is Bonanza Creek approximately 500m from its confluence with Eldorado Creek. Little Skookum Gulch, a tributary of Bonanza Creek is also within the project area.

projectScope.activities: The purpose of this Project is to control erosion along McIntyre Creek through alteration of the stream bank. The Project entails the stabilization of a 130 m section of eroding stream bank on the south side of lower McIntyre Creek so the creek will not breach its banks and flow through reclaimed areas of the former Range Road landfill. The Proponent will undertake in-stream work to excavate the bank for installation of rip-rap. The Proponent intends to establish temporary access to the site via Range Road. Located within the municipality of Whitehorse, the Project is on the Traditional Territories of Ta'an Kwäch'än Council and Kwanlin Dün First Nation, and 0.7 km west of the Yukon River. The Project will commence early July 2015 for approximately one month. Reclamation and decommissioning will take an additional two weeks, and project monitoring/maintenance will occur over the following six years (until 2021).Project activities will include:- Use of heavy equipment (e.g. excavator, bulldozer) and associated fuels- Clearing and grubbing of vegetation (~1 249 m3)- Construction of a temporary access road (400 m x 5 m)- Use of fill (~160 m3)- Excavation of stream bank (~531 m3)- Re-contouring and shaping of the stream bank (130 m)- Installation of rip-rap armouring (~424 m3) along stream bank- Stockpiling of material (i.e. organic material, fill and riprap) on plateau at south end of site (~150 m from water)- Reclamation/re-vegetation of work area (including decommissioning of the road)- Management of waste
projectScope.summary: The proponent, Ta'an Kwa'chan Council, would like to re-enforce a 130 m section of eroding stream bank on the south side of McIntyre Creek by excavating and re-shaping the bank slope. This would involve armouring the bank with rip-rap to stabilize the stream bank, prevent further erosion from occurring within the project area, and provide lasting erosion control to prevent the creek from breaching its banks and flowing through reclaimed areas of the former Range Road landfill.The project is located at McIntyre Creek in Whitehorse, approximately 245 m east of Range road before the creek widens into the Yukon River.

projectScope.summary: 16406 Yukon Inc. has submitted a proposal to continue operating a Class 4 placer mine within 51 placer claims along Hobo Creek. The aim is to mine residual deposits, side pay, and further upstream beyond the current mining extent. Two camp facilities are proposed, consisting of one main camp and one satellite camp. The proponent indicates operations will be conducted from May 1st to October 1st annually for a 10 year period. The Proposal was submitted on August 29th, 2014. The Designated Office has issued an Information Request to the Proponent. The basis of the information request is to obtain project specific information regarding mine operation details, such as the use of the stream as a conduit, in-stream settling, new road development, possible air access, as well as specifics on the camp facilities proposed. The Proponent was also asked to demonstrate consideration for how project activities may affect three identified Wildlife Key Areas that the Project overlaps with. Under the Rules of Evaluations by Designated Office, the proponent has up to 180 days to provide information or advise the Designated Office in writing when the information can be submitted within 2 years.The Project is located within the North Yukon Assessment District within the traditional territory of the Nacho Nyäk Dun. The nearest community is Mayo, approximately 65 km southeast of the Project. The Project is located within the Klondike River watershed.

projectScope.summary: The purpose of the Project is the renewal of a Class 4 Mining Land Use permit and Water License on 6 placer claims along Kimberly Creek, tributary of Jarvis River, in the Alsek River watershed, approximately 46 km northwest of Haines Junction. The Haines Junction Designated Office provided an opportunity for comment on the Project during the Seeking Views and Information period from January 6th to January 20th, 2015. Comments were submitted by the Department of Fisheries and Oceans (DFO), Government of Yukon (YG), and Environment Canada's Canadian Wildlife Service (CWS).  The Designated Office identified three valued components in the assessment: environmental quality, wildlife and wildlife habitat and heritage resources. It was determined by the Designate Office that the Project will have significant adverse effects on all three valued components that can be mitigated and recommends that this Project proceed with terms and conditions.The Decision Bodies, YG and DFO, will review the Recommendation and the accompanying reasons described in the Evaluation Report. The Decision Bodies will issue a Decision Document within 30 days, as prescribed under s. 2 of the Decision Body Time Periods and Consultation Regulations, that will either a) accept the recommendation, b) vary the recommendation, or c) reject the recommendation.  The Decision Body issued a Decision Document on Mar 6, 2015, varying the Recommendation.The Project is located along Kimberly Creek, tributary of Jarvis River, in the Alsek River watershed. The Project is located in the Champagne and Aishihik First Nations Traditional Territory, and on Settlement Land parcel CAFN R-47A. The Project is located approximately 46 km northwest of Haines Junction.

projectScope.activities: The proposed project involves clearing of approximately 230 m2 of land for the purpose of constructing a boat launch, installing of floating docks and establishing a sailing camp on the east shore of Schwatka Lake. projectScope.activities: The proposed project is a helicopter-supported Class 3 quartz mineral exploration program on 250 claims of the Mint Property, approximately 55 km west of Beaver Creek in the St Elias Mountains.  The Project is located within Kluane and White River First Nations' Traditional Territories. Areas of the property are located within Kluane Wildlife Sanctuary and abut the borders of Kluane National Park and Reserve as well as several White River Interim Protected land parcels. Project activities are planned to occur between June 1st and October 30st annually for a period of five years, beginning in 2016.Project Activities:•	Use of heavy equipment•	Access:o	Helicopter supported camps and exploration (up to 8 return flights per day from camp to drill sites and weekly resupply trips from the White River Lodge airstrip and the camp)•	Clearings :o	Up to 20  (5 m x 5 m) clearings per claim for drill siteso	Up to 5 heli-pads (5 m x 5 m)•	Soil sampling•	Geophysical surveys and geological mapping•	Drilling: Reverse circulation, diamond, and/or rotary air blast drilling program (up to 200 holes each; total length of 20 000 m, 40 000 m and 20 000 m respectively) using up to 2 heli-portable drills on a 24hr/day, 7 day/week basis which can also be self-propelled•	Water use from Boulder Creek, McLellan Creek and/or Rabbit Creek: up to 80 m3/day for drilling and camp use•	Temporary/seasonal fly-in camp (up to 30-person)o	Pit privieso	Canvas tents for kitchen and housing and frame structures•	Waste management o	Burning using forced-air burner and disposal at approved landfill•	Fuel handling and storage: 8000 L diesel; 4000 L Jet fuel; 500 L gasoline; 2000 lbs propane; and oil•	Progressive annual reclamation and final decommissioning
projectScope.summary: The Proponent submitted a proposal for a Class 3 Mining Land Use Permit for Quartz Exploration on 250 claims of the Mint Property. The proposal was submitted on June 7, 2015 and was reviewed to determine whether there was adequate information to commence an assessment. The adequacy review identified information gaps in project information, and additional information was requested from the Proponent.  The Proponent supplied the requested information (YOR document 2016-01114-010-1), and the proposal was deemed Adequate on Jul 6, 2016.  The Project went into Seeking Views and Information, with a comment submission deadline of July 27, 2016.  Following a request from the White River First Nation, the deadline for comments was extended to July 29, 2016.      Comments were submitted by the Yukon Conservation Society (YCS), KFN, the local Outfitter, Government of Yukon (YG), Environment Canada and Climate Change Canada and WRFN. Within comment submissions, opposition for the project was expressed from YCS, KFN, the local Outfitter, and WRFN. Their concerns revolved around effects to wildlife, especially to the Chisana Caribou Herd and Dall sheep. Generally, other concerns that were raised spoke to effects to water quality, the environment, other wildlife including grizzly bear, migratory and protected birds, biodiversity, heritage resources, and the outfitting business. The Designated Office identified four valued components in the assessment: environmental quality, wildlife and wildlife habitat, other land users and heritage resources. It was determined by the Designate Office that the Project will have significant adverse effects on all four valued components. Significant adverse effects to environmental quality and heritage resources can be mitigated by implementing the recommended mitigation measures in the Evaluation Report, in addition to those that the Proponent has committed to and relevant legislative requirements; however, significant adverse effects to the remaining two valued components, wildlife and wildlife habitat and other land users, could not be adequately mitigated.  The Designated Office considered mitigations to the spatial and temporal scope of the Project to reduce and/or control effects of the Project. In regards to effects on wildlife, specifically sheep, as well as in regards to other land users, the Designated Office found that the available mitigation is either insufficient or would change the scope of the Project so dramatically that it no longer resembled the project proposal. Therefore, under s. 56(1)(c) of the Yukon Environmental and Socio-economic Assessment Act, the Haines Junction Designated Office recommended to the Decision Body that the Project not be allowed to proceed, as it determined that the Project is likely to have significant adverse environmental and socio-economic effects in or outside Yukon that cannot be mitigated.For further information, please refer to the Evaluation Report.The project is located approximately 55km west of Beaver Creek, partially within the Kluane Wildlife Sanctuary, and adjacent to Asi Keyi Natural Environment Park.  Lignite Creek, McLellan Creek, Rabbit Creek, and the White River flow through the project area.  The project is located in the Southwest Assessment District, within the traditional territories of the Kluane First Nation and White River First Nation.

The Project falls under the 2014 Little Fox Lake Fuelwood II Timber Harvest Plan, and includes the harvesting of approximately 8 674 m3 of fire-killed timber over three operating units totally 103.6 ha in size (BL-D 62.2 ha, BL-G 13.6 ha, BL-H 27.6 ha), as well as access development for the three blocks. projectScope.summary: This Project is the renewal of a permit for the operation of a solid waste disposal facility in Mount Lorne. The proposed activities include storage of municipal solid waste and hazardous (special) waste, as well as the storage of used appliances containing ozone depleting substances, and the reclamation of previous open-burning and landfilling trench.A groundwater review indicates an increasing trend in Chloride and Sulphates, which could be contamination from leachate originating within the facility. The Project requires a waste disposal permit from the Government of Yukon. The Designated Office issued an information request for clarification of project information on October 30, 2014.The Project is located within the Hamlet of Mount Lorne on the Carcross Road. It is appoximately 40 km from Whitehorse and 38 km from Carcross. The Project also occurs within the area of the Mount Lorne/Carcross Road Local Area Plan. The South Klondike Highway is 50 m W of the site and Robinson subdivision is 300 m SE with 4 residential lots within 1 km of the site. Located within the Yukon River Watershed, the Project's nearest water body is Cowly Lakes. There is some discrepancy over the actual location of the facility. The project proposal indicates that the facility is not on the reserve disposition (disposition 860044) set aside for it. Clarification of the reserve boundary is being sought prior to the Project moving forward. A 1.0 km radius of the Project includes multiple First Nation Settlement land blocks.The Project also overlaps the Carcross Caribou Herd Wildlife Key Area, which is located in the Southern Lakes Caribou Recovery Program area.
