The Proponent is proposing to construct a new access road from the Klondike Highway, crossing over the Mayo to Dawson Transmission Line, as well as through an existing gravel pit. The Designated Office is accepting comments on the Project until midnight November 28, 2014.After reviewing the Proponent's Supplementary Information submissions dated from January 14 to April 17, 2014, the Dawson Designated Office determined that additional information was required in order to consider the proposal adequate and commence the evaluation. An Information Request was issued on April 22, 2014. Under the Designated Office Rules, the Proponent had until October 20, 2014 to either submit the requested information, or to advise our office in writing when the requested information will be submitted.On receipt of further details regarding the Project from the Proponent, as well as correspondence with other land users, The Dawson Designated Office determined that the Project was adequate on September 30, 2014. The Project is currently in Seeking Views and Information. Comments must be received by October 14, 2014.      Based on comment submissions received during the Seeking Views and Information stage of the assessment, further clarification was required concerning the scope of the proposed Project. As such, the Dawson Designated Office has issued an Information Request dated October 20, 2014. AsThe proposed project is located in the North Yukon Assessment District, within the Tr'ondëk Hwëch'in Traditional Territory. The nearest community is Dawson City which is approximately 40 km northwest of the Project. The nearest watercourse is an unnamed tributary of the Klondike River which runs through the claim block. Leotta Creek and Leroy Creek lie adjacent to the project area (to the north and south, respectively).

projectScope.activities: The proposed project is a commercial wilderness tourism operation and associated activities within Kluane National Park and Reserve (KNP&R). The Project involves guided day-hikes, backpacking and snowshoeing trips. Approximately 16 trips per summer (14 day trips and 2 overnight), and 12 trips in winter (10 day trips and 2 overnight trips) are expected, from 2015 to 2020. Project Activities:•	Guided day hikes and overnight backpacking trips, and day or overnight snowshoeing trips, on established or existing routes in areas throughout the KNP&R for up to 8 clients. •	Waste management and drinking water treatment.
projectScope.summary: The proposed project is a commercial wilderness tourism operation and associated activities within Kluane National Park and Reserve (KNP&R). The proposal was reviewed to determine whether the information was adequate to commence an assessment, and the proposal was deemed Adequate on Jan 20, 2015.  The Haines Junction Designated Office provided an opportunity for comment on the Project during the Seeking Views and Information period from January 20th to February 3rd, 2015. Comments were submitted by the Kluane First Nation.  The Designated Office identified four valued components in the assessment: fish and wildlife, human and environmental health, visitor experience and heritage resources. Included in heritage resources was also a consideration of potential impacts to traditional harvesting activities. It was determined by the Designated Office that the Project will not result in significant adverse effects to the above-mentioned valued components upon implementation of commitments and mitigations proposed by the Proponent in addition to compliance with the relevant legislation.The Project is located on established or existing routes within KNP&R.  The nearest communities are Haines Junction, Destruction Bay and Burwash Landing.  The nearest watercourses and waterbodies to the Project are Slims River, Sheep Creek, Bullion Creek, Canada Creek, Alsek River, Kluane Lake, Kathleen Lake, Louise Lake, Donjek River, Copper Joe Creek, St Elias Lake, Mush Lake, and the Dezadeash River.  The Project is located within the Southwest Assessment District, within the traditional territories of the Champagne and Aishihik First Nations, Kluane First Nation, and the White River First Nation.

projectScope.summary: Mr. Donald Sandberg has submitted a proposal to establish and operate a Class 4 placer mine within 18 placer claims, a portion which Mr. Sandberg has mined in the past but not in the past 13 years. Infrastructure for the mine would need to be constructed such as a camp, access routes, water crossings, settling facilities, and a diversion of lower Bonanza Creek as it enters the Klondike River. The proposed operation will include mining along the valley bottom as well as the upper bench. The proposed mining season would be February to December over a ten year period. This proposal was submitted in December of 2012; the Designated Office determined that the Proposal submitted was not adequate and does not contain sufficient information to begin the evaluation. Two requests for information have been issued to the Proponent (Jan.10/13 and Jan.29/13). On July 29, 2013, the Proponent did contact the Designated Office indicating a response will come but additional time was needed. The Proponent has until December 23, 2014 to provide the supplementary information. Please note that project activities could change and the Designated Office's understanding of the project could change as a result of the information provided.The Project is located within North Yukon Assessment District within the traditional territory of the Tr'ondëk Hwëch'in.  The nearest community is Dawson City; the Project is located within the municipal boundaries for Dawson City. This Project is adjacent to or within the boundaries of three Tr'ondëk Hwëch'in settlement land parcels (S-211B/D, R-20A, C-76 FS-79A1). Physical works are proposed within R-20A and S-211B/D which is Tr'o-Ju-Wech'in a National Heritage Site.The Proponents claims overlap with Lot 1161 Quad 116B/3 City of Dawson (legal description) and a Yukon Government Reserve (Bridgehead PID 100032279)The nearby watercourse is Bonanza Creek at the confluence with the Klondike River. More specifically, the claims are along the first 1km of the Bonanza Creek beginning at the confluence.

projectScope.activities: The total area of the THP is 455 ha with approx. 15 000 m3 of fire-killed timber, the majority of this volume being blowdown. The Project also includes access development for four operating units (FLB-1, FLB-2, FLB-3, FLB-4). In addition, the Project covers the identification of potential future operating units; however, these contain a majority of blowdown, which can exhibit higher moisture and rot content than standing dead, and as such is less desirable for harvesting.The project area is located approximately 88 km north of Whitehorse/ 86 km south of Carmacks and will be accessed directly off the highway via a proposed new road on the east side of the North Klondike Highway. The Proponent intends to start access construction in the fall of 2015, with harvesting anticipated to commence as early as winter 2015/16 over a 5 year period. Depending on market conditions this period may be extended. Permits and licenses will be issued to individual small volume commercial operators.Post-harvest, the proponent may undertake silvicultural activities and establish a designated personal use fuelwood cutting area.Project Activities:• Harvesting and processing of approximately 4000 m3 of fire-killed timber• Construction of up to 10 km of access road (4 m wide), with an additional 5 km of skid trails• Use of heavy machinery for harvesting and road construction, including feller-buncher, skidder, skid steer, excavator, dozer, grader, logging trucks• Development of multiple landing areas (between 0.01 ha and 0.25 ha each; maximum area of 1 ha/OU)• Installation of a gate at entrance to the forestry road• Seasonal road maintenance• Fuel storage (up to 400 L of diesel and 400 L of gasoline)• Decommissioning of roads and landings and reclamation upon project completion• Silviculture activities, as needed• Establishment of personal use fuelwood cutting area

projectScope.activities: The principal activity is the drilling and installing of three monitoring wells to conduct a groundwater monitoring and sampling program at the Watson Lake Sewage Lagoons as required by the Town of Watson Lake's water license. Two wells are planned to be installed within the existing sewage lagoon area. The third well will be installed down-gradient (south) of the sewage lagoons approximately 350 m north of the Liard River. Work is scheduled to occur in August or September 2014 and drilling will be conducted over a two day period. Project activities include:•	Clearing of a 200 m x 2.5 m access trail by hand with minimal removal of trees•	Use of a truck-mounted air rotary drill rig (approximately 30 000 lbs.)•	Drilling of three monitoring wells
projectScope.summary: The Proponent, the Town of Watson Lake, submitted a proposal to install three groundwater monitoring wells, to conduct a groundwater monitoring and sampling program, at the Watson Lake Sewage Lagoons. Installation of two of the monitoring wells, to be installed within the existing sewage lagoon area, will be access using the existing access road to the lagoons. The third monitoring well, to be located approximately 350m north of the Liard River, will require a trail to be cleared (~2.5 m x 200 m)to accommodate access of the drill rig for the initial installation. Once complete, the trail will be allowed to naturally re-vegetate as the monitoring and sampling  of the well will be conducted on foot.      The proposal was submitted on June 3, 2014. Recommendations were issued August 1, 2014.The Project is located within the municipal boundaries of Watson Lake, at and near the existing sewage lagoons.  The Project is within the traditional territories of the Liard First Nation and the Ross River Dena. Waterbodies in the area includes the Liard River, approximately 350 m south of the third monitoring well.

projectScope.activities: The Project is the continued operation of six diesel generators at the Dawson City Diesel Plant for the purpose of backup power supply during power outages, shortages and generator maintenance. The expected service life of these generators is to 2024 and beyond. The use of the facility is expected to continue indefinitely, but the temporal scope of the assessment is for ten years.
projectScope.summary: Yukon Energy Corporation (YEC) is proposing to continue operating the generators at their Dawson City diesel plant to continue providing power for customers during times when hydro generation cannot meet the demand for electricity. One of the generators will be replaced with a unit from Faro, while the remaining five units are existing at the site.The need for thermal generation is related to:- the need to meet demand for electricity when hydro-electric facilities are taken offline for routine maintenance- the need to meet demand for electricity when hydro-electric facilities are taken offline due to an emergency- the need to meet demand for electricity when there is grid separation and electricity from hydro-electric facilities is not available- the need to exercise a particular diesel unit as part of routine maintenance- the need to meet demand for electricity when hydro-electric facilities are unable to meet the demand for energyThe Dawson City diesel plant has the ability to generate upwards of 35GWhr/year. However, the models provided in the proposal, indicate that even during a drought case, demand from the plant will not exceed 12.1 GWhr/year, which is 35% of the plant's capacity.    After reviewing the proposal, the Dawson Designated Office issued two separate requests for further information.  The Proponent provided the requested information on August 15 and 22, respectively.The Dawson Designated Office is currently seeking public comments on the Project. The deadline to submit comments is September 9, 2014.The Project is located in the North Yukon Assessment District, Tr'ondëk Hwëch'in Traditional Territory. More specifically, the Project is located in Dawson City, Yukon at the corner of Fifth Avenue and Front Street. The Yukon and Klondike Rivers are the nearest watercourses.

Project activities are proposed to occur between April 1 and December 31, for ten years beginning in 2014. Project Activities:• Use of heavy machinery • Water use: withdrawal of up to 32 735 m3/day • Stripping of overburden:  150 000 m3/year     (mechanical) • Material processed: 600 000 m3/year • Construction and use of out-of-stream reservoirs • Construction and use of out-of-stream settling facilities • Construction of 2 diversions (permanent) • Crossing a watercourse   - Use of existing ford location (1)   - Construction and use of new ford location (1) • Exploration activities (drilling)	• Access:   - Use of existing road   - Development of new road (up to 9km) • Handling and storage of fuel:   - Diesel 45 458 L   - Gas 615 L• Camp facilities  - Up to five people   - Trailer with water delivery and shallow well   - Use of outhouses (2)• Storage and disposal of wastes • Reclamation and decommissioning activities
projectScope.summary: TIC Explorations Ltd. (the Proponent) has submitted a proposal to establish and operate a Class 4 placer mine on Congdon Creek. The project title is: Placer Mining - Congdon Creek (the Project). The Project includes 71 contiguous claims. The proposed timeline for Project activities is for the duration of the applied Water License and Mining Land Use Permit, which is 10 years. The Designated Office established a Project Scope outlining the main Project components that will be considered in the assessment. The public was notified of the Project and was asked to provide views and information to the Designated Office during the Seeking Views and Information (SV&I) period. The SV&I period opened on December 19th, 2013, and closed on January 28th, 2014. Numerous extension requests were made during the SV&I period outlining that more time was required to provide comments and information such that the SV&I period was extended to the maximum allowable time, which is 35 days. The Designated Office reviewed submissions made to it by:  White River First Nation, Department of Fisheries and Oceans, Parks Canada, Yukon Conservation Society, Kluane First Nation, Dän Keyi Renewable Resource Council, Canadian Parks and Wilderness Society Yukon Chapter (CPAWS), Government of Yukon, Environment Canada and members of the public. Upon this review, on January 30, 2014 the Designated Office determined that there were a number of items involving the Project that needed further clarification and/or information. An Information Request was sent to the Proponent on January 28, 2014. The Proponent responded to the Information Request on March 30, 2014. Within the Proponent's response, an alternative access road was proposed. Upon review of the response, it was determined that further clarification and/or information was required regarding this alternate access road and another Information Request was sent April 30, 2014.   Based on the comment submissions and other relevant considerations, four valued environmental and socio-economic components were identified: environmental quality, wildlife and wildlife habitat, recreational interests and traditional and cultural interests. The Designated Office has determined that the proposed project will result in significant adverse effects to wildlife, recreational interests and traditional and cultural interests that cannot be mitigated. In particular, these effects relate to the long-term loss of critical habitat and a major wildlife movement corridor within the Congdon Creek drainage for grizzly bear and moose, as well as effects related to reduced populations for these species. Significant adverse effects were also identified for various land uses that are incompatible with mining operations. These uses include recreational land uses, as they relate to the ability of recreationists to enjoy the hiking and wildlife viewing opportunities in the area, and traditional and cultural land uses, as they relate to the ability of the local First Nation to continue subsistence hunting and cultural practices. This determination resulted in the Recommendation of the Designated Office on Sept 5, 2014, that the project not be allowed to proceed.The decision body accepted the recommendation of the Designated Office, that the project as proposed notproceed, in a Decision Document issued on Nov 5, 2014.The proposed Project is located within the Haines Junction (Southwest) Assessment District within the Traditional Territories of Kluane and White River First Nations. It is located approximately 17 km south from the nearest community of Destruction Bay. The nearest watercourse is Congdon Creek, which overlaps with the claims, and the second nearest waterbody is Kluane Lake located 700 m from the claims. The Project is located within the Kluane Wildlife Sanctuary and adjacent to the Kluane National Park and Reserve's northern boundary.

The Project is approximately 10 km west of Burwash Landing, and is situated within the Kluane Wildlife Sanctuary. Activities are proposed to occur seasonally for a period of five years, from May 15th to November 1st beginning in 2014.The proposal was reviewed to determine whether there was adequate information to commence an evaluation. Significant information gaps and/or inaccuracies were identified with respect to project activities as well as potential values that could be impacted. A request for additional information was issued to the Proponent on May 13, 2014, and can be viewed on the Project Details page as YOR document 2014-0069-008-1. The Proponent submitted their response to the Information Request, and after clarifying that no trees will be harvested and providing additional information regarding the site design, the Project was moved into the Seeking Views and Information period of the assessment. The Evaluation Report was completed on August 15th, 2014. A Decision Document was issued on September 16, 2014.The proposed project is located approximately 10 km from Burwash Landing. The project is located within the traditional territory of the Kluane First Nation (KFN) and White River First Nation, and is located entirely on KFN Category A Settlement Land (R-1A). The nearby watercourse is Bee Creek, a tributary of the Duke River.

projectScope.activities: Government of Yukon, Highways and Public Works, Transportation Engineering Branch is proposing geotechnical testing and development of an aggregate pit on a 5 ha parcel of land, located on the left hand side of the road, at km 237.5 of the North Canol Road, approximately 11 km northeast of Ross River. The purpose of the project is to develop a long-term source (25-50 years) of granular material for road reconstruction, watercourse crossings and annual road maintenance. Further, riprap from the quarry will be used for road works involving erosion control along the Ross River, erosion control and slope stabilization for the Deep Creek watercourse crossing, and for erosion control at the Ross River Ferry Crossing. Pit development is planned to begin August 2014. Initial site development will take approximately three to four weeks. Future geotechnical and pit development activities will occur between June and October, annually. The temporal scope for the assessment of the Project is 25 years.Proposed Activities:•	Use of heavy equipment•	Access construction •	Clearing, grubbing, stripping and site preparation •	Burning of brush and slash•	Quarry pit development •	Blasting, granular extraction (total of approximately 150 000 m3), and stockpiling on site•	Geotechnical investigations•	Establishment of a temporary camp at km 235.5 (8-12 people) to support blasting, sorting and stockpiling activities every 4-5 years for approx. 2 weeks•	Establishment of fuel storage and equipment maintenance/storage areas at camp•	Waste management and disposal•	Decommissioning and reclamation

Once the information was received, the Designated Office was able to develop a Project Scope summarizing the main project components. Public input was sought by the Designated Office until June 20, 2014.Recommendations were issued for this Project on July 10, 2014.Listed at the bottom of this summary page are the main documents describing the Project. Other documents that may be of interested are available on the Project Details screen including comments submitted in relation to this Project.Located within the Southeast Yukon Assessment District, the Project site is on the left hand side, at km 237.5, of the North Canol Road; the camp is located at km 235.5. The Project is within the traditional territories of the Ross River Dena and the Liard First Nation. projectScope.activities: The Project is a placer mining operation on 40 claims on lower Arch Creek and three small tributaries. The Project is approximately 40 km northwest of Burwash Landing, within the Kluane Wildlife Sanctuary. Activities are proposed to occur annually from March 1st - December 1st for a period of ten years beginning in 2015.Project Activities:•	Use of heavy machinery for preparation of work area and mining activities•	Use of in- and out-of-stream reservoirs and settling facilities, and by-pass and diversion channels•	Sluicing: up to 5 000 m3 material/year•	Water use from Arch Creek: 2 940 m3/day•	Stripping and overburden removal: 10 000 m3 in first year, and 5 000 m3 in subsequent years•	Drilling and use of explosives•	Fuel storage: diesel (in double-walled 4 600 L and 2 045 L tanks, and 400 L tidy tank), gasoline (in four 817 L steel drums) and propane (in 100-lb bottles)•	Use, and minor maintenance as required, of existing Donjek River access road and Quill Creek access road•	Development and use of new trails (~ 1 000 m)•	Use of existing and new fords (20 total)•	Use and construction of one log bridge (on Arch Creek)•	Camp for up to three people•	Waste management and disposal•	Progressive and final reclamation
projectScope.summary: Nelson Mines submitted a proposal for a Placer Mining project on 40 claims on lower Arch Creek. The proposal was submitted on Dec. 30, 2014 and was reviewed to determine whether there was adequate information to commence an assessment.  The proposal was deemed adequate on Jan 12, 2015.  The Project went into Seeking Views and Information, with a comment submission deadline of Jan 26, 2015.     The Recommendation was issued on Feb 12, 2015 for the project to proceed with mitigations, under 56.1 (b).  A Decision Document was issued on Mar 13, 2015 varying that Recommendation.The project is located approximately 40km northwest of Burwash Landing, within the Kluane Wildlife Sanctuary.  The claims are on lower Arch Creek, a tributary of the Donjek River, which is within the Yukon River watershed.  Access to the claims is via the Quill Creek gravel road off the Alaska Highway (at approx. km 1727.5), with winter access via the Donjek River wagon road.  The Project is located in the Southwest Assessment District, within the traditional territories of the Kluane First Nation and the White River First Nation.

projectScope.summary: The Proponent, Government of Yukon, Highways and Public Works, is proposing the installation of a clear span Oilfield Timber Deck bridge over Deep Creek. The crossing is currently culverted, but the culverts have blown out numerous times (the last time being 2013). This crossing is integral to the continued use of the North Canol Road past km 256. The proposed bridge will have an estimated lifespan of 50 years and will be designed to accommodate a 100-year flood. The Project has an estimated start date of July 2015 and completion by the end of October 2015.Located approximately km 256 of the North Canol Road (approximately 30 km north of Ross River).

projectScope.activities: The proposed project involves the continued operation and maintenance of the existing Solid Waste Management Facility at Deep Creek. The facility recently converted from burning of municipal solid waste (MSW) to a modified transfer station. The facility now primarily accepts MSW and construction and demolition waste (C&D), as well as hazardous wastes and recyclables. It serves residential and commercial users from Deep Creek, Horse Creek, Fox Lake and the Takhini Hot Springs Road. The Project is within the Kwanlin Dün First Nation and Ta'an Kwäch'än Council traditional territories. Located along the North Klondike Highway, the Project is approximately 40 km north of Whitehorse and 1.5 km from the community of Deep Creek. With a user delivery of approximately 2 310 tonnes of MSW and 395 tonnes of C&D over a ten year period, the proposed operation and management of the SWDF will occur beginning January 1, 2015.Project activities include:• Collection of wastes:o MSW in 13 x 16-cubic yard transfer bins o Recyclables in 8 or 30 cubic yard bins (for plastics, tin cans, beverage containers and mixed paper, as well as cardboard)o C&D wasteso Hazardous wastes, including used appliances containing ozone-depleting substances• Transfer of MSW and recyclables to City of Whitehorse Landfill and local recycling processor, respectively • Construction of C&D cells, up to 8 each with 5 years capacity (based on disposal rates and densities)• Landfilling of C&D wastes • Monitoring of groundwater and surface water • Installation of signage• Reclamation of closed areas of the landfill including submission of a closure plan to Yukon Government and placement of intermediate and final cover material over landfilled C&D waste• Scheduled maintenance of the SWDF• Stockpile snow in areas down gradient of landfilled waste
projectScope.summary: Government of Yukon established the Deep Creek Solid Waste Disposal Facility for residents of Deep Creek subdivision, Grizzly Valley subdivision, residents of the Ta'an Kwach'an First Nation, Horse Creek, Fox Lake and Takhini Hot Springs Road. The facility recently converted from burning of Municipal Solid Waste to a modified transfer station. There is some concern around contamination at this site. The waste collected at this facility have resulted in exceedences of parameters of concern, which could lead to contamination of groundwater and soil in the area. Potential sources of groundwater and soil contamination include special waste stored at the facility and leachate originating at the landfill. There seem to be two primary pathways of contamination: 1) percolation of leachate; and 2) transport of contaminates downstream in groundwater. This Project is a renewal for a previous Solid Waste Facility permit. The proposed activity is listed in Part 8, item 8 of the Activity Regulations. The Proponent is Government of Yukon - Community Services.Located along the North Klondike Highway, the Project is approximately 40 km north of Whitehorse and 1.5 km from the community of Deep Creek. The Project overlaps Kwanlin Dun First Nation and Ta'an Kwach'an Council Traditional Territories. It is within the Yukon River Drainage Area with Lake Laberge approximately 1.2 km to the east and Deep Creek approximately 0.5 km to the south.

projectScope.activities: The project activities include:•Use of an existing winter trail to McEvoy Lake and along the shoreline of the lake     o	crossing of two completely frozen creeks in 2017 (creeks •Diversion from the existing winter trail around a swampy area (     o	potentially need to remove a few small trees; no brushing•Diversion from McEvoy Lake to airstrip (approx. 3 km x up to 4 m)    o	some brushing and removal of small trees•Transport of a broken bulldozer and 5 000 gal fuel tank from the existing trail to the airstrip•Transport of a D7 bulldozer from the airstrip to the highway and potential transport of a small excavator from the highway to the airstripThe Designated Office issued its recommendation on December 15, 2015.
projectScope.summary: Inconnu Lodge Limited (the Proponent) is proposing to use an existing winter trail, approximately 35 km, from the Robert Campbell Highway (at approximately km 248) to the west side of McEvoy Lake in southeast Yukon. From McEvoy Lake, the existing route follows the lakeshore (on the lake side) for about 3 km. From this location, a 3 km diversion will be constructed/utilized from the lake to the Proponent's airstrip. In 2016, the Proponent intends to utilize the portion of the winter trail from the airstrip to the lake, along the lakeshore and continue for approximately 100 m west of the lake to recover and transport a broken-down bulldozer and a fuel tank back to the airstrip. In 2017, the Proponent plans to walk a D7 bulldozer out to the Robert Campbell Highway and possibly walk a small excavator back to the airstrip. The use of the winter trail is expected to occur over two years.The location of the  winter trail begins at approximately km 248 on the Robert Campbell Highway, approximately 160 km southeast of Ross River and continues to the airstrip at Inconnu Lodge near McEvoy Lake in southeast Yukon. The Project is located in the traditional territories of the Ross River Dena and the Liard First Nation.

projectScope.activities: The Project is the continued operation of the existing Village of Haines Junction Solid Waste Disposal Facility (SWDF). The SWDF is located on Lot #1083, 2 km north of the Village of Haines Junction on the Alaska Highway. The SWDF currently serves approximately 860 users from Haines Junction and the surrounding areas. The proposed operation and management of the SWDF will occur for a period of nine years, under the guidance of the Solid Waste Management Plan for the 2013-2023 planning period.Project Activities:•	User delivery and segregation of approximately 780 tonnes of Municipal Solid Waste (MSW) and 240 tonnes of construction & demolition waste (C&D) per year;•	User delivery and segregation of recyclables; •	Acceptance of waste such as: MSW; C&D; clean wood and tree trimmings; compost; tires; household hazardous waste including paints, batteries, contaminated soil/water/snow, used oil, oil drums and autobodies; white metal appliances (ODS and non-ODS); scrap metal; electronic waste; and animal carcasses;•	Operation of a transfer station and sorting table at facility entrance to separate recyclables and waste and an on-site Free Store Facility;•	On-site landfilling of:        -	MSW (in series of 1 to 1.5 year capacity cells);       -	C&D (in series of 5 year capacity cells;•	On-site Land Treatment Facility (operated and managed by a local contractor);•	Burning of brush and untreated wood, approximately twice per year;•	Periodic transfer of recyclables to appropriate permitted processor in Whitehorse; •	Use of heavy equipment in the operation of the SWDF; •	Regular maintenance of the SWDF; and•	Monitoring of groundwater at site via three groundwater wells and surface water sampling.
projectScope.summary: The Village of Haines Junction submitted a proposal for permit renewal for the continued operation and management of the Haines Junction Solid Waste Disposal Facility (SWDF).  The proposal was reviewed to determine whether the information was adequate to commence an assessment.  The adequacy review identified information gaps in project information, and additional information was requested from the Proponent.  The Proponent supplied the requested information (YOR document 2014-0139-008-1 and 0139-0013-1), and the proposal was deemed Adequate on February 11, 2015.  The Project went into Seeking Views and Information with a comment submission deadline of February 25, 2015.  Comments were received, a Recommendation was issued on Feb 26, 2015, and the Decision Document was issued on Mar 13, 2015.The Project is located at km 1578 of the Alaska Highway, within the boundaries of the Village of Haines Junction.  The nearest watercourses are Pine Creek and the Dezadeash River, located 2km  to the North, and 3km to the south of the Project, respectively.  An ephemeral creek (unnamed) runs 150m to the south and east of the Project. The nearest waterbody is Pine Lake, 3km to the North.   The Project is located in the Southwest Assessment District, within the traditional territory of the Champagne and Aishihik First Nation.

projectScope.activities: Project Scope:Government of Yukon, Community Services, Operations and Programs is proposing the continued operation and management of the existing Upper Liard Solid Waste Disposal Facility (SWDF). The facility is currently operating as a transfer station but has applied to amend the operations to a modified transfer station (allowing the landfilling of construction and demolition (C&D) waste). The SWDF is located approximately 2 km west of the community of Upper Liard and approximately 15 km west of the Town of Watson Lake. The proposed operation and management of the SWDF will occur over a 9-year period beginning January 2015.Project Activities:•	Operation of the unattended transfer station 24/7•	Acceptance of materials such as: municipal solid waste (MSW); C&D waste; recyclables; and household hazardous waste (only lead-acid batteries) •	Transferring an average of 1 840 kg of MSW and C&D waste each week to the Son of War Eagle Solid Waste Management Facility (SWMF)•	Landfilling of construction and demolition (C&D) waste•	On-site burning of brush and clean wood waste•	Capping of previous MSW and C&D landfill cells•	Transferring recyclables to a Whitehorse processor on an as-needed basis•	Management of waste that has been deposited and now stored on-site and that is continued to be deposited at the site that are not listed as accepted at the site (e.g. waste oils, fuel drums, fuel tanks, tires, white metals including those containing ozone-depleting substances (ODS), scrap metal, propane cylinders/tanks, derelict vehicles, furniture)•	Site operations and maintenance by operations supervisor•	Use of heavy equipment to carry out operations at the site•	Groundwater monitoring via groundwater wellsThe Designated Office is currently Preparing Recommendation.

Upper Liard is accessed via the Alaska Highway.      The Project is within the traditional territories of the Liard First Nation and the Ross River Dena.   Waterbodies in the area include the Albert Creek and the Liard River.

projectScope.summary: The Proponent is proposing to conduct class 4 placer mining activities on a total of 71 placer claims on Seymour Creek (53 claims) and Caribou Creek (18 claims). The Project is located approximately 47 km northwest of Carmacks Yukon and overlaps Little Salmon/Carmacks First Nation Category A Settlement Land (LSC R-8A). Project activities are scheduled to occur from April 20th to October 20th annually for the next ten years.The Project is for the operation of a large placer mine that includes the simultaneous operation of two sluice plants. The combined water use for these operations is up to 10 638 m3/day to process up to 120 000 m3/year of material. Daily water use is expected to be much lower than the maximum specified as the proponent is proposed to recycle process water. Permanent diversions are proposed for both Seymour and Caribou Creeks to accommodate mining across the width of the valleys. Project operations include progressive reclamation of disturbed areas.Views and information were solicited between December 16, 2014 and January 20, 2015. The extended Seeking Views and Information (SVI) Period was to accommodate an extension request from Selkirk First Nation (SFN). SFN noted that staffing shortages over the holiday period and a concern regarding cumulative effects necessitated an inspection of the Project area before comments could be submitted. Unfortunately, no further comments were received from SFN.Comments were received from the Department of Fisheries and Oceans Canada, Yukon Government and Environment Canada. From comments received and other relevant considerations, it was determined that the Project will likely result in significant adverse effects to aquatic resources, wildlife and wildlife habitat, environmental quality and heritage resources. The Mayo Designated Office determined that the significant adverse effects of the Project can be mitigation by the application of additional terms and conditions and that the Project should be allowed to proceed subject to those additional terms.Approximately 47 km northwest of Carmacks Yukon and overlaps Little Salmon/Carmacks First Nation Category A Settlement Land (LSC R-8A)

projectScope.summary: Michael Salvisberg-Longway submitted a spot land application proposal for a rural residential property at Summit Creek.  The proposal was reviewed to determine whether the information was adequate to commence an assessment.  The adequacy review identified information gaps in project information, and additional information was requested from the Proponent.  The planned information submission date was moved back twice at the request of the Proponent, with the Proponent supplying the requested information (YOR document 2014-0135-013-1 and 2014-0135-014-1) on April 9, 2015.  The Project went into Seeking Views and Information with a comment submission deadline of April 23, 2015.   The Seeking Views and Information period was extended until April 28, 2015.   Comments were received, a Recommendation was issued on May 29, 2015, and the Decision Document was issued on June 25, 2015.The Project is located at km 1578 of the Alaska Highway, approximately 3km northwest of the Nygren Subdivision.  The Village of Haines Junction lies 12km to the southeast. The nearest watercourses are Summit Creek, 100m to the west, and Bear Creek, 1.5km to the east.  The nearest waterbody is Pine Lake, 11km to the east.   The Project is located in the Southwest Assessment District, within the traditional territory of the Champagne and Aishihik First Nation.

projectScope.summary: Yukon Government Department of Highways and Public Works (the Proponent) has proposed the southward extension of Azure Road for 320 m with a cul-de-sac at the end of the road. The purpose of the Project is to provide a safe access to existing and future lots by closing off the existing southerly access point and creating a frontage road with a single access only. The existing multi-use trail will be maintained and realigned. The Project is located at km 1432.5 of the Alaska Hwy within the City of Whitehorse, in the Traditional Territories of Ta'an Kwäch'än Council and Kwanlin Dün First Nation. The Project is expected to take place early in the 2015 construction season and take 4-6 weeks to complete. Access to the lots will be maintained during construction, although there will be some access delays. No new utilities are proposed. No fuel will be stored on-site.Azure Road is one access point from the Alaska Hwy to the Crestview subdivision of the City of Whitehorse. It is located at km 1432.5 of the Alaska Hwy. The proposed extension would continue approximately 280m south, running parallel to the Alaska Hwy towards Laberge Rd, along an existing road.The Project occurs on the Traditional Territories of Ta'an Kwäch'än Council and Kwanlin Dün First Nation, but does not directly overlap with Settlement Lands.

projectScope.summary: Yukon Energy Corporation is proposing to construct 22 accesses from the Robert Campbell Highway and one access from Mitchell Road which connects Faro to the highway, to the transmission line that runs between Faro and Ross River. The accesses are required for brushing and maintenance of the power line and right-of-way. Four of these access routes will need to be newly constructed and 19 of them will involve modifications to existing trails/roads. Brushing is expected to begin in 2017 and construction of approaches off of the highway will take place over the 2018 and 2019 seasons (May through August, with clearing proposed to occur in 2017, March 27 - mid-May, if permitting in place). Access to the power line will be maintained indefinitely.

projectScope.activities: The lease area is approximately 9.94 ha, located on the Mount Sima Road, within the Whitehorse city limits. As the site is an existing quarry most of the land has already been prepared for the proposed activities. The Project involves the clearing of organics to access the aggregate, as well as the extraction, stockpiling, crushing and screening of aggregate. Approximately 373 500 m3 of granular resources (sand/gravel) has been determined to be available for extraction and approximately 2 500 m3 of organics primarily for reclamation purposes. The Project overlaps Traditional Territory for Ta'an Kwäch'än Council and Kwanlin Dün First Nation. Access to the site is via an existing road at km 2.9 of Mount Sima Road. No fuel will be stored on-site, and no water use is anticipated. The estimated start for the Project is spring 2015. Project activities will occur annually for 25 years from May through October.Project activities will include:• Clearing of organic material to access aggregate (surface)• Excavation and stockpiling of aggregate (~ 3 823 m3 to 7 646 m3 or 5 000 yd3 to 10 000 yd3 per year)• Screening and crushing of material (~ 4587 m3 to 7646 m3 or 6 000 yd3 to 10 000 yd3 per year)• Screening will occur approximately 20 days/year while crushing will be once or twice annually for 1 to 4 days• Use of heavy equipment and associated fuels• Maintenance/grading of existing site access• Progressive reclamation• Waste management (e.g. stockpiling organics for reclamation)
projectScope.summary: G. Peterson Contracting and Deadman Creek Enterprises have submitted their proposal to continue quarry operations on Government of Yukon lease area near Mt. Sima Road.  Much of this site is already cleared, but continuous stripping of the earth's surface will be required to expose aggregate below. Operations will also include the quarrying, screening, and crushing of aggregate material. Heavy machinery will be used, but no fuel will be stored on-site.The proposed quarry lease is approximately 9.65 km from downtown Whitehorse, near the Mt. Sima Road. The lease area is already an existing quarry that has been in operations for over a decade. Prior to this, the area was the location of the Whitehorse Copper Mine. The proposed project is within Ta'an Kwach'an and Kwanlin Dun traditional territories, but is not located on settlement land

projectScope.summary: This Project is currently in Seeking Views and Information. The proposed project is to infill an existing dredge pond near lots 96 and 1046, approximately 300 m south from the town-site of Dawson City. The purpose of the Project is to reduce the potential for the Klondike River to breach the existing berm. Project activities are proposed to occur from April to October annually for 10 years. The Proposal was submitted on August 15th, 2014, and the Dawson Designated Office issued an Information Request on August 25th, 2014. Under the Rules of Evaluations by Designated Office, the proponent has up to 180 days to provide information or advise the Designated Office in writing when the information can be submitted within 2 years. The Proponent provided a response to the Information Request on October 16, 2014. The Dawson Designated Office determined that supplemental information was required in order to proceed with the evaluation, and a second Information Request was issued to the Proponent on October 23, 2014. Following the Proponent's response to the previous Information Request, the Dawson Designated Office required additional information, and a third Information Request was issued on  December 24, 2014. The response to the third Information Request was received on January 27, 2015, and the Project was deemed Adequate on January 28, 2015. The Dawson Designated Office is now seeking comments until February 11, 2015.The Project is located within the North Yukon Assessment District within the Tr'ondëk Hwëch'in Traditional Territory. The nearest community is Dawson City, approximately 300 m south of the Project. The Project is located along the Klondike River, within the Klondike River watershed.

projectScope.activities: The proposed project consists in the cutting of wood for fuel abatement purposes on Block 3 in the community of Haines Junction. Wood harvesting and road development and decommissioning is planned to occur over two months between October and March and to begin in the winter season following issuance of permits and contracts (most likely by the 2016 season). If required, in-fill planting will occur during summer months. The proposed project is anticipated to take up to 5 years to complete. Project Activities:•	Winter harvesting of approximately 410 m3 of dead spruce trees and 380 m3 of green spruce trees•	Use of an existing gated access road (450 m x 5 m) though Parks Canada property (permission for access has been confirmed) •	Development of winter roads (800 m x 4 m; 0.32 ha total) and skid trails within the block •	Development of up to 3 landings (30 m x 30 m each; 0.24 ha total) within the block•	Use of heavy equipment for access development and timber harvesting•	Transportation of logs on and off public roads•	Fuel storage (less than 500 L of diesel and less than 500 L of gasoline) •	Brush and debris disposal by slash pile burning and/or prescribed burn•	Potential in-fill planting of aspen as per post-harvest regeneration surveys •	Reclamation of roads and silviculture activities (i.e. skid trails and landings)
projectScope.summary: The proposed project consists in the cutting of wood for fuel abatement purposes on Block 3 in the community of Haines Junction. The area was selected for fuel abatement as per the guiding principles of the Strategic Forest Management Plan (SFMP) and Integrated Landscape Plan (ILP) for the Champagne and Aishihik Traditional Territory, and the Haines Junction Community Fuel Abatement Plan. Wood harvesting and road development and decommissioning is planned to occur over two months between October and March and to begin in the winter season following issuance of permits and contracts (most likely by the 2016 season). If required, in-fill planting will occur during summer months. The proposed project is anticipated to take up to 5 years to complete. During the Seeking Views and Information period, from September 23rd to October 7th, 2015, the Designated Office received comments from the Government of Yukon (YG), Champagne and Aishihik First Nations (CAFN), the  St. Elias Cross-Country Ski Club, and a member of the public. YG had two departments submit comments; no concerns were expressed by the departments. CAFN and the member of the public expressed concern for heritage resources and recommended mitigations. And finally, the Ski Club identified concerns for land use conflicts with ski trails and recreationists and also provided measures to reduce conflict. The Haines Junction Designated Office identified four valued components wherein adverse effects were expected: Environmental Quality, Wildlife and Wildlife Habitat, Heritage Resources and Other Land Users and Interests. The Designated Office determined that significant adverse effects will occur from proposed project activities to two of the identified valued components: Environmental Quality and Other Land Users and Interests. However, by implementing the two recommended mitigation measures in the Evaluation Report, in addition to those that the Proponent has committed to and relevant legislative requirements, the Designated Office is satisfied that the adverse effects can be reduced, controlled or eliminated such that they are not significant. The rationale for this determination is presented in the Evaluation Report.Block 3 is located off the Alaska Highway, at approximately km 1583, northeast of the old Parks Canada Headquarters (Park Farm) and within the Village of Haines Junction's westerly boundary.  The nearest watercourse is Pine Creek 150m to the East.  The Project is located within the Southwest Assessment District, within the traditional territory of the Champagne and Aishihik First Nation.

projectScope.activities: The proposed project consists of the clearing and moving of earth for the construction of a new airstrip near the community of Canyon. The new airstrip will replace the current Canyon Creek Airstrip which is also now being used as an access to private property (Lots 1054-1 to -4). Currently, the average number of flights per year at the Canyon Creek Airstrip is 60; future average number of flights per year are expected to remain the same but exact numbers may vary year to year. Canyon is located approximately 30 km east of Haines Junction along the Alaska Highway. The Project is within Champagne and Aishihik First Nations Traditional Territory. Clearing, piling and burning will be completed this winter with the rest of the development completed in the summer of 2016. Use of the airstrip will be for up to 3 years.Project Activities•	Use of heavy equipment•	Clearing, levelling and contouring the land for the construction of the airstrip (980m x 30 m)•	Burning of wood and debris•	Decommissioning
projectScope.summary: The Proponent, Daniel Clunies-Ross, submitted an application for the clearing and moving of earth for the construction of a new airstrip near the community of Canyon. The proposal was reviewed to determine whether the information was adequate to commence an assessment.  The adequacy review identified information gaps in project information, and additional information was requested from the Proponent.  The Proponent supplied the requested information (YOR document 2015-0169-008-1), and the proposal was deemed Adequate on Nov 3, 2015.  The Project went into Seeking Views and Information with a comment submission deadline of Nov 17, 2015.The Project is located approximately 31 km east of Haines Junction, by Canyon Creek at km 1545.7 of the Alaska Highway.  The nearest waterbodies are the Dezadeash River, 600m to the south, and Aishihik River, 1.5km west. The Project is located in the Southwest Assessment District, within the traditional territory of the Champagne and Aishihik First Nation.

Conventional gravity separation placer mining technology will be employed to extract gold from placer gravels.Project Activities:Activities Proposed on Granite Creek:• Road upgrading (2 000 m x 4 m)• Development of new road (3 200 m x 4 m)• Mechanical stripping (up to 500 000 m3/year)• Exploration drilling• Development of out of stream reservoirs• Development of in-stream dug outs• Development of out of stream settling facilities• Material processing (up to 250 000 m3/year)• Development of temporary and permanent diversion channels (up to 2 600 m total)• Development of camp (up to 12 people)• Water use (up to 7 502 m3/day)• Construction of four new ford crossings• Fuel storage (41 733 L diesel; 4 546 L gasoline and 455 L propane)• Progressive and final reclamationActivities Proposed on IM00279:• Development of new roads (12.5 km x 4 m)• Development of two new ford crossings• Mechanical stripping (up to 500 000 m3/year)• Exploration drilling• Development of out of stream reservoirs• Development of in-stream dug outs• Development of out of stream settling facilities• Material processing (up to 250 000 m3/year)• Development of permanent diversions (up to 2 000 m)• Development of camp (up to 12 people)• Water use (up to 6502 m3/day)• Fuel storage (41 733 L diesel; 4 546 L gasoline and 455 L propane)
projectScope.summary: Joe McIntyre is proposing a class 4 placer mine on 17 claims and one placer lease centered along Granite Creek and one unnamed tributary, located approximately 14 km southeast of Keno City. Mining of the two areas will occur concurrently and will be completed by one crew and shared equipment. Duplicate activities for the two Projects reflect the maximum level of activity occurring at one time on either property (e.g. the camp and fuel storage amounts in either proposal reflect the maximum combined amount). Project operations are scheduled to occur between May 1st and October 31st annually for the next ten years. Conventional gravity separation placer mining technology will be employed to extract gold from placer gravels.Mining will be guided by exploration and will utilize conventional gravity separation using a wash plant. Waste water will be treated using settling ponds prior to release back to Granite Creek. Progressive and final reclamation will include spreading of organic matter and fine texture soils on disturbed areas to promote natural re-vegetation.Public comments were sought for two weeks beginning June 1, 2016. Comments were received from the Department of Fisheries and Oceans Canada, the Canadian Wildlife Service, the First Nation of Na-Cho Nyäk Dun and Yukon Government. Based on comments received and other relevant information, aquatic resources, wildlife, environmental quality and heritage resources were four values identified as potentially affected by the Project. Additional mitigation measures are recommended to reduce, eliminate or control significant adverse effects identified.The Project is centered along Granite Creek and one unnamed tributary, located approximately 14 km southeast of Keno City.

The lot enlargement area is located along the west and north sides of the existing lot, adjacent to Teslin Lake. projectScope.summary: Earth and Iron Inc. is proposing amendments to an existing Class 4 placer mine (PM16-015) located along upper Duncan Creek. Amendments include the addition of 105 placer claims, Prospecting Lease IM00325 and increases to mining activities previously licensed on the existing claims. The Proponent will employ conventional placer mining technology to extract gold from placer gravels on site. Project activities are proposed between May 1st and October 15th annually for the next ten years.Public comments were sought for two weeks beginning January 23, 2017. During this period, comments were received from the Department of Fisheries and Oceans Canada, Yukon Government and the Canadian Wildlife Service. From comments received and other relevant information aquatic resources, birds, environmental quality, moose and heritage resources were identified as valued environmental components that are likely to be adversely affected by the Project.Significant adverse effects to the valued components identified can be mitigated through the application of additional mitigative measures provided in this report. The Project proposal is thorough and comprehensive with a number of key mitigative measures contained within. Mitigative measures contained in the Proposal and highlighted in Appendix A, are critical to reducing potential significant adverse effects of the Project.The Project is located along upper Duncan Creek. The Project site is accessed via the existing Duncan Creek Road.

projectScope.activities: The proposed project involves the continued operation and management of the existing Destruction Bay Solid Waste Disposal Facility (SWDF). The SWDF is an unattended modified transfer facility serving approximately 150 users from Destruction Bay and Burwash Landing. The continued operation and management of the SWDF will occur for a period of ten years, under the guidance of the Solid Waste Management Plan for the 2013-2023 planning period.Project Activities:•	Acceptance of waste materials, including municipal solid waste, construction and demolition waste, scrap metal, white metal, clean wood and tree trimmings, tires, reusable goods, and recyclables and hazardous waste•	Weekly transfer of approximately 1 155 kg of municipal solid waste to the Son of War Eagle SWDF in Whitehorse.•	Transfer of recyclables to recycling processor in Whitehorse•	Groundwater monitoring•	Site operations and maintenance by a local contractor
projectScope.summary: Government of Yukon - Department of Community Services submitted a proposal for permit renewal for the continued operation and management of the Destruction Bay Solid Waste Disposal Facility (SWDF).  The proposal was reviewed to determine whether the information was adequate to commence an assessment.  The adequacy review identified information gaps in project information, and additional information was requested from the Proponent.  The Proponent supplied the requested information (YOR document 2014-0165-015-1), and the proposal was deemed Adequate on Nov 18, 2014.  The Project went to Seeking Views and Information with a comment submission deadline of Dec 2, 2014.  The deadline was extended to Dec 9, 2014 upon request from Kluane First Nation.  Comments were received, a Recommendation issued on Dec 30, 2014, and a Decision Document issued on Mar 5, 2015.The Project is located at km 1689.9 of the Alaska Highway.  The nearest community is Destruction Bay, situated 5 km to the south of the Project.  The nearest watercourse is Lewis Creek 400m to the North, and the nearest waterbody is Kluane Lake.   The Project is located within the Southwest Assessment District, within the traditional territories of the Kluane First Nation and White River First Nation.

projectScope.activities: The proposed project is for the winter and dry-season harvesting of timber and access development for two operating units (ME-01 and ME-02) within the Mackintosh East Timber Harvest Plan. ME-01 and ME-02 are located approximately 8 km north-northeast of Haines Junction. The Project Area will be accessed from the Pine Lake side of the Old Mackintosh Road, with additional access development. Activities are proposed to begin in August 2015, with harvesting until 2025 and an additional five years of post-harvest silvicultural activities.Project Activities:•	Winter or dry-season harvest of approximately 12 000 m3 of dead spruce trees and 4 200 m3 of green trees•	Development of approximately 7.4 km of temporary Forest Resources Roads and spur roads •	Use of and upgrades to approximately 1.1 km of the Old Mackintosh Road from the Pine Lake Rd. access•	Development of 14 landings (0.25 ha each)•	Use of heavy equipment for access development and timber harvesting•	Fuel storage (less than 500 L of diesel and less than 500 L of gasoline)•	Installation of bridge or culvert across unnamed tributary to Pine Creek•	Brush and debris disposal by lop and scatter, or slash pile burning•	Decommissioning of Forest Resources roads•	Silvicultural activities
projectScope.summary: YG Forest Management Branch submitted a proposal for the winter or dry-season harvesting of timber and access development for and within two operating units (ME-01 and ME-02) of the Mackintosh East Timber Harvest Plan.  The proposal was reviewed to determine whether the information was adequate to commence an assessment.  The adequacy review identified information gaps in project information, and additional information was requested from the Proponent.  The Proponent supplied the requested information (YOR document 2015-0065-020-1), and the proposal was deemed Adequate on April 13, 2015.  The Project went into Seeking Views and Information with a comment submission deadline of April 27, 2015.      The Evaluation Report and Recommendation was issued on July 24, 2015. The Haines Junction Designated Office recommended to the Decision Body (Government of Yukon - Forest Management Branch) that the Project be allowed to proceed, subject to specified terms and conditions. The Designated Office determined that the Project is likely to have significant adverse environmental and socio-economic effects in or outside Yukon that can be mitigated by those terms and conditions.      The Decision Document was issued on August 21, 2015. The Decision Body, Forest Management Branch, varied the recommendation.The Project is located approximately 2.5km east of the Nygren Subdivision (Bear Creek).  The Village of Haines Junction lies 8km to the southeast. The nearest watercourses are an unnamed tributary of Pine Creek (adjacent), Bear Creek (1.5km west), and Marl Creek (2km north).  The nearest waterbody is Pine Lake, 6km southeast.   The Project is located in the Southwest Assessment District, within the traditional territory of the Champagne and Aishihik First Nation.

The Project includes site preparation activities undertaken by the Proponent such as clearing of land for access construction. Successful applicants of APLAR will undertake farm development activities, including clearing of land for cropping and infrastructure development. Farm development activities must be in compliance with the Ibex Valley Development Area Regulations and the 2006 Yukon Agriculture Policy. The project timeline is seven years, during which preparation and development activities will be completed. The Proponent anticipates commencing road construction in the fall of 2016. The use of the site(s) is infinite.Located within the Hamlet of Ibex Valley, the Project is immediately west of Takhini Crossing at km 1464 of the Alaska Highway. It falls within the Traditional Territories of the Kwanlin Dün First Nation, Ta'an Kwäch'än Council, and Champagne and Aishihik First Nations.

The purpose of the Project is to improve creek flow through the highway culverts and provide a granular material source for future road maintenance. Project activities are proposed to occur during 10 removal events over the next 25 years. Each removal event (approximately every 2-3 years) will occur in summer or early fall, during low-flow conditions, and will take up to two weeks to complete.The Dawson City Designated Office is currently preparing its recommendations in relation to this Project.The Project is located approximately 64 km north of Eagle Plains, at km 433 of the Dempster Highway. The Project is located within the Traditional Territories of the Vuntut Gwitchin and Na-Cho Nyäk Dun First Nations, and also within the Tetlit Gwich'in Council Secondary Use Area.

projectScope.activities: The proposed project is for the development, operation and reclamation of a gravel quarry on a 39.5 ha parcel of land located north of the existing Castle Rock quarry lease along the Haeckel Ski Road, approximately 13 km northwest of downtown Whitehorse, within the municipal boundaries. The majority of the material excavated will be transported directly to development sites as needed, while a small portion of the material excavated will be used in the Castle Rock Quarry Lease for asphalt production.The Project is located within the Whitehorse city limits, approximately 3.5 km northwest of Crestview, and 13 km from downtown. projectScope.summary: Mel Zeiler has proposed a Class 4 Placer Mining operation on 32 claims located on Parent Creek, with the proposed Mine Plan occurring on the first seven claims. This evaluation assesses effects of the proposed Project on aquatic resources, environmental quality, wildlife and wildlife habitat, and heritage resources, identified as valued components of particular relevance through the assessment process. The Project was in the Seeking Views and Information period from February 25 to March 12, 2015. During this time, comments were submitted from the Canadian Wildlife Service, Department of Fisheries and Oceans Canada, and from Nacho Nyäk Dun First Nation. Based on the views and information submitted, as well as information obtained by the Designated Office, the assessment focused on effects to environmental quality, aquatic resources, wildlife and heritage resources. The Designated Office determined that the proposed Project may result in significant adverse effects to all valued components, in particular due to: sedimentation and erosion; introduction of invasive species; impacts to migratory birds and conflicts with bears; and damage or destruction of heritage resources. The Designated Office subsequently recommended terms and conditions that would reduce or eliminate the identified adverse effects, such that they would not be significant.The Project is located approximately 35km north east of Mayo. The Project is accessed from the Duncan Creek Road.

projectScope.activities: The proposed project involves the reclamation and monitoring of the Horsecamp Hill Solid Waste Disposal Facility (SWDF). The SWDF is located at km 1890 of the Alaska Highway, 40 km south of Beaver Creek. The facility, which has been closed since October 15, 2011, was an unsupervised facility that was open 24/7, year-round. The proposed closure activities will be undertaken during the summer of 2015, and are anticipated to be completed by September 2015. The temporal scope for ongoing groundwater monitoring activities is 9 years, from January 1, 2015 to December 31, 2023.Project Activities:•	Use of heavy equipment for decommissioning access to the SWDF.•	Grading and ditching of the site to prevent ponding.•	Application of final cover material (organics) over site.•	Performing minor repairs and maintenance to the final cover or revegetation as required.•	Remove structures from the SWDF, including fencing and water storage containers.•	Ongoing groundwater and surface water monitoring.•	Inspections and monitoring of the facility for settling and erosion.
projectScope.summary: Government of Yukon - Department of Community Services submitted a proposal for the reclamation and monitoring of the Horsecamp Hill Solid Waste Disposal Facility (SWDF).  The proposal was reviewed to determine whether the information was adequate to commence an assessment.  The adequacy review identified information gaps in project information, and additional information was requested from the Proponent.  The Proponent supplied the requested information (YOR document 2014-0177-015-1), and the proposal was deemed Adequate on Dec 9, 2014.  The Project went into Seeking Views and Information with a comment submission deadline of Dec 23, 2014.   Comments were received, a Recommendation issued on Jan 13, 2015, and a Decision Document issued on Feb 5, 2015.The Project is located at km 1890 of the Alaska Highway.  The nearest community is Beaver Creek, situated 40 km to the north of the Project.  The nearest watercourses are Sanpete Creek and White River.   The Project is located within the Southwest Assessment District, within the traditional territories of the Kluane First Nation and White River First Nation.

projectScope.summary: This Project is currently in Information Requested from the Proponent. The Proponent is proposing to continue placer mining on 729 claims located on the Sixtymile River and its tributaries - California Creek, Boucher Creek, Fish Creek, and Fifty Mile Creek.      On November 29, 2015, the Designated Office issued an Information Request to the Proponent, requesting further information on the proposed Project. The Proponent submitted supplemental information on November 4, 2015, and on November 13, 2015,the Proposal was deemed Adequate from the Designated Office, and the evaluation commenced. The Designated Office sought views and information on the Project from November 13, 2015 to November 27, 2017. Based on comments received, the Designated Office determined that supplemental information was required in order to proceed with the evaluation of the Project. As such, and Information Request was issued on December 3, 2015. The Designated Office has requested further clarification on the proposed mine plan, specifically, as it pertains to the Sixty Mile River. Under Designated Office rules, the Proponent has 28 days or up to 1 year after the evaluation commenced to provide the information.The Project is located within the Tr'ondëk Hwëch'in Traditional Territory and is situated approximately 40 km by Top of the World Highway west of Dawson City.

projectScope.activities: The Project is the ongoing operation of a small-scale Arctic char aquaculture facility for non-commercial, private use. The Proponent is using a pond system that is part of a spring fed natural creek, to maintain an adult brood stock of Arctic char. This pond system is part of an existing facility, established in 1991. Currently, the facility contains approximately 500 fish, and its operation involves the addition of fish feed and release of fish excrement into the water. The stock-on-hand is expected to decrease through harvesting and attrition, with the long-range goal to decrease the population to a sustained stock of approximately 250 adult fish. The facility is expected to be used in perpetuity. The Proponent has applied for a Water Use Licence with the Water Board Secretariat.The Project is located in the Kulan Industrial Subdivision, City of Whitehorse, on the Proponent's private lot accessible via Bennett Road. It falls within the Traditional Territories of the Ta'an Kwäch'än Council and the Kwanlin Dün First Nation (KDFN) and is adjacent to Settlement Land parcels KDFN C-175B and KDFN C-190B.Project Activities:- Operation of an aquaculture facility in an existing pond, including feeding fish and releasing pond water back to the creek-  Use of water (up to 350m3/day)-  Transfer of live fish into the facility in order to maintain stock-  Monitoring water quality
projectScope.summary: The Proponent, Kyle Cameron, is proposing the continued operation of his small scale arctic char rearing facility in a pond on his property. Originally created for commercial use in 1991, the facility is now only used for recreational food production. Current estimated fish populations are 620 (600 adults and 20 juveniles) with future plans to reduce and maintain stock at approximately 250 fish.The project is located at 11 Bennett Road (Lot 503) in the Kulan Industrial Area of Whitehorse.  It is located within both  Kwanlin Dün First Nation and Ta'an Kwach'an Council Traditional Territories.

projectScope.summary: The City of Whitehorse is proposing to remove solid waste from the Livingstone Trail Environmental Control Facility (LTECF) to allow for more efficient treatment of waste water and to prevent overflow of the Primary Cells. This would involve the operation, maintenance, decommissioning and reclamation of two sludge drying beds adjacent to the Primary Cells. Sludge drying beds allow sludge from sewage treatment processes to dewater through evaporation and infiltration.The sludge will undergo natural treatment during its drying, though exposure to air and sun resulting in the degradation of volatile organic compounds, decreased pathogen content and reduction of vector attractants. The treated sludge will be placed to the southwest of the LTECF.The Project is located within the Kwanlin Dün and Ta'an Kwäch'än Council Traditional Territories, just north of the city of Whitehorse. It is approximately 4 km from the neighborhood of Porter Creek on the northeastern side of the Yukon River.

projectScope.activities: The proposed project is for the renewal of the Ketza Quarry lease located within the McLean Lake Quarry Area, 5 km south east of Whitehorse. Accessible from the Copper Haul Road, the quarry has been in operation for more than a decade for the purpose of supplying pit run material for commercial use within Whitehorse. The Project involves minimal clearing and stockpiling, as well as the excavation and transport of aggregate. Approximately 50 000 m3 of granular resource (sand/gravel) is available for extraction. The estimated volume per year of extraction is 5 000 m3. Located on the Traditional Territories of the Ta'an Kwäch'än Council and Kwanlin Dün First Nation, the Project is in an industrially zoned area, and designated as part of the Whitehorse Official Community Plan for quarrying use. The anticipated timeline of the quarry is 10 years (2015-2025). Operations occur annually from May through October.Project Activities:- Use of heavy equipment- Clearing and grubbing of vegetation (approximately 0.5 ha)- Excavation of material (approximately 5 000 m3 per year)- Transportation of aggregate off-site (between 2 000 to 4 000 truckloads per year; 10 to 15 trucks per day, up to a maximum of 25)- Management of waste (e.g. on-site storage of domestic garbage and waste petroleum products)- Maintenance of existing access road (i.e. surface grading)- Reclamation of site (e.g. stockpiling of overburden for remediation; sloping exposed faces)
projectScope.summary: The proponent, Skookum Asphalt Ltd, is proposing to continue their existing operations through a renewal of their quarry lease. The Ketza Quarry has been in operations for more than a decade, and the proponent anticipates maintained operations until 2025. Equipment will be stored on site during the work season of May-October, with the purpose of extracting pit run or crushed and screened materials for commercial use in Whitehorse and or use in the onsite asphalt operations.The Project is located within the Whitehorse city limits, southeast from downtown.  The project area is within Kwanlin Dün First Nation and Ta'an Kwäch'än Council's Traditional Territories. The closest parcel of Settlement Land, KDFN C-24B, is 1.7 km away.

projectScope.activities: Yukon Government is proposing a mixed agricultural and rural residential subdivision located approximately 3.6 kilometers north of Mayo. The subdivision will consist of 19 residential lots (ranging in size from 1.6 to 2.9 ha) on both the east and west sides of the Silver Trail Highway and 5 agricultural parcels (ranging in size from 16.1 to 43.4 ha).  The residential lots will be sold through a lottery process with or without development stipulations. Agricultural parcels will be awarded through Yukon Government's (YG) Agricultural Planned Land Application Process. Applicants will be required to provide YG with an approved farm development plan that specifies which farming activities will occur. For the purposes of this assessment, the Mayo Designated Office will consider the full suite of potential farm development and residential activities that could take place on these parcels. Site preparation activities (i.e. access road upgrade/construction) are anticipated to begin in spring 2016 and will take up to 1 year to complete. projectScope.activities: The proposed project is a land lease of approximately 5 hectares, for the purposes of extraction of granular resources and establishment of a dedicated area for screening and stockpiling of granular materials. The Project is located approximately 12 km west of Haines Junction, within Champagne and Aishihik First Nations Traditional Territory. The lease area is a portion of the existing Mackintosh gravel reserve, and is accessible from an existing road at Km 1589.3 on the Alaska Highway. Users of the designated public gravel pit will continue to use the main access road at Km 1587.3. The Project will supply granular materials for the Proponent's business, serving residents of Haines Junction and surrounding areas. Project activities are planned to occur between April 1st and December 1st annually for a period of 5 years, beginning in 2017. The lease may also be renewed in subsequent 5-year terms. General operating hours will be from 8:00 am to 8:00 pm daily.Project Activities:•	Use of heavy equipment (all stored and maintained in Haines Junction, except a front-end loader and screener on site during the operating season)•	Use and maintenance of existing access road (no modifications or upgrades proposed) •	Potential installation of gate and/or granular berms at south entrance of lease area to restrict access•	Staged clearing and removal of overburden (approximately 5 ha over 5+ years)•	Piling and burning of brush and woody debris•	Stockpiling of stripped organics and silts for use in reclamation•	Excavation of granular resources (average depth 10 to 15 feet, maximum 25 feet; 2 000 m3  per year) •	Screening and stockpiling of granular resources as required for projects; maximum 500 m3 of each product type (e.g. pea gravel, sand, drain rock) stockpiled at any given time•	Potential stockpiling of crushed gravel (maximum 500 m3), either purchased or crushed on site (24 hours/day operation, 1 to 2 days per season)•	Transportation of granular resources offsite as required (up to 20 trips per day with gravel truck)•	Progressive reclamation and final decommissioning
projectScope.summary: The proposed project is a land lease of approximately 5 hectares, for the purposes of extraction of granular resources and establishment of a dedicated area for screening and stockpiling of granular materials.  The proposal was submitted on June 8, 2016 and was reviewed to determine whether there was adequate information to commence an assessment. The adequacy review identified information gaps in project information, and additional information was requested from the Proponent.  The Proponent supplied the requested information (YOR documents 2016-0115-009-1, 010-1, 011-1, 012-1, and 013-1), and the proposal was deemed Adequate on Nov 8, 2016.  The Project went into Seeking Views and Information, with a comment submission deadline of Nov 22, 2016.The project is located approximately 12 km northwest of Haines Junction.  The lease area is a portion of the existing Mackintosh gravel reserve, and is accessible from an existing road at Km 1589.3 on the Alaska Highway. Nearest watercourses to the project include Bear Creek, Summit Creek, and the Dezadeash River.  The project is located in the Southwest Assessment District, within the traditional territories of the Champagne and Aishihik First Nation.
