projectScope.activities: The proposed project is a helicopter and ground-supported Class 3 quartz mineral exploration program on 185 claims of the Spy Property, approximately 6 km southwest of Destruction Bay (D-Bay) in the Kluane Ranges.  The Project is located within Kluane and White River First Nations' Traditional Territories. The claim block is also located within Kluane Wildlife Sanctuary and abuts the borders of Kluane National Park and Reserve and a Kluane First Nation settlement land block (KFN R-1A). Project activities are planned to occur between April 1st and October 31st annually for a period of four years, beginning in 2016.Project Activities:•	Use of heavy equipment•	Access:-	Helicopter supported camps and exploration (between 1 to 10 return flights per day from or to D-Bay, camps, access roads and/or work sites)-	Use of existing off-claim roads up Nines Creek and Bock's Creek (to bring supplies and crew closer for heli-transport)•	Clearings :- Up to 10 (20 m x 20 m) clearings per claim for 15 claims, 2 clearings per claim  for 15 claims,  1 clearings per claim  for 20 claims (total clearings: 200)- Up to 35 heli-pads (10 m x 10 m)•	Construction of up to 35 heli-pads (involving use of explosives and/or building pads dug into slopes, perched wooden platforms or rock berms)•	Line cutting (up to100 km, 2 m wide)•	Trenching by blast, excavator and/or hand (up to 50 trenches, 75 m in length x 1 m deep x 1m wide)•	Use of explosives for trenching and construction of drill pads•	Ground and airborne geophysical surveys•	Mapping (UAV drone surveys and aerial photography)•	Soil and rock geochemical sampling•	Diamond drilling and reverse circulation drilling program (up to 150 holes; total length of 30 000 m) using up to 3 heli-portable drills on a 24hr/day, 7 day/week basis•	Water use from unnamed tributaries -	Up to 50 m3/day for drilling -	Up to 5 m3/day for camps•	Use of explosives (up to 500 kg per season)•	Personnel housed in:-	Destruction Bay; and/or-	Up to two temporary fly-in camps in use at any one time (five potential locations)--	10-person camp  used for up to 14 days--     Pit privies--    Canvas or nylon tents for kitchen and housing•	Waste management (burning using forced-air burner or disposal at approved landfill)•	Fuel handling and storage - Storage at up to 5 helipad and/or camp locations-  In total: 8200 L diesel; 2050 L Jet B; 1025 L gasoline; 1000 lbs propane; and 200 L oil•	Progressive annual reclamation and final decommissioning
projectScope.summary: Group Ten Metals submitted a proposal for a Class 3 Mining Land Use Permit for Quartz Exploration. The proposal was submitted on Jul 3, 2015 and was reviewed to determine whether there was adequate information to commence an assessment. The adequacy review identified information gaps in project information, and additional information was requested from the Proponent.  The Proponent supplied the requested information (YOR document 2015-0121-013-1), and the proposal was deemed Adequate on Jan 18, 2016.  The Project went into Seeking Views and Information, with a comment submission deadline of Jan 28, 2015.  Following a request from the Dan Keyi RRC, the deadline for comments was extended to Feb 12, 2016.The project is located approximately 9km southwest of Destruction Bay, within the Kluane Wildlife Sanctuary.  Access is by helicopter, and if conditions permit, travel by gravel road along Nines Creek to within 2km of the property boundary.  Bock's Creek, Mines Creek and Nines Creek and tributaries flow through the project and into Kluane Lake.  The project is located in the Southwest Assessment District, within the traditional territories of the Kluane First Nation and White River First Nation.

projectScope.summary: Norcope Construction Group is proposing to develop a new gravel quarry, operating year-round for at least 25 years. The project area is approximately 17.6 ha in total and the quarry will be developed in three phases. In Phase 1, proposed to be initiated in 2016, an 8.2 ha area will be excavated to generate approximately 1.5 million cubic meters of granular material over 25 years. The remainder of the quarry area (Phases 2 and 3), totaling 6.4 ha, is predicted to have a lifespan of an additional 20 years and is expected to generate a similar volume of granular material. The Project will be accessed via a proposed road connecting to the Alaska Highway at an existing turn-off. projectScope.summary: The Proponent is proposing to continue placer mining on 98 contiguous claims on Barker creek, Dixie Pup and three additional unnamed tributaries. Barker creek is a tributary of the Stewart River. The Project is located approximately 100 km south - southeast of Dawson City, 125 km west of Pelly Crossing. The property is accessed by barge, up the Stewart River during high waters, by road via Thistle Creek, or by air. Project activities are proposed to occur between March 15 to November 15 annually for 10 years.Project activities include works typical of placer mining operations in the area including vegetation removal, water diversions and withdrawals, in and out-of-stream reservoirs and settling ponds, processing of economical material through a wash plant and progressive and final site reclamation and closure.The Proposal was deemed adequate and entered into the public Seeking Views and Information stage on November 17, 2015. During this time comments were submitted by Yukon Government, Department of Fisheries and Oceans Canada, Selkirk First Nation, Canadian Wildlife Services and Tr'ondëk Hwëch'in First Nation. The Department of Fisheries and Oceans Canada and Selkirk First Nation submitted a request for an extension of the Seeking Views and Information period. Upon review of both request the project was extended for an addition seven days.Based on comments received and other relevant considerations, four valued environmental and socio-economic components were identified: Aquatic Resources, Environmental Quality, Wildlife and Wildlife Habitat and Heritage Resources.The Mayo Designated Office determined that the Project will result in significant adverse effects to the valued environmental and socio-economic components identified, such that further mitigation is required. The application of existing legislation, the Proponent's commitments (Appendix A), as well as the recommended mitigation measures listed below are adequate to mitigate the significant adverse effects of the Project.Barker creek is a tributary of the Stewart River. The Project is located approximately 100 km south - southeast of Dawson City; 125 km west of Pelly Crossing. The property is accessed by barge along the Yukon River to a landing on Thistle Creek during high waters, by road via Thistle Creek, or by air.

projectScope.summary: Flooding has occurred annually on the Lower Mayo River adjacent to the Village of Mayo as a result of elevated discharge to the system for the purposes of hydroelectric production. During the past several years, flooding has been mitigated reactively through emergency measures resulting in considerable damage to the riparian habitats of the Lower Mayo River. The Project includes a suite of activities designed to both remediate damage done previously as a result of emergency flood mitigation measures and to increase the hydraulic capacity of this portion of the river to prevent future flooding. Project activities are scheduled to occur between April 1st and October 31st. Initial works will be completed in one season, but sediment removal will occur periodically over the next 20 years to maintain hydraulic capacity into the future.A public comment period was held between November 5 and December 3, 2015. During this period, comments were provided by Environment Canada, Yukon Government, Department of Fisheries and Oceans Canada, Yukon Energy Corporation and Na-Cho Nyäk Dun First Nation. From the comments received and other relevant considerations, fish and fish habitat, birds and environmental quality were identified as values that would be affected by the Project. The Mayo Designated Office recommends that the Project be allowed to proceed subject to additional terms and conditions designed to reduce, eliminate or control potential significant adverse effects of the Project.The Lower Mayo River adjacent to the Village of Mayo.

projectScope.summary: The Project is currently in Information Requested from Proponent.The Project is the installation of a photo voltaic solar panel array. The purpose of the proposed project is to generate renewable energy in order to reduce diesel fuel usage in the village of Old Crow. Project activities are expected to begin as early as the summer of 2017, and the proposed life cycle of the project is 25 years.The Proposal was submitted on March 31, 2016. Following review of the Proposal, the Designated Office determined that supplemental information was required from the Proponent and thus an Information Request was issued on April 11, 2016. The Proponent provided supplementary information on June 29, 2016. On July 8, 2016, the Designated Office determined that the Proposal was adequate, and the Project was moved into Seeking Views and Information until July 22, 2016. The Designated Office determined that supplemental information was required and issued an Information Request to the Proponent on July 28, 2016.The Project is proposed to be located approximately 80 m northwest of the Old Crow airstrip in the village of Old Crow within the Vuntut Gwitchin Traditional Territory.

projectScope.summary: The Proponent, Bryan Pitman, is proposing  to operate a placer mine on nine claims on Upper Bonanza Creek and five claims on Rainbow Gulch, within the Klondike River Watershed. Project activities are proposed to occur from April 15 to October 30, annually for 10 years. The Proposal was submitted on July 1, 2016. Following an initial review of provided proposal documents, the Designated Office determined that supplemental information was required, and as such, an Information Request was issued on July 4, 2016. The Proponent provided additional information on August 23, 2016, and the Proponent was deemed adequate on August 29, 2016. Views and Information on the proposed Project were sought from August 29, 2016 until September 12, 2016. Based on comment received, the Designated Office determined that additional information was provided by the Proponent, and an Information Request was issued on September 16, 2016. The response to this Information Request was provided on October 27, 2016, and further clarification was sought by the Designated Office on November 1, 2016. The Project is now in Preparing RecommendationThe Project is located approximately 35 km from Dawson City, and is situated within the Traditional Territory of the Tr'ondëk Hwëch'in.

projectScope.summary: The Proponent, Northern Exposures Inc. is proposing to conduct placer mining activities on 675 claims on the Indian River and Ruby Creek. The Proposal was submitted on August 26, 2015. Upon review of the Project, the Designated Office determined that supplemental information was required, and thus an Information Request was issued on September 8, 2015. The Proponent responded to the Information Request on November 13, 2015. Following review of the response, the Designated Office determined that the response was deficient, specifically in regards to questions asked pertaining to wetland habitat within the project area. The Proponent responded to the Designated Office's deficiencies on November 23, 2015, and the Project was deemed adequate on December 1, 2015. The Designated Office sought views and information on the proposed project from December 1, 2015 to December 22, 2015. The Designated Office is currently preparing the recommendation or referral for this Project. Under the Designated Office rules, the deadline for Preparing Recommendation or Referral is January 13, 2016.The Project is located in the Indian River watershed, and within the traditional territory of the Tr'ondek Hwech'in.

projectScope.summary: Teslin Tlingit Council is proposing a pilot project directed at the restoration of a spawning Chinook salmon population in Deadman Creek through the seeding of fertilized eggs. Pursuant to Part 8 of the Fishery (General) Regulations, the Project requires a license for the Introduction, Transplant or Transport of Live Fish, Fish Eggs or Aquatic Invertebrates within the Yukon Territory. Project activities will be focused on the lower 5 km of Deadman Creek and will make use of the Morley River as a control stream from Morley Bay in Teslin Lake to Morley Lake. Project activities are proposed to occur as soon as relevant authorizations are granted in 2016 and run until June 30 2017.Both watercourses are located in the Teslin River watershed and fall within the Traditional Territory of the Teslin Tlingit Council. Deadman Creek is located approximately 30 km northwest from the community of Teslin; the mouth of the Morley River is approximately 15 km southeast

projectScope.summary: The Proponent (Takhini Hot Springs Ltd.) is applying for a water licence to build a new hot springs facility. The new facilities will consist of three components: a public pool area, a rental pool area, and a Nordic spa pool area. It will also involve a new heat exchange system. The Proponent will create one new pond and one new water discharge stream to provide for additional chlorine evaporation and water treatment. Construction activities are proposed to occur from 2016 through the summer of 2018, with operations and monitoring activities as required for the life of the pool facility (estimated 50 to 100 years). The existing facility will remain open until the new facility is completed, at which point it will be decommissioned. The work areas will be fenced off from public access. Work crews will stay in the existing campground and hostel facilities; no work camp is required.The Project occurs on the Proponent's property, at the end if the Hot Springs Road off the North Klondike Highway, approximately 30 km (by road) from downtown Whitehorse. It overlaps with the Traditional Territories of the Kwanlin Dün First Nation and the Ta'an Kwach'an Council, and is adjacent to Settlement Land parcels. As well, it is approximately 1 km east of the boundary for the Traditional Territory of the Champagne and Aishihik First Nation.

projectScope.summary: This Project proposes the clearing of alder and willow regeneration along an existing road right-of-way connecting km 110 of the South Canol Road to Big Salmon Lake. The purpose of the vegetation clearing is to improve snowmobile access for trapping purposes (concession #238). The trail to be cleared is 14 km in length and is located within the Traditional Territories of Teslin Tlingit Council and Kaska Dena Nation (Ross River Dena and Liard). It is currently used by the Proponent to access his trapline; by a private owner to access their cabin on an island in Big Salmon Lake; and occasionally by hunters or recreationists. No surface upgrades will be carried out and no overburden will be removed. The trail does not cross watercourses and does not require bridges or culverts. Activities will take place in late summer/fall 2017, between August 15 and October 15 for approximately 10 days.The trail is located in the traditional territories of the Teslin Tlingit Counciol and the Kaska Dena  Nation (Ross River Dena and Liard), along an existing road right-of-way connecting km 110 of the South Canol Road to Big Salmon Lake.

projectScope.summary: The proposed project is for Class III quartz exploration on 280 claims of the Plata Property. The exploration program initially consists of soil sampling, geophysical surveys, geological mapping, prospecting and mechanized and hand trenching. These activities will likely be followed by diamond drilling and/or percussion drilling and mechanized trenching in 2016 or subsequent years, ending in 2020. Activities will occur from June 1 through October 30 each year. Drilling equipment will be mobilized to the project area by fixed wing aircraft from Mayo to an existing airstrip on-site. Helicopters may be used to move crew members to/from drill sites and access other parts of the property. Other access is by ATV on new and existing trails and roads. The Proponent will not utilize the winter road connecting the site to the North Canol Road. Water will be sourced from Fido Creek for camp and drilling.The Plata Property located in east-central Yukon, approximately 190 km east of Mayo and 165 km north of Ross River. The Project falls within the Traditional Territory of the First Nation of Na-Cho Nyäk Dun.

projectScope.activities: The proposed project is a landscape photography course at Kathleen Lake, located approximately 27 km south of Haines Junction and within Kluane National Park and Reserve (KNPR). The Project is located within Champagne and Aishihik First Nations Traditional Territory. The Project's purpose is to improve course participants' skills in landscape photography (specifically, by photographing the sunrise at Kathleen Lake) and will involve up to ten people, including instructors. Project activities are planned to occur on one day in September 2016, with a duration of approximately three hours. Project Activities:•	Use of established Kathleen Lake viewing area closest to the parking lot•	Access by personal and/or rental vehicles•	Food and waste management, utilizing established Kathleen Lake day use area facilities
projectScope.summary: The proposed project is a landscape photography course at Kathleen Lake, located within Kluane National Park and Reserve (KNPR). The proposal was submitted on July 11th, 2016 and was reviewed by the Haines Junction Designated Office to determine whether the information was adequate to commence an assessment. The proposal was deemed adequate on July 13th, 2016.  The Designated Office provided an opportunity for comment on the Project during the Seeking Views and Information period from July 13th to July 27th, 2016. No comments were received during this period.Given the very short duration of the Project (three hours), that no activities are proposed outside of the established and well-used parking lot and immediate surrounding areas, and in consideration of the Proponent's commitments as well as relevant legislation, little to no adverse effects to valued components were apparent to the Designated Office. However, to ensure due diligence, the Designated Office discussed its considerations made in regards to six valued components: Aquatic Resources, Environmental Quality, Wildlife and Wildlife Habitat, Heritage Resources, Visitor Experience, and Health and Safety. The rationale for this determination is presented in the Evaluation Report.The Evaluation Report and Recommendation was issued on August 16th, 2016. The Designated Office recommended to the Decision Body (Parks Canada) that the Project be allowed to proceed, with no additional terms and conditions. The Decision Document has not yet been issued for this Project.The Project is located at the Kathleen Lake day-use area within Kluane National Park and Reserve (KNPR).  The nearest community is Haines Junction, located 27 km to the north.  The nearest watercourses and waterbodies to the Project are Kathleen Lake and the Kathleen River.  The Project is located within the Southwest Assessment District, within the Traditional Territory of the Champagne and Aishihik First Nations.

projectScope.summary: The proposed project is a commercial wilderness tourism operation within Kluane National Park and Reserve (KNPR). The Project involves guided day-hikes and backpacking trips combined with wilderness skills training including: route-finding, navigation, winter survival, wilderness first aid, environmental interpretation, First Nation and cultural interpretation, tourism training, leave-no-trace principles, environmental studies and awareness, leadership, wilderness photography, conservation principles and avalanche safety. The Proponent has proposed up to 12 day trips and 6 overnight or multi-day trips (up to 8 days each) per year, for a period of five years (2016 to 2021).      The Haines Junction Designated Office provided an opportunity for comment on the Project during the Seeking Views and Information period from May 12th to June 9th, 2016. Comments were submitted by the Kluane First Nation (KFN).  The Designated Office identified four valued components in the assessment: fish and wildlife, human and environmental health, visitor experience and heritage resources and traditional pursuits. It was determined by the Designated Office that the Project will not result in significant adverse effects to the above-mentioned valued components upon implementation of commitments and mitigations proposed by the Proponent, in addition to compliance with the relevant legislation.Kluane National Park and Reserve

projectScope.activities: Northern Cross (Yukon) Ltd. is proposing to drill and conduct extended flow testing on up to 20 wells as part of a crude oil and natural gas exploration program. Project activities are proposed to begin January 2016 and will occur annually for eight years. The Project will take place in a 325 km2 area, approximately 30 km south of Eagle Plains. It also is to occur within the Vuntut Gwitchin and First Nation of Nacho Nyäk Dun traditional territories as well as the Tetlit Gwich'in Secondary Use Area. The Project is in the North Yukon Planning Region, and at the headwaters of the Porcupine River and Upper Porcupine-Bell River watersheds.Project ActivitiesWell Development and Evaluation•	Establishment of 20 drill pads including use of wooden mats, local granular material or wood chips generated in situ•	Drilling of 20 wells                6 candidate winter wells                14 candidate summer wells•	Well testing, including extended flow tests•	Flaring•	Establishment of Central Processing Facility located at Main Camp•	Storage and transportation of produced fluid and crude oil•	Waste disposal                Establishment of Mix Bury Cover Sump                Deep well injection                Transport to approved waste disposal sites•	Well suspension and/or abandonment Water Use•	Up to 300 m³/day total for drilling and winter access developmentBaseline Data Collection•	Fisheries sampling at each watercourse crossing (e.g. trapping, electrofishing, beach seining or  angling)•	Surface water sampling (18 sites)•	Groundwater sampling (installation of shallow monitoring wells down gradient of exploration wells.•	Permafrost sampling (temperature probing)Access and Access Development•	Helicopter access to support sampling/monitoring programs•	Transportation/Traffic along Dempster Highway, Klondike Highway, Alaska Highway•	Establishment of up to 82 km of winter access•	Establishment of up to 82 km of all season access•	Establishment of 5 water crossings•	Decommissioning and reclamation of access routesBorrow Pit Development•	Development of approximately 21  borrow pits•	Excavation and transportation of approximately 410 400m³ fill for well pads (21 600 m³/well)•	Excavation and transportation of approximately 1 276 140 m³ fill for all season access development•	Use of Explosives•	Final pit reclamation and rehabilitation of borrow pitsMobile Drill Camp•	Use of drill rig camp (5-8 people)Fuel Storage and Use•	151 000L diesel•	24 000L propane
projectScope.summary: The Designated Office is currently preparing it's recommendation or referral under section 56(1) of the Yukon Environmental and Socio-Economic Assessment Act.  The deadline for the Designated Office has been extended to January 6, 2016.*** YESAB ONLINE REGISTRY - VIEWING PROJECT DETAILSThe Designated Office recognizes that the volume of documents on the Registry, including multiple revisions to some documents, is difficult to navigate. We have tried to organize the presentation of the file on the Registry in a manner that makes available to you the most relevant versions. TO VIEW ONLY THE PROJECT PROPOSAL (including Appendices and Responses to Information Requests) Click on the "Project Proposal Documents" link in the upper right corner of this Summary Page. We recommend removing the obsolete documents from view by checking off the "Hide Obsolete Documents" option (located beside the "Show" Button.   Project Summary: Northern Cross (Yukon) Ltd. (NCY) is proposing to drill and conduct extended flow tests on up to 20 exploratory oil and gas wells in the Eagle Plains region.  Up to ten wells will be drilled and tested per year (to a maximum of 20 wells total) from December to mid-April and May 1 to October 31, over eight years. Drill cuttings will be disposed of in a mix-bury-cover sump. Drilling fluids require approximately 5m³/day of water and will be reused between wells. Drilling wastes may be disposed of through a mix bury cover (MBC) sump or deep well injection. Wells will be evaluated through a variety of methods that include drill stem tests, swabbing, acid stimulation and flow tests. Any discoveries will be followed by extended flow tests, which would require all season access. Extended flow tests at each discovery could last up to two years. The Project includes the construction and use of up to 82 km of roads; roads will first be constructed as winter roads and will be converted to all-season roads if extended flow testing is warranted. Roads will be constructed during winter (November 1-April 15) and summer (May 1 to October 31). Winter roads will have a minimum snowpack of 10 cm and will require up to 300 m³/day of water from the Eagle and/or Ogilvie Rivers. Access to the site includes up to five water crossings.Total borrow material required for road construction, pullouts/passing zones, well pads is 1,686,440 m3. Borrow pits will be designed and developed during winter 2016. NCY has identified 23 borrow site locations; these sites were determined through data from earlier geotechnical evaluations and NCY's 3D seismic program in 2014.The Project will be supported by an existing main camp at kilometer 325 of the Dempster Highway, mobile camps and drill site camps; the main and mobile camps can support up to 75 individuals each, while the drill site camps can support up to 8 individuals.Project activities will take place across an area of 325 km2, which is transected by the Dempster Highway; the area is approximately 30 km south of Eagle Plains. The Project is situated within the First Nation of Nacho Nyäk Dun and Vuntut Gwitchin traditional territories, and the Tetlit Gwich'in Secondary Use Area.  The Project is located in Land Management Unit 9, Eagle Plain, of the North Yukon Planning Region, and at the Headwaters of the Porcupine River and Upper Porcupine-Bell River watersheds.

KBL proposes to construct and operate a facility for separating contaminated soils and treating select soils. Hydrocarbon contaminated soil will be treated on site using conventional mechanical aeration treatment and thermal desorption. Soil and co-mingled material, e.g. metal or wood debris, will be separated and recycled. Untreatable soil and non-recoverable materials will be temporarily stored and shipped for disposal or treatment. The facility may serve as a platform for researching soil remediation methods. The facility will be co-located with KBL's existing Special Waste Transfer Facility at the Whitehorse Landfill. Office facilities, washrooms, etc. will be provided through KBL's existing facility. The project is located within the traditional territories of the Kwanlin Dün First Nation and the Ta'an Kwäch'än Council. The facility is designed for a 16 year life span and is scheduled to be decommissioned in 2032

projectScope.summary: Earth and Iron Inc. is proposing a class 4 placer mine on 11 claims and four placer leases centered along Granite Creek, located approximately 14 km southeast of Keno City. Project operations are scheduled to occur between April 1st and October 31st annually for the next ten years. Conventional gravity separation placer mining technology will be employed to extract gold from placer gravels. Mining will be guided by exploration and will utilize conventional gravity separation using a wash plant. Waste water will be treated using settling ponds prior to release back to Granite Creek. Progressive and final reclamation will include spreading of organic matter and fine texture soils on disturbed areas to promote natural re-vegetation.Views and Information were sought between January 26 and February 17, 2017. The period was extended upon request from Na-Cho Nyäk Dun First Nation. Based on comments received and other relevant information, aquatic resources, birds, environmental quality, moose and heritage resources were four values identified as potentially affected by the Project. Additional mitigation measures are recommended to reduce, eliminate or control significant adverse effects identified.The Project is located along Granite Creek north of Mayo Lake.

projectScope.summary: This proposed project combines two proposals received by the Designated Office for activities taking place within the Nisutlin River Delta National Wildlife Area (NWA) located in the Traditional Territory of Teslin Tlingit Council (TTC). The goal of the Project is to overwinter and graze a maximum of 30 outfitting horses in the abovementioned floodplain for 10 years. A capacity grazing assessment, performed by Government of Yukon, Agriculture, would be carried out over one to two days between June 15 and August 30, 2017 to assess the land's capacity for grazing. Grazing is planned to occur in the proposed grazing area while activities related to the capacity assessment may occur anywhere within the National Wildlife Area. The overwintering of horses would occur seasonally, with the horses trailed into the project area around mid-October and trailed out prior to spring flooding, which generally occurs at the beginning of May.The project is proposed to occur within the floodplain of the Nisutlin River Delta National Wildlife Area (NWA) located in the Traditional Territory of Teslin Tlingit Council (TTC).

The winter access road (Yukon route option) overlaps TH, WRFN and Kluane First Nation Traditional Territories. The Project is located on the east fork of Swamp Creek, a tributary of Scottie Creek. total length of 500 m)•	Rotary/auger drilling program for soil sampling (20 holes @ 5 to 10 m in depth; maximum total length of 200 m)o	Digging of 25 sumps for drill cuttings (1 m x 2 m x 1 m each)o	Water use (up to 85 m3/year) including:•	Camp use (up to 75 m3/year, sourced from collected rainwater and/or unnamed creek located east of camp)•	Diamond drilling (up to 10 m3/year, sourced from Swamp Creek and/or second unnamed creek)o	Use of existing camp (maximum 5 people, 500 person-days per season) consisting of 2 trailers and 4 frame tents or cabinso	Transportation, handling and storage of petroleum products (up to 92,000 L diesel, 4,000 L gasoline, up to 300 L of oil and other lubricants)o	Waste management•	Kitchen waste burned on site in incinerator or backhauled to approved landfills•	Camp graywater disposal via buried sump•	Use of 2 existing pit privies•	Burial of scrap metal on site•	Backhaul of waste oil and other hazardous materials to Dawson City or Beaver Creek for disposal at approved landfillso	Progressive annual reclamation and final decommissioning
projectScope.summary: The Proponent submitted a proposal for a Class 3 Mining Land Use Permit for Quartz Exploration on 234 claims of the East Fork Project. The proposal was submitted on Dec 18, 2015 and was reviewed to determine whether there was adequate information to commence an assessment. The adequacy review identified information gaps in project information, and additional information was requested from the Proponent.  The Proponent supplied the requested information (YOR documents 2015-0199-016-1, 019-1, 021-1, 023-1), and the proposal was deemed Adequate on Jul 8, 2016.  The Project went into Seeking Views and Information, with a comment submission deadline of Jul 22, 2016.  Following a request from the White River First Nation, the deadline for comments was extended to Aug 12, 2016.The project is located approximately 65km north of Beaver Creek, and adjacent to the Alaska border in the Moosehorn Mountain area.  The Project is located on the east fork of Swamp Creek, a tributary of Scottie Creek.  The project is located in the Southwest Assessment District, within Tr'ondëk Hwëch'in (TH) Traditional Territory and overlaps with a White River First Nation (WRFN) interim protected land parcel (WRFN S-147B). The winter access road (Yukon route option) overlaps TH, WRFN and Kluane First Nation Traditional Territories.

projectScope.summary: The proposed project is a ground and helicopter-supported Class 3 quartz mineral exploration program on 447 claims on the Ultra Property. Project activities are planned to occur between April 1 and October 31 annually, for a period of five years, beginning in 2016.      The Haines Junction Designated Office sought views and information on the proposed project from March 7 to 29, 2016. A request for an extension until April 12, 2016 was granted at the request of CAFN. Comments were received from Natural Resources Canada, Champagne and Aishihik First Nation, Yukon Conservation Society, Parks Canada, various Government of Yukon departments, Environment and Climate Change Canada, Alsek Renewable Resource Council, and various members of the public. Based on the comments received and other relevant considerations, four valued environmental and socio-economic components were identified: Environmental Quality, Wildlife and Wildlife Habitat, Heritage Resources, and Other User Interests. On July 15, 2016, the Haines Junction Designated Office determined that the Project was likely to result in significant adverse effects to Environmental Quality, Wildlife and Wildlife Habitat, and Heritage Resources such that additional mitigations are recommended. The application of existing legislation, the Proponent's commitments (Appendix A), as well as the recommended mitigation measures in the Evaluation Report are adequate to mitigate the significant adverse effects of the Project.The Decision Body, Government of Yukon, issued their Decision Document on August 17, 2016 which varied the recommendations.The Project is located approximately 42 km west of Haines Junction in the Kuane Ranges. The property is located within Champagne and Aishihik First Nation Traditional Territory. The claim block is also located in the Kluane Wildlife Sanctuary and abuts the border of Kluane National Park and Reserve.

projectScope.activities: The proposed project is the construction of new ditches and clean out of existing ditches, at various locations from Km 1710 to Km 1790.5 on the Alaska Highway, north of Burwash Landing. The ditches are required to prevent ponding water at culverts, which can cause accelerated melting of permafrost and potential culvert failures. The Project will occur in conjunction with the existing Shakwak Highway Project road repair and maintenance work in the same area and will use the same camps. The Project is located within Kluane First Nation (KFN) and White River First Nation (WRFN) Traditional Territories. Some ditches are proposed to be constructed on KFN Settlement Land parcels R-47A, R-49B and R-20B or on crown land outside of the highway Right-of-Way (ROW). One ditch is adjacent to WRFN interim protected land selection S-108B. Project activities are planned to occur between September 1st and October 31st, 2016 or early spring or fall 2017.Project Activities:•	Use of heavy equipment•	Clearing and piling of vegetation and soil•	Ditch Work:- Excavation of 7 new offtake ditches, each approximately 80 to 200 m long- Excavation of 1 new interceptor ditch (within the ROW)- Clean out of 6 existing offtake or interceptor ditches with extensions where required•	Transportation and handling of petroleum products (up to 12 000 L of diesel)
projectScope.summary: The proposed project is the construction of new ditches and clean out of existing ditches, at various locations from Km 1710 to Km 1790.5 on the Alaska Highway, north of Burwash Landing. The proposal was reviewed to determine whether the information was adequate to commence an assessment, and additional information was requested on Jul 26, 2016. The proponent supplied the additional information (YOR Documents # 2016-0130-012-1, 2016-0130-014-1, and 2016-0130-013-2) and the proposal was deemed Adequate on Sept 7, 2016.  The Project went into Seeking Views and Information and has a comment submission deadline of Sept 21, 2016.The Project is located at various locations from Alaska Highway Km 1710 to Km 1790.5.  The nearest community is Burwash Landing, located 11km south of the first ditch at Km 1712.5.  The nearest watercourses and waterbodies to the Project are Duke River, Burwash Creek, Sakiw Creek, Kluane River, Swede Johnson Creek, Andrew Lake, and Quill Creek.  The Project is located within the Southwest Assessment District, within the traditional territories of the Kluane First Nation and the White River First Nation.

projectScope.summary: The Project is a combination of two agricultural land applications for the development of a joint hay farm and elk viewing tourism operation. The agricultural land applications are for approximately 65 ha each, for a total of 130 ha (1.3 km2),which will be cleared for hay production. Hay fields will be rotated to allow elk to forage on fallow fields.Elk viewing will occur during any time of the year but will be mostly during the rut season. Guests will overnight in tents and/or yurts. Agricultural development will commence upon approval and will take approximately 4 years to complete, while the Project will have an indefinite lifespan.The parcels are located approximately 10 km east of theMendenhallSubdivision and 63 km west of Whitehorse. The Project also overlaps with the Takhini Valley Elk Herd corehabitat range and the Traditional Territories of Champagne and Aishihik and Kwanlin Dün FirstNations.

projectScope.summary: The British Exploring Society is proposing the establishment of a camp at Rose Lake along the South Canol Road. This camp will be the base camp for trekking and paddling expeditions for youth aging from 16-25. The expeditions include a choice of a 3-week trek, a 3-week paddle or a 5-week trek & paddle in the Pelly Mountain area and along the Teslin River. The expedition teams will explore the surrounding areas by foot or canoe and take part in a variety of science projects such as flora and fauna surveys, permafrost transects and water sampling. The expedition is anticipated to occur in the summer of 2017, from mid-late July to late August, depending on flights.Camp located approximately 75 km southwest of Ross River and 150 km northeast of Johnson's Crossing.

projectScope.summary: Under the Agriculture Planned Land Application Review (APLAR) process, the Proponent proposes to develop one 6.0 ha parcel for the sale and establishment of commercial agriculture production and residential use. Applicants to the APLAR process will be required to provide the Proponent with an approved farm development plan that specifies which farming activities will occur. In the first year of the Project, the Proponent will undertake site preparation activities, such as clearing of land for access construction. The Project also includes farm development activities, which will be completed within seven years by the successful applicant. The use and occupation of the area is permanent. For the purposes of this assessment, the Designated Office will consider the full suite of potential activities that could take place on this parcel.The Hotsprings Road Development Area Regulations will apply to the Project. In order to maintain the required minimum parcel size, the Project involves the extension of the parcel approximately 5 m into the Yukon Energy Corporation's powerline easement.At the corner of the Hotsprings Road and 3 Mile Road, the Project is located approximately 2 km north of the Takhini River. It falls within the Traditional Territories of Kwalin Dün First Nation and Ta'an Kwäch'än Council.

projectScope.summary: The purpose of the Project is the development (clearing, leveling, widening) of the existing Skyline Ski Trail, to allow grooming and skiing between September and April each year. The Skyline Trail is particularly used in the shoulder of the ski season (October/November and April/May) when skiing is not possible on lower trails. The Proponent has an existing lease for the trail, and is proposing to widen the trail to 4 m width, to allow grooming equipment to pass through. The trail is currently 1.5 m wide and 4.5 km long.The trail connects Fish Lake Road to the McIntytre Ascent/Jack Fraser Loop trails in the Mount McIntyre recreation area. It overlaps with the Traditional Territory of the Kwanlin Dün First Nation.

projectScope.activities: The proposed project consists of the installation, maintenance and decommissioning of four seismometers; two within Ivvavik National Park (INP) and two within Kluane National Park and Reserve (KNPR).  The seismometers are part of a larger ongoing research initiative conducted by the IRIS, called the EarthScope: Transportable Array (USArray) Project. The sites are located within the Inuvialuit Settlement Region and the Kluane First Nation and White River First Nation Traditional Territories. Work to the remote sites will be supported by fixed wing or helicopter flights from either Inuvik in the Northwest Territories, Haines Junction or Whitehorse, or existing park research camps, depending on the site. Each installation and decommissioning event will take up to 2 days to undertake; maintenance will occur on an as need basis. The sites will be installed in 2016 and 2017, between the months of mid-May and mid-October, as weather permits, and all four sites will be decommissioned in 2019. Project Activities:•	Approximately 2 to 3 fixed wing and/or helicopter return flights per site for each installation, maintenance and decommissioning event•	Drilling with a lightweight transportable drill (up to 5 m deep)•	Installation, maintenance and decommissioning of USArray station (with a footprint of 10 m x 10 m each) consisting of:-	A seismometer inserted in a PVC or steel casing within drilled hole -	An insulated electronics and power system house (1.5 m x 1.5 m x 2 m) •	Temporary fuel storage (up to 416 L of Jet Fuel, and up to 38 L of  gas)•	Use of existing park research camps (i.e. Sheep Creek Camp in INP)
projectScope.summary: Incorporated Research Institution of Seismology (IRIS) submitted a proposal for the installation of four seismometers within Ivvavik and Kluane National Parks. The proposal was submitted on Nov 24, 2015 and was reviewed to determine whether there was adequate information to commence an assessment. The adequacy review identified information gaps in project information, and additional information was requested from the Proponent.  The Proponent supplied the requested information (YOR document 2015-0186-014-1), and the proposal was deemed Adequate on Dec 8, 2015.  The Project went into Seeking Views and Information, with a comment submission deadline of Dec 22, 2015.      The Haines Junction Designated Office provided an opportunity for comment on the Project during the Seeking Views and Information period from December 8th to 22nd, 2015. Comments were submitted by KFN and Environment Canada's Canadian Wildlife Service (CWS). Within comment submissions, concerns that were raised spoke to migratory and protected birds and aircraft use. The Designated Office identified two valued components in the assessment: wildlife and wildlife habitat and traditional and cultural values. It was determined by the Designate Office that the Project will have significant adverse effects on one valued component: wildlife and wildlife habitat. However, by implementing the recommended mitigation measures in the Evaluation Report, in addition to those that the Proponent has committed to and relevant legislative requirements, the Designated Office is satisfied that the adverse effects can be reduced, controlled or eliminated such that they are not significant. The rationale for this determination is presented in the Evaluation Report.The project is located at four remote locations, two within Kluane National Park and Reserve, and two within Ivvavik National Park.  The Kluane locations lie within the icefields, below Mt Upton (028M-T1), and at the confluence of the Dusty and Lowell Glaciers (029M-T3). The sites are accessed by helicopter or ski-equipped fixed-wing aircraft, approximately one hour flying time (80-140km) west of Haines Junction.  The Ivvavik sites are located west of Malcolm River (D27M-1), and at Stokes Point (D28M-5).  Access is by helicopter from Inuvik, NWT, with equipment also staged at Sheep Creek camp for access to D27M-1.  The sites lie within the Peel and SW Beaufort Sea, Copper and Alsek drainages.  The project is located in both the Southwest and North Yukon Assessment Districts, within the traditional territories of the Kluane First Nation and the White River First Nation, and the Inuvialuit Settlement Area.

projectScope.activities: Project ScopeThe proposed project is for the renewal of a class 4 placer mining operation on 351 claims on Gladstone Creek, seven of its tributaries, Kluane Lake, and Cyr Creek. The Project is located approximately 10 km east of the community of Destruction Bay across Kluane Lake within the Traditional Territories of the Kluane First Nation and the White River First Nation. Although several claims overlap with two Kluane First Nation settlement land blocks (KFN R-13A and KFN 1-18B), no mining activities are planned for these claims. The site will be accessed by an existing road and by summer and winter travel on Kluane Lake. No new access roads or trails are proposed within the claim block. Activities are proposed to occur between February 1st and November 15th, starting in 2016, for ten years.Project Activities:•	Use of heavy equipment for mining exploration, extraction and associated activities•	Water use:      o	Maximum daily use of 51 830 m3/day from Gladstone Creek, tributaries, and Kluane Lake           -30 006 m3/day for sluicing,           -21 822 m3/day for hydraulic stripping, and           -2 m3/day for camp use•	Overburden removal:      o	up to 350 000 m3/year mechanically, and      o	up to 20 000 m3/year hydraulically•	Sluicing: up to 490 000 m3/year•	Drilling: up to 8 holes per claim with a 4 m2 clearing per hole and a maximum of 112 holes per year•	Use of the creek as a conduit (all creeks except Gladstone Creek)•	Use of out-of-stream reservoir ponds and cross valley dams •	Use of out-of-stream and instream settling ponds•	Creek diversions:      o	up to 14 935 m of intermittent diversions in Gladstone Creek,      o	up to 4115 m in Cyr Creek, and      o	between 1067 m and  2424 m in each of the seven tributaries•	Water crossings:     o	use of 50 existing fords on Gladstone Creek, tributaries and Cyr Creek     o	ice road and summer boat crossings on Kluane Lake•	Use and maintenance of existing roads (possible culvert repairs and/or installation on access road)•	Use of a camp for up to 10 people     o	use of trailers, shipping containers and a framed structure     o	use of 4 pit privies•	Fuel storage:     o	up to 3075 L of gasoline, and      o	up to 272 760 L of diesel •	Waste management •	Progressive and final reclamation
projectScope.summary: TIC Exploration submitted a proposal for the renewal of a Class 4 Mining Land Use permit and Water License for 351 claims on Gladstone Creek. The proposal was submitted on Oct 28, 2015 and was reviewed to determine whether there was adequate information to commence an assessment. The adequacy review identified information gaps in project information, and additional information was requested from the Proponent.  The Proponent supplied the requested information (YOR document 2015-0155-027-1), and the proposal was deemed Adequate on Nov 17, 2015.  The Project went into Seeking Views and Information, with a comment submission deadline of Dec 1, 2015.      Comments were submitted by the Government of Yukon (YG), Environment Canada's Canadian Wildlife Service (CWS), KFN, and late submissions from Department of Fisheries and Oceans (DFO) and CAFN. Within comment submissions, concerns that were raised spoke to access, heritage resources, water quality, the environment, wildlife, migratory and protected birds, fish and rare plants. The Designated Office identified four valued components in the assessment: water quality, environmental quality, wildlife and wildlife habitat and heritage resources. It was determined by the Designate Office that the Project will have significant adverse effects on all four valued components. However, by implementing the recommended mitigation measures in the evaluation report, in addition to those that the Proponent has committed to and relevant legislative requirements, the Designated Office is satisfied that the adverse effects can be reduced, controlled or eliminated such that they are not significant.The project is located on Gladstone Creek, Kluane Lake, Cyr Creek, and 7 No-name Pups that drain into Gladstone Creek.  The Project area lies on the East side of Kluane Lake, approx. 10km from Destruction Bay, and 40km North of Silver City.  Access is seasonally by the Cultus Bay road from Silver City, or across Kluane Lake.  Gladstone Creek is a tributary of Kluane Lake, which is in turn a tributary of the White River, lying within the Yukon River watershed.  The project is located in the Southwest Assessment District, within the traditional territories of the Kluane First Nation and the White River First Nation.  It overlaps Settlement Land parcel KFN R-18B, is adjacent to KFN R-13A and downstream of KFN R-24A.

The Project will take place from May 1st to October 31st for ten years.Project Activities:•	Water use: up to 10 000 m3/day •	No to minimal overburden removal •	Material processing: up to 300 000 m3/year •	Hydraulic stripping•	Heavy equipment use •	Progressive reclamation •	Use of stream as a conduit•	Settling facilities:o	Out-of-streamo	Instream•	Reservoirs:o	Out-of-stream o	Instream dug outs o	Instream cross-valley dams •	Permanent diversion o	Up to 4060 m (intermittent over this length) •	Water Crossings o	Use of 3 existing fords o	Construction of 5 new fords •	Access: o	Landing barge in the summero	Ice road in wintero	Use and maintenance of existing roads and trailso	New road construction (4 000 m x 3 m) •	Waste Management o	Incineration of kitchen waste o	Removal of hazardous waste to an approved facility in Whitehorse •	Camp use for up to 6 peopleo	One existing pit privy o	Use of generatoro	Potable water from surface water•	Fuel storage o	Diesel up to 5000 L o	Gas up to 2300 L
projectScope.summary: The Proponent, Nathan Dunford of Jabberwocky Exploration Ltd, submitted a proposal to establish a class 4 placer mine on 29 claims along two unnamed tributaries of Granite Bay, Talbot Arm, Kluane Lake for the next ten years. The proposal was reviewed to determine whether the information was adequate to commence an assessment.  The adequacy review identified information gaps in project information, and additional information was requested from the Proponent.  The Proponent supplied the requested information (YOR document 2015-0094-012-1), and the proposal was deemed Adequate on May 22, 2015.  The Project went into Seeking Views and Information with a comment submission deadline of June 5, 2015. Comments were submitted by the White River First Nation, Department of Fisheries and Oceans Canada, Government of Yukon, Kluane First Nation and Environment Canada's Canadian Wildlife Services. The Designated Office identified four valued components in the assessment: aquatic resources, environmental quality, wildlife and wildlife habitat and heritage resources. It was determined by the Designate Office that the Project will have or is likely to have significant adverse effects on all four valued components that can be mitigated and recommended on July 2, 2015 that this Project proceed with terms and conditions.The project is located approximately 15km northeast of Burwash Landing near the Granite Bay area of Talbot Arm (Kluane Lake).  The claims are on two unnamed tributaries of Kluane Lake, which lies within the White River watershed.  The Project is located in the Southwest Assessment District, within the traditional territories of the Kluane First Nation and White River First Nation.

projectScope.activities: The purpose of the Project is the renewal of a Class 4 Mining Land Use permit and Water License on 10 placer claims along Kimberly Creek, tributary of Jarvis River, in the Alsek River watershed. The Project is located in the Champagne and Aishihik First Nations Traditional Territory, and on Settlement Land parcel CAFN R-47A. The Project is located approximately 46 km northwest of Haines Junction. Activities are proposed to occur from June to end of October for ten years. Project Activities:•	Water use: up to 6820 m3/day •	Material processing: 125 000 m3/year•	Access: - Construction of a new access road (1500 m by 5 m)- Use of existing access roads and fords on Kimberly Creek and Jarvis River- Use of 1 existing ford on claims •	Creek diversion: up to 1500 m long - Use of out-of-stream settling facilities and reservoirs•	Heavy equipment use •	Fuel Storage: - Diesel: up to 25 890 L - Gas: up to 205 L •	Camp use (up to 4 persons) - Use of trailers- Installation of a new pit privy - Drinking water from creek•	Waste Management: - Disposal at approved facility •	Progressive reclamation
projectScope.summary: Tana Contracting submitted a proposal for the renewal of a Class 4 Mining Land Use permit and Water License on Kimberly Creek, a tributary of the Jarvis River. The proposal was submitted on May 6, 2015 and was reviewed to determine whether there was adequate information to commence an assessment. The adequacy review identified information gaps in project information, and additional information was requested from the Proponent.  The Proponent supplied the requested information (YOR documents 2015-0100-010-1 and 2015-0100-011-1), and the proposal was deemed Adequate on May 29, 2015.  The Haines Junction Designated Office provided an opportunity for comment on the Project during the Seeking Views and Information period from May 29th to June 19th, 2015. Comments were submitted by the Department of Fisheries and Oceans (DFO), Environment Canada's Canadian Wildlife Service (CWS), Government of Yukon (YG) and Champagne and Aishihik First Nations (CAFN).  The Designated Office identified four valued components in the assessment: water quality, environmental quality, wildlife and wildlife habitat and heritage resources. It was determined by the Designate Office that the Project will have significant adverse effects on three valued components that can be mitigated and recommended that this Project proceed with terms and conditions.The project is located approximately 46km northwest of Haines Junction, within the Kluane Wildlife Sanctuary.  The claims are on Kimberly Creek, a tributary of the Jarvis River and within the Alsek River watershed.  The project is located in the Southwest Assessment District, within the traditional territory of the Champagne and Aishihik First Nation, on Settlement Land parcel CAFN R-47A.

projectScope.activities: The proposed project is the construction of a new access road from the Alaska Highway at Km 1790.6 to the Kluane First Nation (KFN) settlement land block KFN S-72B to support a permanent harvest and cultural camp. The proposed road is located approximately 90 km north of Burwash Landing and less than 1 km north of Koidern River. The Project is within KFN and White River First Nation Traditional Territories. Construction of the road will begin in early June and take approximately one month to complete. Use of the access road will be indefinite.Project Activities:•	Use of heavy equipment•	Clearing and levelling of land for the construction of the road (500 m x 30 m)•	Installation of one culvert•	Laying of gravel•	Burning of wood and debris•	Waste management
projectScope.summary: The Proponent, Kluane First Nation, submitted an application for the construction of an access road connecting settlement block KFN S-72B1 with the Alaska Highway, for the purposes of establishing a permanent harvest camp.   The proposal was reviewed to determine whether the information was adequate to commence an assessment.  The adequacy review identified information gaps in project information, and additional information was requested from the Proponent.  The Proponent supplied the requested information (YOR documents 2015-0184-006-1 and 2015-0184-008-1), and the proposal was deemed Adequate on Jan 22, 2016.  The Haines Junction Designated Office provided an opportunity for comment on the Project during the Seeking Views and Information period from January 22nd to February 5th, 2016. Comments were submitted by three Departments in Government of Yukon (YG). All three departments from YG that provided comments: Environment, Highways and Public Works (HPW) and Tourism-Heritage, had no concerns with the Project.  The Designated Office identified one valued component in the assessment: Environmental Quality, and it was determined that the Project will have significant adverse effects on this valued component. However, by implementing the recommended mitigation measures in the Evaluation Report, in addition to those that the Proponent has committed to and application of relevant legislative requirements, the Designated Office is satisfied that the adverse effects can be reduced, controlled or eliminated such that they are not significant.The Project is located approximately 80km south of Beaver Creek, and 90km north of Burwash Landing at approximate km 1790.5 of the Alaska Highway, 100m North of the Koidern Bridge.  The Koidern River is 35m south of the southern extent of the project, and the nearest waterbody is Pickhandle Lake, 11km to the north. The Project is located in the Southwest Assessment District, within the traditional territories of the Kluane First Nation and the White River First Nation.

projectScope.activities: The proposed project is a Class 3 quartz mineral exploration program on 28 claims near Swede Johnson Creek, approximately 40 km northwest of Burwash Landing. The Project is found within the White River and Kluane First Nations Traditional Territories. Winter transport of fuel and equipment are planned to occur in March and exploration activities are planned to occur between June 1st and September 15th annually for a period of four years, beginning in 2016.Project Activities:•	Use of heavy equipment •	Access (winter access with loader and truck; summer access with ATV)-Use of existing off-claims access road (12 km x 4 m) -Use of existing on-claims access road (1.5 km x 5 m)-Use of 1 existing off-claims ford •	Clearings (1 clearing per claim, with up to 3 clearings per year, 20 m x 20 m)•	Mechanical trenching (up to 3 trenches/year, 100 m in length x 3 m wide x 1 m deep)•	Water use from Swede Creek (up to 300 m3/day for camp)•	Fuel handling and storage (600 L diesel)•	Existing camp: (2 person camp with trailers and storage shed)-Use of pit privy-Use of grey water pit      •	Waste management: -Burning of domestic waste in burn barrel•	Progressive reclamation and final decommissioning
projectScope.summary: The Proponent submitted a proposal for a Class 3 Mining Land Use Permit for Quartz Exploration. The proposal was submitted on Aug 4, 2015 and was reviewed to determine whether there was adequate information to commence an assessment. The adequacy review identified information gaps in project information, and additional information was requested from the Proponent.  The Proponent supplied the requested information (YOR document 2015-0142-010-1), and the proposal was deemed Adequate on Jan 14, 2016.  The Project went into Seeking Views and Information, with a comment submission deadline of Jan 28, 2016.  Following a request from the Dan Keyi RRC, the deadline for comments was extended to Feb 12, 2016.  Comments were submitted by the KFN, Environment Canada's Canadian Wildlife Service (CWS), Government of Yukon (YG), and Dän Keyi Renewable Resources Council (DKRRC). Within comment submissions, concerns that were raised spoke to effects to water quality, the environment, wildlife including sheep, grizzly bear and caribou, migratory and protected birds, and heritage resources. The Designated Office identified three valued components in the assessment: environmental quality, wildlife and wildlife habitat and heritage resources. It was determined by the Designate Office that the Project will have significant adverse effects on all three valued components. However, by implementing the recommended mitigation measures in this report, in addition to those that the Proponent has committed to and relevant legislative requirements, the Designated Office is satisfied that the adverse effects can be reduced, controlled or eliminated such that they are not significant. The rationale for this determination is presented in the Evaluation Report.The project is located approximately 40km northwest of Burwash Landing, within the Kluane Wildlife Sanctuary.  Swede Johnson Creek flow through the project and into the Kluane River.  The project is located in the Southwest Assessment District, within the traditional territories of the Kluane First Nation and White River First Nation.

projectScope.summary: Golden Predator Corp. is proposing to amend its existing MLU Permit LQ00319b for the 3 Aces Property to include activities not scoped in the existing MLU. projectScope.activities:
Mining activities include water management infrastructure, a dry-stacked tailings facility for tailings disposal and waste rock storage facilities for tailings and waste rock. Access to the mine site will be by air and by ground. Ground traffic will be by the existing Tote Road, which is approximately 24 km in length and extends from the Robert Campbell Highway, south to the Mine site.  
For full project scope details, please see the Draft Screening Report YOR Document #2017-0083-3612.
projectScope.summary: BMC Minerals (No.1) Ltd. (proponent) is proposing mining, construction and mineral processing activities of an open pit and underground copper, lead, zinc, silver and gold mine approximately 115 km southwest of Ross River, Yukon. The Kudz Ze Kayah (KZK) Mine will produce an average of 180,000 tonnes (t) of zinc, 35,000 t of copper and 25,000 t lead concentrates annually and is expected to be in operation for a minimum of 10 years.The KZK Mine is located in the ABM Deposit located approximately 115 km southeast of Ross River and 24 km south of Finlayson Lake on the northern edge of the Pelly Mountains.

projectScope.summary:
Newmont Goldcorp Corporation (proponent) is proposing the construction, operation, decommissioning and closure of an open-pit gold mine in the White Gold District of west-central Yukon. The Coffee Gold Mine will extract ore at a rate of up to nine million tonnes per year and is expected to be in operation for 10 years.
The Coffee Gold Mine is located approximately 130 km south of the City of Dawson and 330 km northwest of Whitehorse on the northern access route (a 214 km all-weather access road with barge crossings across the Stewart and Yukon rivers between Dawson and the mine site).
